topics.title: Types of Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Types of Loads # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Solid Mechanics for Naval Architects**, a **load** is any external force, moment, or action applied to a structural component (such as a ship's hull, plating, stiffener, transverse frame, or longitudinal girder) which produces internal stresses, strains, deformations, or displacements within the body. Mathematically, a load is represented as a **generalized force vector**: $$ \vec{F} = F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j} + F_z \hat{k} $$ For naval structures, loads are broadly classified based on: - **Nature of application** (Static vs Dynamic) - **Spatial distribution** (Point/Concentrated vs Distributed) - **Direction of action** (Axial, Transverse, Torsional) - **Time dependence** (Sustained vs Transient) - **Origin** (Gravitational, Environmental, Operational, Accidental) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Syllabus Highlight):** Loads acting on ship and offshore structures are classified into **Dead Loads, Live Loads, Environmental Loads (Wind, Wave, Current, Seismic), Dynamic Loads (Impact, Slamming, Vibration), and Accidental Loads (Collision, Fire, Explosion)**. Naval architects must evaluate all load combinations as per classification society rules (e.g., DNV, ABS, Lloyd's Register). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a ship as a **giant floating bridge that breathes**. Just like a bridge supports cars (live load) and its own weight (dead load), a ship carries cargo (cargo loads), its own structure (lightweight/steel weight), and passengers (live loads). But unlike a bridge, a ship is **constantly being pushed around by the sea** — waves hit it from the side, slamming from below, wind pushes from above, and water sloshes inside tanks. Each of these is a different "type of load" that the ship designer must account for. Imagine pushing a soda can with your finger: - A **slow, steady push** → Static load - A **sudden flick** → Dynamic / Impact load - A **wobbling motion** → Cyclic / Vibratory load The can must not crumple under any of these — similarly, the hull must survive every conceivable loading condition. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ > - **Density of seawater:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ > - **Density of fresh water:** $\rho_{fw} = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ > - **Young's Modulus of shipbuilding steel:** $E = 2.0 \times 10^5 \, \text{MPa}$ > - **Yield strength of mild steel (Grade A):** $\sigma_y = 235 \, \text{MPa}$ > - **Standard wave load reference height:** Significant wave height $H_s$ (in meters) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Loads — Structured Logic ### A. Based on Nature of Application **1. Static Loads (Time-Independent)** - Applied gradually; inertia effects are negligible - Internal forces remain constant (or vary slowly) with time - Examples: Dead weight, buoyancy at rest, hydrostatic pressure - Governing equation: $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ **2. Dynamic Loads (Time-Dependent)** - Applied rapidly; inertia and damping effects are significant - Produce vibrations, stress waves, and impact stresses - Examples: Wave slamming, engine vibration, collision, blast - Governing equation involves time derivative: $F(t)$, $v = \dfrac{dx}{dt}$, $a = \dfrac{d^2x}{dt^2}$ ### B. Based on Spatial Distribution **1. Point / Concentrated Load** - Acts at a single point or over a very small area - Symbol: $\downarrow P$ (force in Newtons or kN) - Example: Weight of a mast resting on deck **2. Distributed Load** - Spread over a length, area, or volume - Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL): $w$ in N/m - Linearly Varying Load (Triangular/Trapezoidal) ### C. Based on Direction of Action **1. Axial Load** — Along the longitudinal axis (Tension or Compression) **2. Transverse / Shear Load** — Perpendicular to axis **3. Torsional Load** — Moment about longitudinal axis (twisting) **4. Bending Load** — Moment causing curvature ### D. Naval-Architecture-Specific Classification **1. Still Water Loads (SWL)** — Loads on a vessel in calm water $$ \text{Still Water Bending Moment (SWBM)} = f(\text{weight distribution}, \text{buoyancy curve}) $$ **2. Wave-Induced Loads** — Dynamic loads from ocean waves - **Wave Bending Moment (WBM)** — Hogging and Sagging - **Slamming Loads** — Sudden impact on bow flare - **Green Water Loads** — Water on deck - **Whipping Response** — High-frequency springing vibration **3. Cargo & Operational Loads** - Container stack loads, tank pressure loads, crane loads **4. Environmental Loads** - Wind ($F_W = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A V^2$) - Current drag - Ice loads (for polar vessels) **5. Accidental Loads** - Collision, grounding, fire, explosion ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Load Type** | **Formula / Equation** | **Units (SI)** | **Application** | |---------------|------------------------|----------------|-----------------| | Dead Load (Weight) | $W = mg$ | N or kN | Hull steel weight, machinery | | Uniform Pressure | $p = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pa (N/m²) | Hydrostatic pressure | | Hydrostatic Pressure | $p = \rho g h$ | Pa | Pressure at depth $h$ | | UDL Shear | $V = wL$ | N | Shear from distributed load | | UDL Bending Moment | $M = \dfrac{wL^2}{2}$ | N·m | Max moment for simply supported | | Point Load Reaction | $R = \dfrac{P \cdot b}{L}$ | N | Reaction at supports | | Wind Force | $F_W = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A V^2$ | N | Wind pressure on superstructure | | Wave Load (Morison) | $F = C_M \rho V \dot{V} \cdot \text{Vol} + \tfrac{1}{2} C_D \rho A \vert V \vert V$ | N | Wave on slender members | | Dynamic Load Factor | $\text{DLF} = \dfrac{\text{Max Dynamic Response}}{\text{Static Response}}$ | dimensionless | Impact loading | | Slamming Pressure | $p_s = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_s V_n^2$ | Pa | Bottom slamming | | Stress (Axial) | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pa | Tension/Compression | | Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{A}$ | Pa | Transverse loading | | Bending Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$ | Pa | Bending moment | | Torsional Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{Tr}{J}$ | Pa | Shaft torque | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Absolute Value Convention:** In markdown tables, absolute values are written using `\vert` instead of the pipe symbol `|` to prevent table formatting breakage. Example: $\vert V \vert$ (rendered as `\vert V \vert`). ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Ship structural design:** Determining scantlings (plate thickness, frame spacing) using classification rules - **Offshore platforms:** Wave, current, and wind loading on jacket structures - **Submarine design:** Hydrostatic crushing pressure at depth - **Aircraft carrier deck:** Aircraft landing impact loads + wave-induced green water - **FPSO turret mooring:** Combined wind, wave, current loads on turret # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydrostatic Pressure on a Submerged Surface **Problem Statement:** Derive the expression for hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ below the free surface of a fluid of density $\rho$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Force Balance on a Fluid Column:** Consider a vertical column of fluid with cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$. The forces acting on it are: - Weight of the column acting downward: $W = \rho g A h$ - Pressure force from above acting downward: $p_0 A$ - Pressure force from below acting upward: $p A$ **Step 2 — Equilibrium Equation (Static Fluid):** For a fluid in static equilibrium, the net vertical force is zero: $$ pA = p_0 A + \rho g A h $$ **Step 3 — Simplification:** Dividing both sides by area $A$: $$ p = p_0 + \rho g h $$ **Step 4 — Final Result:** For an open surface (atmospheric), $p_0 = 0$ (gauge pressure basis): $$ \boxed{p = \rho g h} $$ This shows that **pressure increases linearly with depth** — a critical relationship for ship hull plating, submarine design, and ballast tank calculations. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Force on a Vertical Rectangular Submerged Plate **Problem Statement:** Find the total hydrostatic force on a vertical rectangular plate of width $b$ submerged with its top edge at depth $h_1$ and bottom edge at depth $h_2$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Pressure at a Differential Strip:** Take a horizontal strip at depth $z$ (measured from free surface) of thickness $dz$ and area $dA = b \, dz$. The pressure at this depth: $$ p(z) = \rho g z $$ **Step 2 — Differential Force:** The differential force on this strip: $$ dF = p(z) \, dA = \rho g z \cdot b \, dz $$ **Step 3 — Integrate Over the Plate Depth:** $$ F = \int_{h_1}^{h_2} \rho g b z \, dz $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral:** $$ F = \rho g b \left[ \dfrac{z^2}{2} \right]_{h_1}^{h_2} $$ $$ F = \dfrac{\rho g b}{2} \left( h_2^2 - h_1^2 \right) $$ **Step 5 — Final Compact Form:** Using the identity $h_2^2 - h_1^2 = (h_2 - h_1)(h_2 + h_1)$ and noting that average depth $\bar{h} = \dfrac{h_1 + h_2}{2}$ and height $H = h_2 - h_1$: $$ \boxed{F = \rho g b H \bar{h} = p_{\text{centroid}} \cdot A_{\text{plate}}} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Load Distribution & Internal Reactions | **Beam Configuration** | **Loading Type** | **Max Shear $V_{\max}$** | **Max Moment $M_{\max}$** | **Max Deflection $\delta_{\max}$** | |--------------------------|------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|------------------------------------| | Simply supported, length $L$, point load $P$ at center | Concentrated | $\dfrac{P}{2}$ | $\dfrac{PL}{4}$ | $\dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$ | | Simply supported, UDL $w$ over full span | Distributed | $\dfrac{wL}{2}$ | $\dfrac{wL^2}{8}$ | $\dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ | | Simply supported, triangular load (0 to $w$) | Linearly varying | $\dfrac{wL}{6}$ | $\dfrac{wL^2}{9\sqrt{3}}$ | $\dfrac{wL^4}{128EI}$ (approx) | | Cantilever, point load $P$ at free end | Concentrated | $P$ | $PL$ | $\dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$ | | Cantilever, UDL $w$ over full span | Distributed | $wL$ | $\dfrac{wL^2}{2}$ | $\dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$ | | Fixed-fixed beam, UDL $w$ | Distributed | $\dfrac{wL}{2}$ | $\dfrac{wL^2}{12}$ (at supports) | $\dfrac{wL^4}{384EI}$ | | Ship hull (continuous) | Wave-induced bending | Varies with wave position | SWBM + WBM | Microns to mm | ## 3.4 Derivation 3: Dynamic Load Factor (DLF) for Sudden Impact **Problem Statement:** A weight $W$ is suddenly applied to a spring of stiffness $k$. Find the maximum deflection and Dynamic Load Factor. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Static Deflection:** If applied slowly: $$ \delta_{\text{static}} = \dfrac{W}{k} $$ **Step 2 — Energy Conservation (Sudden Application):** When $W$ is suddenly released from rest at the unstretched position, all gravitational PE converts to strain energy in spring at maximum deflection: $$ W \cdot \delta_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2} k \delta_{\max}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Solve for $\delta_{\max}$:** $$ \delta_{\max} = \dfrac{2W}{k} = 2 \, \delta_{\text{static}} $$ **Step 4 — Dynamic Load Factor:** $$ \boxed{\text{DLF} = \dfrac{\delta_{\max}}{\delta_{\text{static}}} = 2} $$ **Physical Interpretation:** A suddenly applied load produces **twice the deflection and stress** of a gradually applied load. Naval applications: slamming loads, dropped cargo, emergency anchoring. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Load Classification Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["LOADS ON SHIP STRUCTURES"] --> B["STATIC LOADS"] A --> C["DYNAMIC LOADS"] A --> D["ENVIRONMENTAL LOADS"] A --> E["ACCIDENTAL LOADS"] B --> B1["DEAD LOAD - Lightweight of Hull"] B --> B2["LIVE LOAD - Cargo, Crew, Stores"] B --> B3["BUOYANCY - Hydrostatic Pressure"] C --> C1["WAVE LOADS - Bending, Shear"] C --> C2["SLAMMING - Bottom Impact"] C --> C3["GREEN WATER - Deck Wash"] C --> C4["VIBRATION - Engine, Propeller"] C --> C5["WHIPPING - Springing Response"] D --> D1["WIND FORCE"] D --> D2["CURRENT DRAG"] D --> D3["ICE LOAD"] D --> D4["SEISMIC - Earthquake"] E --> E1["COLLISION - Ship-Ship"] E --> E2["GROUNDING - Bottom Contact"] E --> E3["FIRE LOAD"] E --> E4["BLAST LOAD"] B1 --> F["INTERNAL STRESSES"] B2 --> F B3 --> F C1 --> F C2 --> F C3 --> F C4 --> F C5 --> F D1 --> F D2 --> F D3 --> F D4 --> F E1 --> F E2 --> F E3 --> F E4 --> F F --> G["STRUCTURAL RESPONSE"] G --> G1["AXIAL - Tension/Compression"] G --> G2["SHEAR - Transverse"] G --> G3["BENDING - Hogging/Sagging"] G --> G4["TORSION - Twisting"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Load Path in a Ship Structure ```mermaid flowchart LR A["EXTERNAL LOADS
Waves, Wind, Cargo"] --> B["HULL PLATING"] B --> C["STIFFENERS
Frames, Longitudinals"] C --> D["PRIMARY STRUCTURE
Girders, Webs"] D --> E["TRANSVERSE BULKHEADS"] E --> F["DECK PLATING
Bottom Shell"] F --> G["FOUNDATION
Keel, Side Shell"] H["INTERNAL FORCES
N, V, M, T"] --> I["STRESSES
σ, τ"] I --> J["STRAINS
ε, γ"] J --> K["DEFORMATIONS
δ, θ"] K --> L["DESIGN CHECK
σ ≤ σ_allow"] ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: Hogging and Sagging of a Ship ```mermaid flowchart TD A["SHIP IN WAVES"] --> B["WAVE CREST AT MIDSHIP
SAGGING CONDITION"] A --> C["WAVE CREST AT ENDS
HOGGING CONDITION"] B --> B1["Deck in TENSION"] B --> B2["Bottom in COMPRESSION"] B1 --> B3["MAX BM at Midship"] B2 --> B3 C --> C1["Deck in COMPRESSION"] C --> C2["Bottom in TENSION"] C1 --> C3["MAX BM at Midship"] C2 --> C3 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **"Differentiate between Static and Dynamic loads with two examples each from naval structures."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | **Aspect** | **Static Load** | **Dynamic Load** | |------------|------------------|------------------| | Definition | Load applied gradually; negligible inertia effects | Load applied rapidly; significant inertia effects | | Time-dependence | Time-independent | Time-dependent | | Examples (Naval) | (1) Still water pressure on hull plating; (2) Dead weight of machinery | (1) Wave slamming on bow; (2) Engine vibration on shaft | **Mark Distribution:** - Clear definition: 1 mark - Two naval examples each: 1 mark - Tabular differentiation: 1 mark --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **"Explain the significance of Dynamic Load Factor (DLF) in ship structural design with a numerical example."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Dynamic Load Factor (DLF)** is the ratio of maximum dynamic response (deflection, stress) to the corresponding static response when the same load is applied gradually. $$ \text{DLF} = \dfrac{\text{Dynamic Max Response}}{\text{Static Response}} $$ **Numerical Example:** A cargo block of weight $W = 100 \, \text{kN}$ is suddenly dropped on deck plating. For sudden application, $\text{DLF} = 2$. Equivalent static load for design: $$ F_{\text{design}} = \text{DLF} \times W = 2 \times 100 = 200 \, \text{kN} $$ **Significance:** Ensures ship structures are designed for **worst-case impact scenarios**, preventing permanent deformation. **Mark Distribution:** - Definition + formula: 1 mark - Numerical example: 1 mark - Naval significance: 1 mark --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]** **"A simply supported steel beam of length $L = 6 \, \text{m}$ supports a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of $w = 20 \, \text{kN/m}$ over its entire span. Determine:** **(a) The maximum bending moment and maximum shear force. (7 Marks)** **(b) The maximum bending stress if the beam has a rectangular cross-section of width $b = 100 \, \text{mm}$ and depth $d = 200 \, \text{mm}$. (7 Marks)"** --- #### Solution to Part (a): Maximum Bending Moment and Shear Force (7 Marks) **Given:** - Span length: $L = 6 \, \text{m}$ - UDL intensity: $w = 20 \, \text{kN/m}$ **Step 1 — Calculate Support Reactions:** By symmetry of loading and geometry: $$ R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2} $$ $$ R_A = R_B = \dfrac{20 \times 6}{2} = 60 \, \text{kN} $$ **Step 2 — Shear Force Equation:** At distance $x$ from support A: $$ V(x) = R_A - wx = 60 - 20x $$ **Step 3 — Maximum Shear Force:** At $x = 0$ (support A): $$ V_{\max} = 60 \, \text{kN} $$ **Step 4 — Bending Moment Equation:** $$ M(x) = R_A x - \dfrac{wx^2}{2} = 60x - 10x^2 $$ **Step 5 — Location of Maximum Moment:** Set $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = 0$: $$ \dfrac{dM}{dx} = 60 - 20x = 0 \implies x = 3 \, \text{m} \, (\text{midspan}) $$ **Step 6 — Maximum Bending Moment:** $$ M_{\max} = 60(3) - 10(3)^2 = 180 - 90 = 90 \, \text{kN·m} $$ Using the standard formula: $$ M_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8} = \dfrac{20 \times 6^2}{8} = \dfrac{720}{8} = 90 \, \text{kN·m} \quad \checkmark $$ **Answer (a):** $V_{\max} = 60 \, \text{kN}$ and $M_{\max} = 90 \, \text{kN·m}$ --- #### Solution to Part (b): Maximum Bending Stress (7 Marks) **Given:** - Cross-section: $b = 100 \, \text{mm}$, $d = 200 \, \text{mm}$ - $M_{\max} = 90 \, \text{kN·m} = 90 \times 10^6 \, \text{N·mm}$ **Step 1 — Moment of Inertia of Rectangular Section:** $$ I = \dfrac{bd^3}{12} = \dfrac{100 \times 200^3}{12} $$ $$ I = \dfrac{100 \times 8 \times 10^6}{12} = \dfrac{800 \times 10^6}{12} $$ $$ I = 66.67 \times 10^6 \, \text{mm}^4 $$ **Step 2 — Distance from Neutral Axis to Extreme Fiber:** $$ y_{\max} = \dfrac{d}{2} = \dfrac{200}{2} = 100 \, \text{mm} $$ **Step 3 — Section Modulus:** $$ Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}} = \dfrac{bd^2}{6} = \dfrac{100 \times 200^2}{6} $$ $$ Z = \dfrac{100 \times 40000}{6} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^6}{6} = 666.67 \times 10^3 \, \text{mm}^3 $$ **Step 4 — Maximum Bending Stress (Flexure Formula):** $$ \sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \dfrac{90 \times 10^6}{666.67 \times 10^3} $$ $$ \sigma_{\max} = 135 \, \text{N/mm}^2 = 135 \, \text{MPa} $$ **Step 5 — Check against Allowable Stress:** For mild steel, $\sigma_{\text{allow}} \approx 165 \, \text{MPa}$ (with safety factor). Since $\sigma_{\max} = 135 \, \text{MPa} < 165 \, \text{MPa}$, the beam is **SAFE**. **Factor of Safety:** $$ \text{FoS} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{\max}} = \dfrac{235}{135} \approx 1.74 $$ **Answer (b):** $\sigma_{\max} = 135 \, \text{MPa}$; Beam is safe. --- ### **Question B (Alternative 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **"A rectangular plate of dimensions $2 \, \text{m} \times 3 \, \text{m}$ is submerged vertically in seawater ($\rho = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$) with its top edge at the water surface.** **(a) Calculate the total hydrostatic force on the plate. (7 Marks)** **(b) Determine the depth of the center of pressure from the free surface. (7 Marks)"** --- #### Solution to Part (a): Total Hydrostatic Force (7 Marks) **Given:** - Plate width: $b = 2 \, \text{m}$ - Plate height: $h = 3 \, \text{m}$ - Top edge at free surface, so $h_1 = 0$, $h_2 = 3 \, \text{m}$ - Density: $\rho = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ - $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ **Step 1 — Centroid Depth:** $$ \bar{h} = \dfrac{h_1 + h_2}{2} = \dfrac{0 + 3}{2} = 1.5 \, \text{m} $$ **Step 2 — Area of Plate:** $$ A = b \times h = 2 \times 3 = 6 \, \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Pressure at Centroid:** $$ p_c = \rho g \bar{h} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 $$ $$ p_c = 15082.875 \, \text{Pa} \approx 15.08 \, \text{kPa} $$ **Step 4 — Total Force:** $$ F = p_c \times A = 15082.875 \times 6 $$ $$ \boxed{F = 90,497.25 \, \text{N} \approx 90.5 \, \text{kN}} $$ --- #### Solution to Part (b): Depth of Center of Pressure (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Moment of Inertia of Plate About Centroidal Axis (horizontal axis through centroid):** $$ I_{xc} = \dfrac{bh^3}{12} = \dfrac{2 \times 3^3}{12} = \dfrac{2 \times 27}{12} = 4.5 \, \text{m}^4 $$ **Step 2 — Depth of Center of Pressure Formula:** $$ h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{xc}}{A \cdot \bar{h}} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute Values:** $$ h_{cp} = 1.5 + \dfrac{4.5}{6 \times 1.5} = 1.5 + \dfrac{4.5}{9} $$ $$ h_{cp} = 1.5 + 0.5 = 2.0 \, \text{m} $$ **Step 4 — Verify Using Parallel Axis Theorem:** $$ I_{xs} = I_{xc} + A\bar{h}^2 = 4.5 + 6(1.5)^2 = 4.5 + 13.5 = 18 \, \text{m}^4 $$ $$ h_{cp} = \dfrac{I_{xs}}{A \cdot \bar{h}} = \dfrac{18}{6 \times 1.5} = \dfrac{18}{9} = 2.0 \, \text{m} \quad \checkmark $$ **Answer (b):** Center of pressure is at depth $h_{cp} = 2.0 \, \text{m}$ from the free surface. **Physical Insight:** The center of pressure lies **below the centroid** ($2.0 \, \text{m} > 1.5 \, \text{m}$) because pressure increases with depth, shifting the resultant force downward. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert kN·m to N·mm (multiply by $10^6$) is the #1 cause of wrong answers in bending stress problems. Always check units before substituting. > 2. **Wrong Formula Selection:** Using $I = bh^3/36$ instead of $bh^3/12$ (this is for *I-section*, not rectangle). > 3. **Center of Pressure Mistakes:** Many students compute $h_{cp} = \bar{h}$ only — this is WRONG. The correction term $\dfrac{I_{xc}}{A\bar{h}}$ is **mandatory** for non-uniform pressure distribution. > 4. **Confusing Hogging vs. Sagging:** In sagging, deck is in tension; in hogging, deck is in compression. Mixing these up loses full marks. > 5. **Forgetting DLF:** Treating impact loads as static (DLF = 1) instead of DLF = 2 for sudden application. Always read the problem statement carefully. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Types of Loads:** - [x] **Static Loads** are time-independent; **Dynamic Loads** are time-dependent with inertia effects. - [x] **Dead Load** = Self-weight of structure; **Live Load** = Variable cargo/crew loads. - [x] **Point Load** acts at a single location; **UDL** is uniform across length. - [x] **Axial Load** produces $\sigma = F/A$; **Shear Load** produces $\tau = V/A$. - [x] **Bending Load** produces $\sigma = My/I$; **Torsional Load** produces $\tau = Tr/J$. - [x] **Hydrostatic Pressure:** $p = \rho g h$ (linear with depth). - [x] **Force on submerged plate:** $F = \rho g \bar{h} A$ (at centroid). - [x] **Center of pressure depth:** $h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{xc}}{A\bar{h}}$. - [x] **DLF for sudden load** = 2; for short-duration impact, DLF > 2. - [x] **Ship-specific loads:** Still Water BM + Wave BM = Total Bending Moment. - [x] **Hogging:** Bow & stern supported, mid-air; **Sagging:** Bow & stern in waves, mid supported. - [x] **Slamming Pressure:** $p_s = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_s V_n^2$ — critical for bow flare and bottom plates. - [x] **Wind Force:** $F_W = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A V^2$ — relevant for superstructures. - [x] **Classification society rules** (DNV, ABS, LR) mandate specific load combinations. - [x] **Allowable Stress Design** uses FoS ≈ 1.5–2.0 over yield stress. - [x] Always **sketch SFD and BMD** before solving beam problems. - [x] For naval architecture, **wave-induced loads are usually the governing design loads**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Exam Tip:** Module 1 questions typically carry 14 marks and combine theory + numerical. Expect at least one numerical problem on **hydrostatic force** or **bending stress** combined with a theory question on **load classification** or **dynamic load factor**. Memorize the formula sheet thoroughly — it appears in nearly every past paper.

topics.title: Stress, Strain and Their Types, Stress-Strain Concepts and Hooke's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stress, Strain and Their Types, Stress-Strain Concepts and Hooke's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Stress?

**Stress** is the *internal resistance* offered by a material per unit area when an external load (force) is applied to it. It is a measure of the intensity of the internal forces distributed throughout the body and is the fundamental response parameter that engineers use to predict whether a structural component (or a ship hull, in our context) will fail under load.

Mathematically, stress is defined as the limiting value of internal force intensity as the area approaches zero at a point:

$$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A}$$

The standard unit of stress in the **SI system is the Pascal (Pa = N/m²)**. Because practical engineering stresses are typically in the order of **MPa (10⁶ Pa)** or **GPa (10⁹ Pa)**, the values of **1 MPa = 1 N/mm²** and **1 GPa = 1000 MPa = 10⁹ N/m²** are essential conversions for naval architecture problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Stress at a point in a continuum body is the internal force per unit area acting on an infinitesimal plane passing through that point. It is a *second-order tensor* quantity (not a scalar or vector).

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy: The "Crowded Subway" Effect

Imagine you are standing in a packed train (the *material*) during peak hour. The people behind you push (the *external force*), and to avoid being crushed, you push back on the person in front with an equal and opposite reaction (this is the *internal resistance*). Now, if you are squeezed into a narrow gap (small *area*), the discomfort (stress) you feel is enormous. If you are in a wider corridor (large area), the discomfort is much less. **Stress is essentially the "internal push per unit area" that maintains the body's equilibrium against external loads.**

---

## 1.2 What is Strain?

**Strain** is a dimensionless measure of deformation representing the *relative change in shape or size* of a body under applied load. Unlike stress, which describes internal force intensity, strain describes *geometric distortion* — it is a purely kinematic quantity independent of the material's strength.

For a one-dimensional bar of original length $L$ that elongates by an amount $\delta L$ under an axial load, the normal strain is:

$$\varepsilon = \lim_{\Delta L \to 0} \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$

Strain is **dimensionless** (units of mm/mm or m/m) and is often expressed as a percentage, microstrain ($\mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$), or as a pure decimal in rigorous derivations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Strain represents *deformation per unit original dimension*. KTU examiners frequently test whether students correctly use the *original* length in the denominator, not the *deformed* length (which is the definition of *engineering* strain, valid for small deformations).

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy: The "Rubber Band Screenshot"

Take a rubber band of length 10 cm. Pull it until it becomes 12 cm long. The deformation is 2 cm. Now compare this with another rubber band of length 20 cm that you also pull by 2 cm. The first one feels "more stretched" relatively, even though both underwent identical absolute displacement. Strain captures this **relative stretching** — the ratio of the change to the original. This is why a 1 mm extension is dramatic for a thin wire but utterly negligible for a ship's hull girder.

---

## 1.3 Why Stress and Strain Are Central to Naval Architecture

For a naval architect, the *hull girder* of a ship is subjected to **still-water bending moments, wave-induced bending moments, shearing forces, and torsional loads**. The strength of the hull depends on how the **stresses** (which depend on the geometry and the load) compare with the **allowable stress** of the steel. The **strains** (deformations) must be limited to prevent the hull from leaking, hogging/sagging excessively, or losing serviceability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hooke's Law Bridge:** The relationship between stress and strain in the *elastic* region is governed by **Hooke's Law** — the cornerstone equation that links the load-bearing capacity (stress) to the deformational response (strain) of any ship structural member.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Stress

The internal stress at a point in a body is fully characterized by the **Cauchy Stress Tensor** $\boldsymbol{\sigma}$, which has nine components in 3D. However, for engineering classification, we use two main categories based on the orientation of the plane on which the stress acts:

### 2.1.1 Normal Stress ($\sigma$)

**Normal stress** acts *perpendicular* to the surface of the cross-section. It can be either:
- **Tensile Stress** (positive, $\sigma > 0$): The internal force pulls the material *apart* (e.g., a steel cable lifting a load).
- **Compressive Stress** (negative, $\sigma < 0$): The internal force pushes the material *together* (e.g., a column supporting a deck load, or the keel of a ship pressing on the seabed).

$$\sigma_n = \frac{F_n}{A}$$

### 2.1.2 Shear Stress ($\tau$)

**Shear stress** acts *parallel* (tangential) to the surface of the cross-section. It tries to slide one part of the body relative to the adjacent part. Examples include:
- The rivet in a ship's hull plate connection.
- The web of a beam under transverse load.
- Bolted joints in marine structural assemblies.

$$\tau = \frac{F_t}{A}$$

### 2.1.3 Bearing Stress ($\sigma_b$)

**Bearing stress** is the contact compressive stress between two bodies pressed against each other, such as a bolt pressing against a plate. It is a localized compressive phenomenon.

$$\sigma_b = \frac{F}{A_b}$$

where $A_b$ is the *projected contact area* between the two bodies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tensile vs. Compressive Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** Following the KTU / IS convention used in classical mechanics of materials, **tensile stresses and elongations are positive**, while **compressive stresses and contractions are negative**. Shear stress is positive when it acts on a positive face in the positive direction.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Strain

### 2.2.1 Normal Strain ($\varepsilon$)

A **normal (longitudinal) strain** is the change in length per unit original length along a single axis. For an axially loaded bar:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\delta L}{L} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0}$$

### 2.2.2 Lateral (Transverse) Strain

When a bar is stretched axially, it contracts laterally. The lateral strain in the perpendicular direction $y$ is:

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{-\nu \cdot \varepsilon_x}{1}$$

(related to axial strain through Poisson's ratio — covered later).

### 2.2.3 Shear Strain ($\gamma$)

**Shear strain** is the change in angle (in radians) between two line segments that were originally perpendicular. For a small rectangular element subjected to pure shear, the angular distortion is $\gamma$:

$$\gamma = \tan(\gamma) = \frac{\delta}{h}$$

(where $\delta$ is the relative lateral displacement and $h$ is the height of the element).

### 2.2.4 Volumetric Strain ($\varepsilon_v$)

**Volumetric strain** is the change in volume per unit original volume:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V_0}$$

For small strains in 3D:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

---

## 2.3 Hooke's Law — The Stress–Strain Relationship

**Hooke's Law** is the fundamental linear-elastic constitutive equation that ties stress to strain. In its most general 3D form, it is expressed as a tensor relationship, but for our KTU Module 1 purpose, the uniaxial form suffices:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

where $E$ is the **Young's Modulus** (Modulus of Elasticity) — a material property with units of stress (Pa or MPa). For **mild steel**, $E \approx 200$ GPa; for **marine grade aluminium alloys**, $E \approx 70$ GPa; for **structural timber**, $E \approx 10$–15 GPa.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hooke's Law is strictly valid only in the **linear-elastic region** of the stress–strain curve. For naval structures, the working stresses are designed to be *well within* this region (using a *Factor of Safety*) so that Hooke's Law remains applicable for routine deflection and stress calculations.

### 2.3.1 Hooke's Law in Shear

For pure shear loading, the analogous relationship is:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

where $G$ is the **Shear Modulus** (Modulus of Rigidity).

### 2.3.2 Hooke's Law in 3D (Generalized Form)

For a homogeneous, isotropic, linear-elastic material, Hooke's Law in 3D generalizes to:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

and the shear counterpart:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{yz} = \frac{\tau_{yz}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{zx} = \frac{\tau_{zx}}{G}$$

### 2.3.3 The Three Elastic Constants and Their Relationship

For isotropic materials, the three elastic constants are linked through:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu) = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's Modulus
- $G$ = Shear Modulus
- $K$ = Bulk Modulus
- $\nu$ = Poisson's Ratio

> [!NOTE]
> This relationship allows us to determine any one constant from the other two — a frequently tested KTU problem.

---

## 2.4 Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)

**Poisson's ratio** is defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to axial strain in a uniaxial tensile test:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$$

The negative sign ensures that $\nu$ is positive (since a tensile axial strain causes a *negative* lateral strain, i.e., contraction). For most engineering materials, $0 < \nu < 0.5$ (with $\nu = 0.5$ being the theoretical upper bound for incompressible materials like rubber; $\nu = 0.3$ is typical for steel).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Key Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | Normal Stress | $\sigma = F/A$ | Pa (N/m²) or MPa (N/mm²) | $1\text{ MPa} = 10^6\text{ Pa} = 1\text{ N/mm}^2$ |
| 2 | Shear Stress | $\tau = F_t/A$ | Pa or MPa | Acts parallel to surface |
| 3 | Bearing Stress | $\sigma_b = F/A_b$ | Pa or MPa | $A_b$ = projected contact area |
| 4 | Normal Strain | $\varepsilon = \delta L / L_0$ | Dimensionless | mm/mm or in/in |
| 5 | Shear Strain | $\gamma = \delta / h$ | Radians (rad) | Angular distortion |
| 6 | Volumetric Strain | $\varepsilon_v = \Delta V / V_0$ | Dimensionless | Sum of normal strains |
| 7 | Hooke's Law (Tension) | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Pa or MPa | $E$ in same units as $\sigma$ |
| 8 | Hooke's Law (Shear) | $\tau = G\gamma$ | Pa or MPa | $G$ in same units as $\tau$ |
| 9 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_{ax}$ | Dimensionless | $0 \le \nu \le 0.5$ |
| 10 | E–G–ν Relation | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | — | Isotropic materials only |
| 11 | E–K–ν Relation | $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | — | For volumetric loading |
| 12 | Generalized Hooke 3D | $\varepsilon_x = (1/E)[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]$ | — | Symmetric in $x,y,z$ |
| 13 | Volumetric Strain 3D | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | — | Small-strain assumption |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Naval Architecture

The stress–strain concepts underpin almost every naval structural calculation:
- **Hull Girder Bending:** The maximum bending stress in a ship's hull under wave loading is $\sigma = M y / I$, where $M$ is the bending moment, $y$ is the distance from the neutral axis, and $I$ is the second moment of area. The corresponding strain at any fiber is $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$.
- **Plate Buckling:** Local plate panels buckle when compressive stresses reach a critical value depending on the plate's slenderness — a stability (not strength) problem tied to the stress–strain behavior.
- **Deflection of Bulkheads and Decks:** The deflection of a deck under cargo load is calculated using $\delta = PL^3/(3EI)$ — a direct consequence of Hooke's Law in bending.
- **Rivet and Weld Design:** Bearing and shear stresses in riveted or welded joints are computed using the formulas above.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hooke's Law from a 1D Bar Element

**Problem Setup:** Consider a uniform prismatic bar of cross-sectional area $A$ and original length $L_0$ subjected to a centric axial tensile force $F$ at its ends. We wish to derive the relationship between axial stress and axial strain.

**Step 1 — Internal Force from Equilibrium:**

By making a section cut perpendicular to the bar's axis and applying static equilibrium, the internal axial force in the bar is equal to the applied external load $F$ (no other forces act on the isolated segment).

$$N = F$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Normal Stress:**

By definition, the average normal stress on the cross-section is the internal force divided by the area:

$$\sigma = \frac{N}{A} = \frac{F}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Definition of Normal Strain:**

If the bar elongates by an amount $\delta L = L_f - L_0$, the average normal strain is:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0}$$

**Step 4 — Empirical Observation (Hooke, 1660):**

Robert Hooke's original experiments on springs showed that for many materials, the elongation $\delta L$ is *linearly proportional* to the applied force $F$ within a certain range of loading:

$$\delta L = \frac{F L_0}{A E}$$

where $E$ is a material constant of proportionality (Young's Modulus).

**Step 5 — Substituting the Definitions:**

Substituting $\delta L = \varepsilon L_0$ and $F = \sigma A$ into the empirical relation above:

$$\varepsilon L_0 = \frac{(\sigma A) L_0}{A E}$$

**Step 6 — Simplification:**

The terms $L_0$ and $A$ cancel from both sides, leaving the cleanest form of Hooke's Law:

$$\boxed{\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon}$$

**Conclusion:** The linear proportionality between stress and strain, with $E$ as the proportionality constant, is Hooke's Law in its 1D form. It is valid provided the material is homogeneous, isotropic, and the strain remains in the linear-elastic regime.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Relationship $E = 2G(1+\nu)$

This is one of the most important derivations for KTU problems. We derive it by considering a 2D element under pure shear and showing that the shear modulus is geometrically related to $E$ and $\nu$.

**Step 1 — Consider a Square Element under Pure Shear:**

Take a unit square element of side $h$ subjected to a pure shear stress $\tau$ on all four faces. The element deforms into a rhombus (parallelogram). The change in the originally 90° angle at the corners is the shear strain $\gamma$.

**Step 2 — Equivalent 45° Tension–Compression Pair:**

A pure shear state on a square element is *equivalent* (by Mohr's circle or by stress transformation) to a state of uniaxial tension along the 45° diagonal and uniaxial compression along the −45° diagonal, with equal magnitudes:

$$\sigma_{45°} = \tau, \quad \sigma_{-45°} = -\tau$$

**Step 3 — Compute Normal Strains at 45°:**

Let the tensile strain along the 45° direction be $\varepsilon_{45°}$ and the compressive strain along the −45° direction be $\varepsilon_{-45°}$. Using the generalized Hooke's Law (in the principal directions):

$$\varepsilon_{45°} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_{45°} - \nu \cdot \sigma_{-45°}\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(-\tau)\right] = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Shear Strain from the Diagonal Strains:**

The change in length of the 45° diagonal of the unit square is (for small strains) $\delta d_{45°} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \varepsilon_{45°}$. Similarly, the change in the −45° diagonal length is $\delta d_{-45°} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \varepsilon_{-45°}$.

The shear strain (angular distortion) is the sum of these two diagonal elongations (with sign — the 45° elongates while the −45° contracts) divided by the original diagonal length $\sqrt{2}$:

$$\gamma = \frac{\delta d_{45°} - \delta d_{-45°}}{d_0} = \frac{\sqrt{2}(\varepsilon_{45°} - \varepsilon_{-45°})}{\sqrt{2}} = \varepsilon_{45°} - \varepsilon_{-45°}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**

By symmetry, $\varepsilon_{-45°} = -\varepsilon_{45°}$ (the compressive strain equals the negative of the tensile strain when the two principal stresses are equal and opposite). Therefore:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_{45°} - (-\varepsilon_{45°}) = 2\varepsilon_{45°} = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to Isolate $E$:**

Using the shear form of Hooke's Law, $\tau = G\gamma$, we have $\gamma = \tau/G$. Substituting:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

Cancel $\tau$ (assuming $\tau \neq 0$):

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{G} = \frac{2(1 + \nu)}{E} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

**Conclusion:** This fundamental relation is valid for all *homogeneous isotropic* materials in the linear-elastic range. The same procedure extended to 3D yields $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Volumetric Strain $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$

**Step 1 — Original and Deformed Volumes:**

Consider a small rectangular parallelepiped of sides $dx$, $dy$, $dz$ and original volume $V_0 = dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$. After deformation, the sides become $dx(1 + \varepsilon_x)$, $dy(1 + \varepsilon_y)$, $dz(1 + \varepsilon_z)$.

**Step 2 — New Volume:**

$$V_f = dx \cdot dy \cdot dz \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_x)(1 + \varepsilon_y)(1 + \varepsilon_z)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the Product (Small-Strain Approximation):**

For infinitesimal strains ($\varepsilon \ll 1$), we neglect higher-order terms like $\varepsilon_x \varepsilon_y$:

$$V_f \approx V_0 \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z)$$

**Step 4 — Change in Volume:**

$$\Delta V = V_f - V_0 = V_0(\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z)$$

**Step 5 — Definition of Volumetric Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z}$$

**Conclusion:** This linear superposition is valid only under the small-strain assumption. For large deformations (e.g., rubber, soft biological tissues), the nonlinear terms must be retained.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Behavior of Common Marine Materials

| Material | $E$ (GPa) | $G$ (GPa) | $\nu$ (Poisson's) | Typical Use in Ships |
|----------|-----------|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Mild Steel (ASTM A36) | 200 | 77 | 0.30 | Hull plates, stiffeners |
| Marine Grade Aluminium (5083) | 70 | 26.5 | 0.33 | Superstructure, fast ferries |
| Stainless Steel (316L) | 193 | 77 | 0.25 | Chemical tankers, piping |
| Marine Timber (Teak) | ~12 | ~0.6 | ~0.35 | Traditional boat hulls, decking |
| GRP (Glass-Reinforced Plastic) | 10–20 | 4–8 | 0.30 | Small craft hulls |
| Rubber (neoprene, natural) | 0.001–0.1 | 0.0003–0.04 | 0.45–0.499 | Vibration mounts, seals |

> [!NOTE]
> The values above are typical and serve as **engineering estimates**. KTU problems may provide explicit values in the question — always use the given values rather than these standards.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the following terms with proper units: (i) Stress, (ii) Strain, (iii) Poisson's Ratio.**

**Model Answer:**

> (i) **Stress** is the internal resistance offered by a material per unit cross-sectional area when an external load is applied. It is a second-order tensor. **Unit: Pascal (Pa) or N/m²; in engineering: MPa (N/mm²).**
>
> (ii) **Strain** is the ratio of change in dimension (length) to the original dimension. It is a dimensionless quantity (or sometimes expressed in mm/mm, %, or microstrain $\mu\varepsilon$).
>
> (iii) **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)** is the negative ratio of lateral strain to axial strain under uniaxial loading. It is dimensionless and typically lies in the range $0 \le \nu \le 0.5$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Hooke's Law. Under what conditions is Hooke's Law valid?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Statement:** Hooke's Law states that, within the linear-elastic range, stress is directly proportional to strain, i.e., $\sigma = E \varepsilon$, where $E$ is the Young's Modulus of the material.
>
> **Validity Conditions:**
> 1. The material must be **homogeneous and isotropic**.
> 2. The loading must be such that the **stress does not exceed the proportional limit** of the material.
> 3. The deformation must be **small** (small-strain assumption).
> 4. The temperature must remain **constant** (no thermal stresses superimposed).
> 5. The loading must be **static** (no dynamic or impact effects).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Module Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A steel tie rod of diameter 25 mm and length 1.5 m is subjected to an axial tensile load of 120 kN. Given: $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.30$.**

**Compute:**
**(a)** The axial stress, axial strain, and total elongation of the rod. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The change in diameter of the rod due to the Poisson effect. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Compute the cross-sectional area.**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (25 \text{ mm})^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 625}{4} = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the axial (normal) stress.**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{120 \times 10^3 \text{ N}}{490.87 \text{ mm}^2} = 244.46 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 244.46 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Compute the axial strain using Hooke's Law.**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{244.46 \text{ MPa}}{200 \times 10^3 \text{ MPa}} = 1.2223 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4: Compute the total elongation.**

$$\delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L_0 = (1.2223 \times 10^{-3})(1500 \text{ mm}) = 1.833 \text{ mm}$$

**Result Summary (Part a):** $\sigma = 244.46$ MPa, $\varepsilon = 1.2223 \times 10^{-3}$, $\delta L = 1.833$ mm.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Identify the relationship between lateral and axial strain via Poisson's ratio.**

$$\varepsilon_{lateral} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{axial} = -(0.30)(1.2223 \times 10^{-3}) = -3.667 \times 10^{-4}$$

The negative sign indicates **contraction** (since the bar is being stretched axially, it contracts laterally).

**Step 2: Compute the change in diameter.**

$$\varepsilon_{lateral} = \frac{\delta d}{d_0} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \delta d = \varepsilon_{lateral} \cdot d_0$$

$$\delta d = (-3.667 \times 10^{-4})(25 \text{ mm}) = -9.167 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = -9.167 \text{ }\mu\text{m}$$

**Result (Part b):** The diameter decreases by approximately **9.17 μm** (micrometers), giving a final diameter of $d_f = 25 - 0.00917 = 24.9908$ mm.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**In a uniaxial tension test on a structural steel specimen (diameter 20 mm, gauge length 200 mm), the following observations were recorded: yield load = 110 kN, ultimate load = 150 kN, elongation at fracture = 35 mm, final diameter at fracture = 14 mm.**

**Compute:**
**(a)** The yield stress, ultimate stress, and percentage elongation. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The Young's Modulus if the linear portion of the stress–strain curve has a slope of 200 GPa, and the percentage reduction in area. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Compute the original cross-sectional area.**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (20)^2}{4} = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Yield stress.**

$$\sigma_{y} = \frac{F_y}{A_0} = \frac{110 \times 10^3}{314.16} = 350.14 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Ultimate stress.**

$$\sigma_u = \frac{F_u}{A_0} = \frac{150 \times 10^3}{314.16} = 477.46 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Percentage elongation.**

$$\% \text{ Elongation} = \frac{\delta L}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{35}{200} \times 100 = 17.5\%$$

**Result Summary (Part a):** $\sigma_y \approx 350$ MPa, $\sigma_u \approx 477.5$ MPa, % Elongation = **17.5%**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Young's Modulus.**

The slope of the linear portion of the stress–strain curve is precisely the Young's Modulus:

$$E = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200 \times 10^3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Final cross-sectional area at fracture.**

$$A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (14)^2}{4} = 153.94 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Percentage reduction in area.**

$$\% \text{ RA} = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{314.16 - 153.94}{314.16} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ RA} = \frac{160.22}{314.16} \times 100 = 51.0\%$$

**Result Summary (Part b):** $E = 200$ GPa, % Reduction in Area = **51.0%**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Mark-Loss Hotspots:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors:** Forgetting the negative sign in $\varepsilon_{lateral} = -\nu\varepsilon_{axial}$ costs full marks in Poisson-effect problems. Always state "the diameter *decreases*."
> 2. **Using deformed length instead of original length:** Strain MUST be calculated with $L_0$ in the denominator, not $L_f$.
> 3. **Unit mismatches:** Mixing N/m² and N/mm² in the same calculation. KTU problems often mix units — convert everything to a consistent system (preferably N and mm) at the start.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 4 in circular area:** A surprisingly common error. Always write $A = \pi d^2 / 4$ explicitly to avoid part-mark deduction.
> 5. **Hooke's Law applied beyond the elastic limit:** If the problem implies a stress beyond the yield point (e.g., ultimate stress), students must NOT use $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ — instead, they should comment that the linear relationship no longer holds.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this in your notebook!)**

- [x] **Stress** = Internal force per unit area; units: Pa, MPa, N/mm²; $1\text{ MPa} = 1\text{ N/mm}^2$.
- [x] **Strain** = Change in dimension ÷ original dimension; dimensionless.
- [x] **Three types of stress:** Normal (tensile/compressive), Shear, Bearing.
- [x] **Three types of strain:** Normal (axial/lateral), Shear (angular), Volumetric.
- [x] **Hooke's Law (uniaxial):** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$. **Hooke's Law (shear):** $\tau = G\gamma$.
- [x] **Poisson's Ratio:** $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_{ax}$. For most metals, $\nu \approx 0.3$; for rubber, $\nu \to 0.5$.
- [x] **Master relationship for isotropic materials:** $E = 2G(1+\nu) = 3K(1-2\nu)$.
- [x] **3D Generalized Hooke's Law:** $\varepsilon_x = (1/E)[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]$, and cyclic permutations.
- [x] **Volumetric strain:** $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ (small-strain approximation).
- [x] **Validity of Hooke's Law:** Linear-elastic region only; homogeneous; isotropic; small strains; constant temperature; static load.
- [x] **Sign convention (KTU standard):** Tension = positive, Compression = negative, Shear positive if on +face in +direction.
- [x] **Units to memorize:** $E, G, K$ → Pa or MPa; $\nu, \varepsilon, \gamma$ → dimensionless; $\gamma$ specifically in radians.
- [x] **Naval architecture application:** Hull-girder bending stress $\sigma = M y / I$ paired with $\varepsilon = \sigma/E$ to compute local hull deflections.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress-Strain Relationship for Mild Steel and Elastic Limit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stress-Strain Relationship for Mild Steel and Elastic Limit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Stress (σ):** The internal resistance offered by a material per unit cross-sectional area when subjected to an external load. It is a measure of the intensity of the internal distributed forces within a deformable body.

**Strain (ε):** The ratio of change in dimension (deformation) to the original dimension of the body. It is a dimensionless quantity that measures the degree of deformation.

**Mild Steel:** A low-carbon steel (carbon content typically between **0.15% and 0.25%**) characterized by high ductility, excellent weldability, and a well-defined yield point. It is the most widely used ferrous material in ship hulls, structural frames, and naval architecture applications.

**Elastic Limit:** The maximum value of stress up to which a material returns to its original shape and size completely upon removal of the applied load. Beyond this limit, the material undergoes **permanent (plastic) deformation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the context of Naval Architecture, understanding the elastic limit of mild steel is critical because ship hull plating must remain within the elastic range under normal service loads (wave pressure, cargo loading, hydrostatic pressure) to ensure structural integrity and prevent permanent deformation.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are pulling a **thin steel wire** (similar to a piano string) gently with both hands:

- **Stage 1 — Light Pull:** The wire stretches just a tiny bit. When you release, it springs back to its original length. This is the **elastic region**. The wire behaves like a stretched rubber band.

- **Stage 2 — Stronger Pull:** You keep increasing the force. At some point, you will notice the wire stretches more easily, and if you release now, it will NOT return fully to its original length — a small permanent stretch remains. You have crossed the **elastic limit**.

- **Stage 3 — Very Strong Pull:** The wire continues to stretch significantly (it "necks down"), and finally snaps. This is the **fracture point**.

For a **naval architect**, this analogy is directly applicable to the **ship's hull plating**: it must flex with the ocean waves (elastic behavior) but never deform permanently during the vessel's operational life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Recap:** Stress is what is *applied*; strain is what *happens* to the material. The stress-strain curve is the "fingerprint" of a material's mechanical behavior.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Stress and Strain Equations

**Tensile/Axial Stress:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A}
$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = Normal (axial) stress in **N/m²** or **MPa**
- $P$ = Applied axial load in **N**
- $A$ = Original cross-sectional area in **m²** or **mm²**

**Tensile/Axial Strain:**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L}
$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon$ = Longitudinal strain (dimensionless)
- $\delta L$ = Change in length in **m** or **mm**
- $L$ = Original gauge length in **m** or **mm**

**Hooke's Law (within elastic limit):**

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon
$$

Where:
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of Elasticity in **GPa** (for mild steel, $E \approx \mathbf{200 \text{ GPa}}$)

**Elongation of a uniform bar:**

$$
\delta L = \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E}
$$

### 2.2 Key Points on the Mild Steel Stress-Strain Curve (Tension Test)

The tensile test on a standard mild steel specimen produces a characteristic curve with the following landmarks:

1. **Proportional Limit (A):** The maximum stress at which stress remains directly proportional to strain (Hooke's Law is fully valid).

2. **Elastic Limit (B):** The maximum stress up to which the material recovers its original dimensions fully upon unloading. Slightly above the proportional limit but practically treated as coincident for engineering calculations.

3. **Upper Yield Point (C):** The stress at which the material begins to deform plastically without any increase in load (or with a small drop in load).

4. **Lower Yield Point (D):** The constant stress at which plastic deformation continues under nearly constant load — the well-defined yield phenomenon of mild steel.

5. **Ultimate Stress / Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) (E):** The maximum stress the material can withstand; beyond this, necking begins.

6. **Breaking/Fracture Point (F):** The stress at which the material finally fractures.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Standard Unit** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Axial Stress | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = P/A$ | $\text{N/m}^2$ or $\text{MPa}$ | Engineering stress uses original area |
| Axial Strain | $\varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon = \delta L / L$ | Dimensionless | Ratio, often in $\mu$ (micro) |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | $\text{GPa}$ | Mild steel $\approx 200\text{ GPa}$ |
| Elongation | $\delta L$ | $\delta L = PL/(AE)$ | $\text{m}$ or $\text{mm}$ | Valid within elastic limit only |
| Shear Stress | $\tau$ | $\tau = V/A$ | $\text{MPa}$ | $V$ = shear force |
| Shear Strain | $\gamma$ | $\gamma = \delta x / L$ | $\text{rad}$ | Angular distortion |
| Shear Modulus | $G$ | $G = \tau / \gamma$ | $\text{GPa}$ | $G \approx 77\text{ GPa}$ for mild steel |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} / \varepsilon_{\text{long}}$ | Dimensionless | $\nu \approx 0.30$ for mild steel |
| Volumetric Strain | $\varepsilon_v$ | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | Dimensionless | For triaxial loading |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **naval architecture**, the stress-strain relationship dictates:

- **Plate thickness selection** for hull plating based on permissible working stress (typically **0.4 to 0.6 times the yield stress**, factoring in safety).
- **Classification society rules** (e.g., Lloyd's Register, DNV, IRS) use the yield stress of mild steel (typically **$\sigma_y = 235 \text{ MPa}$** for Grade A steel) as the foundation for scantling calculations.
- **Failure analysis** of ship structural members when collision, grounding, or heavy weather loading occurs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Young's Modulus from the Stress-Strain Curve

**Step 1 — Definition of stress.**
Consider a uniform prismatic bar of original length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$, subjected to a gradually increasing axial tensile load $P$.

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Stress is defined as force distributed over the area resisting that force. Because we assume the cross-section is uniform and the load is purely axial, the internal resisting force at any section equals the applied external load $P$.

**Step 2 — Definition of strain.**
Under the load $P$, the bar elongates by an amount $\delta L$.

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Strain is a normalized measure of deformation. By dividing the elongation by the original length, we obtain a dimensionless quantity that is independent of the gauge length — a true material property.

**Step 3 — Hooke's Law statement.**
Within the proportional/elastic limit, experiments show that stress is directly proportional to strain:

$$
\sigma \propto \varepsilon
$$

**Step 4 — Introducing the constant of proportionality.**
Introducing the constant $E$ (Young's Modulus) to make the proportionality an equation:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting the expressions.**
Substituting the formulas from Steps 1 and 2:

$$
\frac{P}{A} = E \cdot \frac{\delta L}{L}
$$

**Step 6 — Rearranging for elongation.**

$$
\delta L = \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E}
$$

**Step 7 — Expressing $E$ explicitly.**

$$
E = \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot \delta L} = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Young's Modulus is the slope of the stress-strain curve in the linear elastic region. A high value of $E$ (like $200 \text{ GPa}$ for mild steel) means the material is stiff and resists elastic deformation.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:**
A mild steel tie-rod in a ship's structural frame has a circular cross-section of diameter **20 mm** and an original length of **1.5 m**. It is subjected to an axial tensile load of **60 kN**. Take $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$ for mild steel. Calculate:
(a) The stress in the rod.
(b) The strain in the rod.
(c) The total elongation of the rod.

**Given Data:**

$$
D = 20 \text{ mm}, \quad L = 1.5 \text{ m}, \quad P = 60 \text{ kN} = 60 \times 10^3 \text{ N}
$$

$$
E = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2
$$

**Part (a) — Stress:**

Cross-sectional area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (20 \times 10^{-3})^2
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 400 \times 10^{-6} = 314.159 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Stress:

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{60 \times 10^3}{314.159 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\sigma = 190.99 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 191 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Part (b) — Strain:**

Using Hooke's Law:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{190.99 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = 0.000955 \approx 9.55 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Part (c) — Total Elongation:**

$$
\delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L = 9.55 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 1.5
$$

$$
\delta L = 1.4325 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 1.43 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $\sigma = 191 \text{ MPa}$ is below the typical yield stress of mild steel ($\approx 235 \text{ MPa}$), Hooke's Law is valid and the rod will return to its original length upon unloading. This is the **elastic region**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Stress-Strain Curve for Mild Steel

The following flowchart maps the functional regions of a typical mild steel stress-strain curve, from loading to fracture:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Load Applied - P increases gradually"] --> B["Region 1 - Linear Elastic Zone"]
    B --> C{"Stress exceeds Proportional Limit"}
    C -- "No" --> D["Hookes Law Valid - sigma = E x epsilon"]
    C -- "Yes" --> E["Region 2 - Elastic Limit Reached Point B"]
    E --> F{"Stress crosses Yield Point"}
    F -- "No" --> G["Material returns to original shape"]
    F -- "Yes" --> H["Upper Yield Point C - Plastic flow begins"]
    H --> I["Lower Yield Point D - Constant load plateau"]
    I --> J["Strain Hardening Region"]
    J --> K["Ultimate Tensile Strength E - Maximum stress"]
    K --> L["Necking begins - Cross section reduces"]
    L --> M["Fracture Point F - Material breaks"]
    M --> N["Failure of Mild Steel Specimen"]
    G --> O["Unloading path - Returns along elastic line"]
    O --> P["Original Shape Restored - No permanent deformation"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Stress-Strain Analysis Pipeline

The following block diagram illustrates the analytical workflow used by naval architects to evaluate mild steel components using the stress-strain relationship:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Input - Service Load P"] --> A2["Compute Stress sigma = P over A"]
    A2 --> A3{"Compare with Yield Stress sy"}
    A3 -- "Below sy" --> A4["Elastic Region - Use Hookes Law"]
    A3 -- "Above sy" --> A5["Plastic Region - Permanent Deformation"]
    A4 --> A6["Compute Strain epsilon = sigma over E"]
    A6 --> A7["Compute Elongation deltaL = PL over AE"]
    A7 --> A8["Design Verification - Factor of Safety"]
    A8 --> A9{"FoS greater than Required"}
    A9 -- "Yes" --> A10["Design ACCEPTED"]
    A9 -- "No" --> A11["Redesign - Increase Area or Use Higher Grade"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic Insight:** Notice how the flowchart highlights the critical decision point — whether the applied stress stays below the yield stress. This decision governs whether the ship hull component remains in the safe **elastic regime** or enters the dangerous **plastic regime** that leads to permanent damage.

### 4.3 Tabular Description of Curve Regions

| **Region** | **Stress Range** | **Behavior** | **Recovery on Unloading** |
|---|---|---|---|
| OA | 0 to Proportional Limit | Linear elastic | Fully recovered |
| AB | Proportional to Elastic Limit | Non-linear but still elastic | Fully recovered |
| BC | Elastic Limit to Upper Yield | Onset of plastic flow | Partial recovery |
| CD | Upper to Lower Yield | Yield plateau | Small permanent set |
| DE | Lower Yield to UTS | Strain hardening | Significant permanent set |
| EF | UTS to Fracture | Necking and failure | Material breaks |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define the following terms with neat sketches:**
(a) Elastic limit
(b) Proportional limit
(c) Yield point

**Model Answer:**

- **(a) Elastic Limit:** It is the maximum stress within which a material regains its original shape and dimensions completely upon removal of the applied load. Beyond this stress, permanent deformation occurs.

- **(b) Proportional Limit:** It is the maximum stress at which the strain remains directly proportional to the stress (i.e., Hooke's Law is valid). On the stress-strain curve, it is the endpoint of the straight-line portion from the origin.

- **(c) Yield Point:** It is the stress at which the material begins to deform plastically without any further increase in load (or with a small drop in load). Mild steel exhibits a distinct upper and lower yield point.

---

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2. State Hooke's Law. A mild steel rod of 25 mm diameter and 2 m length is subjected to an axial pull of 75 kN. Find the stress and strain in the rod. Take $E = 200$ GPa.**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $D = 25 \text{ mm}$, $L = 2 \text{ m}$, $P = 75 \text{ kN}$, $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (25)^2 = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Stress:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{75 \times 10^3}{490.87 \times 10^{-6}} = 152.79 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Strain (Hooke's Law):**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{152.79}{200 \times 10^3} = 7.64 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Hooke's Law Statement:** Within the elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain. Mathematically, $\sigma = E \varepsilon$, where $E$ is Young's Modulus.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between stress, strain, and Young's Modulus for a mild steel bar subjected to axial tensile loading. State Hooke's Law and clearly indicate the range of its validity.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mild steel rod in a ship hull structural member has a rectangular cross-section of **40 mm × 20 mm** and an original length of **800 mm**. Under service load, the rod experiences an axial tensile force of **96 kN**. Calculate:
   (i) The stress in the rod.
   (ii) The strain in the rod.
   (iii) The total elongation.
   (iv) Whether the rod remains in the elastic region, given yield stress = 235 MPa and $E = 200$ GPa.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**
- Consider a uniform mild steel bar of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$.
- Apply axial load $P$, producing elongation $\delta L$.
- Stress: $\sigma = P/A$.
- Strain: $\varepsilon = \delta L / L$.
- Within the proportional limit: $\sigma \propto \varepsilon \Rightarrow \sigma = E \varepsilon$.
- Substituting: $P/A = E (\delta L / L)$, hence $\delta L = PL/(AE)$.
- **Validity:** Hooke's Law is valid only up to the proportional limit on the stress-strain curve.

**(b) Numerical:**

Given: $A = 40 \times 20 = 800 \text{ mm}^2 = 800 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$, $L = 800 \text{ mm} = 0.8 \text{ m}$, $P = 96 \text{ kN} = 96 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$.

**(i) Stress:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{96 \times 10^3}{800 \times 10^{-6}} = 120 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 120 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(ii) Strain:**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{120 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 6 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**(iii) Elongation:**

$$
\delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L = 6 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.8 = 4.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.48 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iv) Elastic Region Check:**
Since $\sigma = 120 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_y = 235 \text{ MPa}$, the rod remains in the **elastic region**. Hooke's Law is valid and the rod returns to its original length on unloading.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the help of a clearly labeled sketch, describe the complete stress-strain curve for mild steel under axial tension. Identify all key points and regions.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A circular mild steel bar of diameter **30 mm** and length **1.2 m** is tested in a Universal Testing Machine (UTM). At a load of **120 kN**, the elongation observed is **0.45 mm**. Determine:
   (i) The stress and strain in the bar.
   (ii) Young's Modulus of the material.
   (iii) The Factor of Safety, given yield stress = 250 MPa.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stress-Strain Curve Description:**
- The stress-strain curve starts from the origin O and rises linearly up to point A (proportional limit).
- From A to B, it deviates slightly but is still elastic (elastic limit at B).
- At C, upper yield point occurs with a small drop in stress.
- From D (lower yield point), strain hardening occurs up to E (ultimate tensile strength).
- After E, necking begins until the specimen fractures at F.
- This characteristic yield plateau is what makes mild steel a "ductile material."

**(b) Numerical:**

Given: $D = 30 \text{ mm}$, $L = 1.2 \text{ m} = 1200 \text{ mm}$, $P = 120 \text{ kN}$, $\delta L = 0.45 \text{ mm}$.

**(i) Stress and Strain:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 30^2 = 706.86 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\sigma = \frac{120 \times 10^3}{706.86 \times 10^{-6}} = 169.77 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{0.45}{1200} = 3.75 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**(ii) Young's Modulus:**

$$
E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{169.77 \times 10^6}{3.75 \times 10^{-4}} = 452.72 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} \approx 452.72 \text{ GPa}
$$

*(Note: This unusually high value is theoretically infeasible for mild steel; the KTU examiner may give idealized data.)*

**(iii) Factor of Safety:**

$$
\text{FoS} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{\text{working}}} = \frac{250}{169.77} \approx 1.47
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Mark-Loss Hotspots:**
> - **Unit Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert mm² to m² or kN to N. Always use SI units consistently in the final answer.
> - **Region Violation:** Applying Hooke's Law beyond the elastic limit to compute strain. Always check $\sigma < \sigma_y$ first.
> - **Using $E = 200$ GPa vs $200 \times 10^9$ N/m²:** These are numerically equivalent but unit consistency must be maintained throughout.
> - **Skipping Sketch in Descriptive Questions:** For 7-mark descriptive questions (like Q1A above), a **neat labeled sketch** is mandatory. Skipping it costs a minimum of **1 to 2 marks**.
> - **Confusing Proportional Limit with Elastic Limit:** These are subtly different — the proportional limit is where linearity ends, while the elastic limit is where permanent deformation begins.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mild steel** is a ductile material with carbon content **0.15–0.25%** and $E \approx \mathbf{200 \text{ GPa}}$.
- **Stress** $\sigma = P/A$ — internal resistance per unit area (units: **Pa** or **MPa**).
- **Strain** $\varepsilon = \delta L / L$ — dimensionless ratio of deformation to original length.
- **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ — valid **only within the proportional limit**.
- **Elastic limit** is the maximum stress for full recovery on unloading; **not** the same as the proportional limit.
- **Yield point** is the stress where plastic deformation begins without further load increase (distinct upper and lower yield for mild steel).
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** is the maximum stress the material can sustain; **fracture point** is where it breaks.
- **Factor of Safety (FoS)** = $\sigma_y / \sigma_{\text{working}}$ — a critical design parameter for ship structures.
- Always check **$\sigma < \sigma_y$** before applying Hooke's Law in any problem.
- For naval architecture applications, typical yield stress for **Grade A mild steel** = **235 MPa**.
- Poisson's ratio for mild steel $\nu \approx \mathbf{0.30}$; Shear modulus $G \approx \mathbf{77 \text{ GPa}}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Yield Point
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Yield Point

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Yield Point ($\sigma_y$)** is the specific magnitude of stress at which a material ceases to behave purely elastically and begins to undergo **plastic (permanent) deformation**. It is the transition stress on the engineering stress–strain curve that separates the **elastic region** (recoverable strain) from the **plastic region** (irrecoverable strain).

In the context of **PCNST303 – Solid Mechanics for Naval Architects**, the yield point is the foundational mechanical property used to define the permissible design stress for marine structures such as **ship hull plating, bulkheads, transverse frames, and stiffened panels**. The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the yield point because naval structures must remain in the elastic regime under normal service loading (e.g., still-water bending, wave-induced hull girder loads), and only approach yield under ultimate limit state scenarios (e.g., collision, grounding, or heavy weather).

Mathematically, the yield point is defined as:

$$\sigma_y = \left. \frac{F_y}{A_0} \right|_{\text{onset of plastic flow}}$$

where $F_y$ is the load at yield and $A_0$ is the **original cross-sectional area**.

## 1.2 The Two Sub-Regions: Upper and Lower Yield Point

For materials exhibiting a discontinuous yielding phenomenon (notably **mild steel / low-carbon steel**, the dominant hull material historically used in shipbuilding), the yield point is split into two distinct stress values:

| Term | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Upper Yield Point** | $\sigma_{yu}$ | Stress at which plastic deformation **first initiates** (dislocation pinning breakaway). |
| **Lower Yield Point** | $\sigma_{yl}$ | Stress at which plastic flow **propagates steadily** through the specimen; this is the **design value**. |

$$\sigma_{yl} < \sigma_{yu}$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Paperclip & The Rubber Band

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a **steel paperclip**. If you gently bend it, it springs back — it is in the *elastic* zone. Keep bending it. At some well-defined angle, you'll feel a sudden "give" — the clip bends easily without much extra force. That exact instant is the **yield point**. Bend any further and the paperclip stays bent permanently — the *plastic* zone.

A second analogy helpful for naval architects: consider a **ship's hull girder in a storm**. Under normal wave action, the hull flexes and returns to shape (elastic). During an extreme rogue wave, if the maximum bending stress just touches the yield stress, the hull begins to **set permanently** (hogging or sagging residual curvature) — design codes (e.g., **IACS S21**, **Common Structural Rules**) ensure this never happens under service loads.

## 1.4 Distinction from Related Points on the Curve

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Do **not** confuse the yield point with neighbouring critical points:

- **Proportional Limit ($\sigma_p$):** Stress up to which stress is linearly proportional to strain (Hooke's Law holds: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$).
- **Elastic Limit ($\sigma_e$):** Maximum stress for **full recovery** on unloading (often nearly equal to $\sigma_p$ but conceptually distinct).
- **Yield Point ($\sigma_y$):** Stress at which measurable plastic deformation begins.
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_u$):** Maximum stress the material can sustain.

## 1.5 Why This Matters for Naval Architecture

For a **ship's hull girder**, the **section modulus** $Z$ and **yield stress** $\sigma_y$ directly determine the **ultimate bending moment capacity**:

$$M_u = Z \cdot \sigma_y$$

The **IACS hull girder ultimate strength criterion** explicitly compares the still-water plus wave bending moment against this yield-based capacity, applying partial safety factors per **IACS S21 / CSR-B & CSR-T**.
