## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]`

**Define the term "Working Stress" and state its relationship with the Factor of Safety.**

**Model Answer (Board-Exam Style, 3 marks):**

Working stress is defined as the maximum permissible stress that a structural member is allowed to carry under service conditions, ensuring the material remains in the elastic region with an adequate margin of safety. It is obtained by dividing the yield stress (for ductile materials) or ultimate stress (for brittle materials) by a numerical factor called the **Factor of Safety (n)**.

$$\sigma_{working} = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{n} \quad \text{(ductile materials)}$$

The Factor of Safety is therefore the ratio of the limiting stress of the material to the working stress:

$$n = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{working}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse "yield stress" with "working stress." Remember: **working stress < yield stress**. Also, FoS is *dimensionless* — never write units for it.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]`

**List any THREE assumptions made in the Working Stress Method of design.**

**Model Answer (Board-Exam Style, 3 marks):**

The Working Stress Method is based on the following key assumptions:

1. **Elastic behaviour:** The material obeys Hooke's Law throughout its service life, and the stress-strain relationship is linear. The structure is designed such that stresses remain below the elastic limit.

2. **Homogeneity and isotropy:** The material is assumed to be uniform in composition and has identical mechanical properties in all directions. This allows standard strength-of-materials equations to be applied uniformly.

3. **Deterministic loads:** The applied loads are assumed to be known with reasonable accuracy. The Factor of Safety is selected to compensate for the *residual* uncertainties not captured by the assumed loads.

4. *(Bonus point if mentioned)* The **Factor of Safety is a single global constant**, not differentiated for load type, material, or failure consequence — a limitation of WSM.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Pitfall:** Don't just list "assumptions" vaguely. For full marks, link each assumption to a *practical consequence* in ship design (e.g., "linear elastic ⇒ no plastic reserve strength is utilized").

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A mild steel tie-rod in a ship's engine foundation is subjected to a tensile load of 80 kN. The diameter of the rod is 22 mm. The yield stress and ultimate stress of the steel are 260 MPa and 440 MPa, respectively. Calculate the actual stress induced in the rod. If a Factor of Safety of 3.5 is adopted (based on ultimate stress), check whether the rod is safe.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Working Stress Method of design. Compare it with the Ultimate Stress Method, giving TWO advantages and TWO disadvantages of WSM.

---

### Model Solution — Question A(a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Given data:**

$$P = 80 \text{ kN} = 80{,}000 \text{ N}, \quad d = 22 \text{ mm}, \quad \sigma_y = 260 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_u = 440 \text{ MPa}, \quad n = 3.5$$

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (22)^2}{4} = \frac{1520.53}{4} = 380.13 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Actual (induced) tensile stress:**

$$\sigma_{induced} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{80{,}000}{380.13} = 210.45 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 210.45 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Working (allowable) stress:**

$$\sigma_{working} = \frac{\sigma_u}{n} = \frac{440}{3.5} = 125.71 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Strength check:**

$$\sigma_{induced} = 210.45 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \sigma_{working} = 125.71 \text{ MPa}$$

Since **σ_induced > σ_working** (210.45 > 125.71), the rod is **NOT SAFE**.

**Step 6 — Required new diameter (for completeness):**

$$A_{required} = \frac{P}{\sigma_{working}} = \frac{80{,}000}{125.71} = 636.38 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$d_{required} = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_{required}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 636.38}{\pi}} = 28.47 \text{ mm} \approx 30 \text{ mm}$$

The rod should be replaced with one of diameter ≥ 30 mm.

---

### Model Solution — Question A(b) (7 Marks)

**Working Stress Method (WSM) — Explanation:**

The Working Stress Method is a design philosophy in which a structural member is sized such that the **maximum stress** in the member, computed under the **most adverse expected service load**, does not exceed a **permissible working stress**. The working stress is obtained by dividing the material's yield (or ultimate) stress by a Factor of Safety:

$$\sigma_{working} = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{n}$$

The method assumes **linear-elastic** material behaviour and is the basis of traditional ship scantling rules (e.g., IRS, Lloyd's Register pre-2000 rules).

**Comparison with the Ultimate Stress Method (USM):**

| Aspect | WSM | USM |
|---|---|---|
| Design variable | Stress | Load |
| Strength basis | σ_y / n (elastic limit) | σ_u / n (collapse load) |
| Plastic reserve | Not considered | Fully considered |
| FoS definition | n = σ_y / σ_w | n = P_ultimate / P_working |
| Failure mode | First yield | Collapse / rupture |

**Two Advantages of WSM:**

1. **Simplicity:** Calculations are based on elementary strength-of-materials formulas (P/A, M/Z, τ = VQ/Ib). This makes it easy to apply in routine design.
2. **Deflection control:** Because stresses are kept low, deflections are automatically limited — the structure remains "stiff" under service loads.

**Two Disadvantages of WSM:**

1. **Non-uniform FoS:** A single global FoS is applied to all loads and materials. This is *unrealistic* — a dead load has less uncertainty than a wave load.
2. **Wastes material strength:** WSM does not utilize the *plastic reserve strength* of ductile steel. A ductile structure can carry 30–50% more load before collapse, but WSM stops the design at first yield.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A simply-supported plate of a ship's deck has dimensions 1.0 m × 0.6 m and is subjected to a uniformly distributed pressure load of 50 kN/m². The plate is made of mild steel with σ_y = 250 MPa. If the plate thickness is 12 mm and the design requires a Factor of Safety of 2.5, calculate the maximum bending stress in the plate and check its safety. Take the plate as simply supported on all four edges with aspect ratio b/a = 1.5 (use plate-bending coefficient k ≈ 0.108).

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the difference between **working stress**, **yield stress**, and **ultimate stress** on a stress-strain diagram for mild steel. Also state TWO reasons why the Factor of Safety is essential in ship structural design.

---

### Model Solution — Question B(a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Given data:**

$$a = 1.0 \text{ m} = 1000 \text{ mm}, \quad b = 0.6 \text{ m} = 600 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{or swapped per aspect ratio})$$

For aspect ratio b/a = 1.5, with simply-supported on all four edges, use the *shorter span* as the design span:

$$l = 600 \text{ mm}, \quad t = 12 \text{ mm}, \quad p = 50 \text{ kN/m}^2 = 0.05 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Plate-bending coefficient: **k = 0.108**

**Step 2 — Working stress:**

$$\sigma_{working} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n} = \frac{250}{2.5} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum bending stress in a rectangular plate (isotropic, simply supported):**

The general formula for maximum bending stress in a plate under uniform pressure is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{k \cdot p \cdot l^2}{t^2}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{0.108 \times 0.05 \times (600)^2}{(12)^2}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{0.108 \times 0.05 \times 360{,}000}{144}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{1944}{144} = 13.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Strength check:**

$$\sigma_{max} = 13.5 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \sigma_{working} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

Since **σ_max ≪ σ_working** (13.5 << 100), the plate is **VERY SAFE**. In fact, the design is over-conservative — the designer could potentially reduce thickness. However, **deflection**, **corrosion allowance**, and **classification rules** typically govern the minimum plate thickness on real ships, not just the bending-stress check.

---

### Model Solution — Question B(b) (7 Marks)

**Stress-Strain Diagram for Mild Steel — Key Stresses Identified:**

```
        Stress (sigma)
          |
   sigma_u|----.       *
          |     '.     /
   sigma_y|----. '.---/    ← Yield point
          |     '.|  /
          |     .| /       ← Elastic region (linear)
          |    . |/
          |   .  /
          |  .  /
          | .  /
          |.  /
          +-+--+------------ Strain (epsilon)
            |
            ε_working
            (small)
```

**Description of the Three Stresses:**

1. **Working Stress (σ_w):** The stress the material is *actually* allowed to carry in service. It lies well below the elastic limit. For the example: σ_w = 100 MPa.

2. **Yield Stress (σ_y):** The stress at which the material begins to deform *plastically* (permanently). Beyond this point, the deformation will not fully recover when the load is removed. For mild steel: σ_y ≈ 250 MPa.

3. **Ultimate Stress (σ_u):** The *maximum* stress the material can carry before necking and fracture. For mild steel: σ_u ≈ 400–500 MPa.

**Two Reasons Why Factor of Safety is Essential in Ship Structural Design:**

1. **To account for uncertainties in loads and material properties:** Wave loads in the ocean are statistical and unpredictable. The actual sea state could exceed the design sea state (the 10⁻⁸ probability wave, for example). Material properties also vary between batches of steel. FoS provides a buffer against these uncertainties.

2. **To compensate for corrosion, wear, and structural degradation:** A ship operates for 25–30 years in a corrosive saltwater environment. The plate thickness reduces over time. The FoS ensures the structure remains safe even after decades of service. Classification societies typically prescribe a **corrosion addition** of 0.5–3.0 mm on top of the strength-calculated thickness, and the FoS works alongside this allowance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — High-Mark-Loss Zones:**
>
> 1. **Unit inconsistency is the #1 killer.** Always convert kN·m to N·mm and MPa to N/mm² *before* plugging into a formula. Mixing units forfeits 1–2 marks easily.
> 2. **Confusing "working stress" with "induced stress."** The working stress is a *property of the material + code* (fixed number). The induced stress is what the *load* causes in the member.
> 3. **Forgetting to specify "simply supported" or "fixed" boundary conditions** in plate problems — the plate-bending coefficient *k* depends entirely on edge conditions.
> 4. **Not stating the governing material strength.** Always write "Working stress is based on yield (for ductile materials) or ultimate (for brittle materials)." Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Pack — Topic: Working Stress**

- ✅ **Working stress (σ_w)** = σ_yield / n (for ductile materials like ship steel).
- ✅ **Factor of Safety (n)** = σ_yield / σ_working (dimensionless, always > 1).
- ✅ WSM assumes **linear-elastic** material behaviour — Hooke's Law applies throughout.
- ✅ Typical FoS for **ship hull plating in mild steel** ≈ 2.5 (on yield) → σ_w ≈ 100–160 MPa.
- ✅ Typical FoS for **propellers (cast bronze)** ≈ 5–6 (on ultimate) due to brittle nature.
- ✅ The **strength check** is: σ_calculated ≤ σ_working.
- ✅ For a beam, **Z_required = M / σ_working** (this is the design equation examiners test most).
- ✅ For combined loading (axial + bending + torsion), use **principal stress** formula: σ_1,2 = (σ_x+σ_y)/2 ± √[((σ_x−σ_y)/2)² + τ²].
- ✅ Always check the **maximum principal stress** (σ_1) against σ_working, not just one component stress.
- ✅ WSM is **conservative** — it ignores plastic reserve. The Limit State Method (LSM) addresses this.
- ✅ The classification society rules (IRS, LR, DNV) prescribe **scantlings** so that ship structural components never exceed σ_w under normal service.
- ✅ **Hooke's Law linkage:** ε_working = σ_w / E (used for deflection/stiffness checks in ship hulls).
- ✅ **Standard material data to remember:** Mild steel σ_y ≈ 250 MPa, σ_u ≈ 410 MPa, E = 2×10⁵ MPa.
- ✅ Plate bending formula: **σ_max = k·p·l² / t²** — k depends on aspect ratio and edge conditions.
- ✅ **Units used throughout:** N, mm, N/mm² (= MPa), N·mm. Avoid mixing cm and mm.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Working Stress** | *PCNST303 — Solid Mechanics for Naval Architects* | KTU 2024 Scheme

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Solid Mechanics for Naval Architects**, the **Factor of Safety (FoS)** — also called the **Safety Factor (SF)** or **Design Margin** — is a dimensionless ratio that quantifies the reserve capacity of a structural component or material beyond its expected service loading. Mathematically, it is defined as the ratio between the **ultimate (or yield) capacity** of a structure and the **actual (or design) working stress** applied to it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim syllabus spirit):**
> The Factor of Safety is the ratio of the maximum load a structural member can sustain to the actual (working) load imposed upon it. It provides a numerical measure of the structural reliability against unforeseen overloads, material imperfections, fabrication errors, and environmental uncertainties.

Mathematically:

$$F.o.S = \frac{\text{Ultimum Load Capacity}}{\text{Working / Design Load}}$$

or equivalently in stress terms:

$$F.o.S = \frac{\sigma_{\text{ultimate}}}{\sigma_{\text{working}}} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{\sigma_{\text{working}}}$$

where the appropriate strength parameter (ultimate or yield) is selected based on the failure criterion under consideration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are crossing a frozen lake. The ice appears thick enough to hold 5 people safely. Would you let **exactly** 5 people walk onto it? Almost certainly not — you would restrict it to **2 or 3 people** to leave a margin. That "extra room" you deliberately leave is, in engineering terms, the **Factor of Safety**.

In naval architecture, a ship's hull plate is never designed to operate at exactly its theoretical collapse stress. Instead, it is engineered to remain well below that limit, because:

- Sea states are unpredictable (rogue waves, slamming loads).
- Steel thickness can vary slightly during rolling.
- Welds may have minor defects.
- Corrosion slowly reduces thickness over the ship's service life.

Hence, designers intentionally **derate** the structural capacity by a Factor of Safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** FoS is *not* a sign of poor engineering — it is the **acknowledgement of uncertainty** in materials, loads, and the environment. A well-designed ship will *never* approach its theoretical collapse load in normal operation.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Typical FoS for ship structural steel (yield-based):** **1.5 to 2.5** (depending on classification society).
- **Typical FoS for yield-to-ultimate ratio of mild steel:** approximately **1.5 to 1.7**.
- **Units:** **Dimensionless** (pure ratio).
- **Commonly designated symbol in naval architecture texts:** **γ** (gamma), *n*, or **SF** (Safety Factor).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is a Factor of Safety Required?

The necessity of a Factor of Safety arises from multiple sources of uncertainty in real engineering systems. KTU examiners frequently probe *why* designers don't simply set working stress equal to material strength. The structured logic is:

- **Material Variability:** Manufactured steel has scatter in yield strength, ultimate strength, and ductility. The nominal value used in design is a statistical lower bound.
- **Load Uncertainty:** Environmental loads (waves, wind, slamming, vibration) cannot be predicted exactly. Statistical distributions of wave height, period, and direction govern design loads.
- **Manufacturing Defects:** Welds may have porosity, inclusions, or undercutting. Plate thickness tolerances exist.
- **Corrosion and Fatigue:** Over a 25-year service life, corrosion can reduce plate thickness by 1–3 mm.
- **Simplification of Analysis:** Theoretical models (beam theory, plate theory) approximate the real 3D stress state.
- **Consequence of Failure:** Failure of a hull girder is catastrophic (loss of life, vessel, cargo, environmental damage), so a conservative margin is mandatory.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Whenever asked "Why is FoS > 1 needed?", always mention at least **three** of the above reasons — single-point answers lose marks.

### 2.2 Types of Factor of Safety

#### (a) Factor of Safety based on Yield Strength

$$F.o.S_{y} = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{\text{working}}}$$

Used for ductile materials like mild shipbuilding steel where yielding (plastic deformation) is the failure criterion.

#### (b) Factor of Safety based on Ultimate Strength

$$F.o.S_{u} = \frac{\sigma_{u}}{\sigma_{\text{working}}}$$

Used for brittle materials (cast iron, some alloys) where fracture occurs without significant yielding.

#### (c) Factor of Safety based on Buckling Load

$$F.o.S_{b} = \frac{P_{\text{cr}}}{P_{\text{working}}}$$

Critical for ship plates, stiffened panels, and slender columns where elastic instability governs.

#### (d) Factor of Safety based on Fatigue Endurance

$$F.o.S_{f} = \frac{S_{e}}{S_{\text{working}}}$$

Used for components subjected to cyclic wave loading over the design life.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application in Naval Architecture | Typical Value |
|---|---------|-----------------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $F.o.S = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{strength}}}{\sigma_{\text{working}}}$ | General definition | $\geq 1.5$ |
| 2 | $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{F.o.S}$ | Allowable stress design | varies |
| 3 | $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{u}}{F.o.S}$ | Brittle material design | varies |
| 4 | $F.o.S_{y} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{w}}$ | Yield-based design (steel) | $1.5$–$2.0$ |
| 5 | $F.o.S_{u} = \dfrac{\sigma_{u}}{\sigma_{w}}$ | Ultimate-based design (cast iron) | $3.0$–$4.0$ |
| 6 | $n = \dfrac{P_{\text{cr}}}{P_{\text{applied}}}$ | Column/plate buckling | $2.0$–$3.0$ |
| 7 | $\sigma_{w} = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Working stress from axial load | — |
| 8 | $M_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{M_{y}}{F.o.S}$ | Allowable bending moment for hull girder | — |
| 9 | $\gamma_{m} \sigma_{w} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\gamma_{M}}$ | Partial safety factor (LRFD) form | — |
| 10 | $SF = \dfrac{M_{\text{ult}}}{M_{\text{service}}}$ | Ultimate hull girder moment check | $1.2$–$1.5$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Naval Architecture Context:** Classification societies such as **DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, and IRS** each prescribe their own minimum FoS values for hull girder ultimate strength, plate yielding, and buckling. Always cite the relevant rule when solving KTU problems.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In ship design, the Factor of Safety governs:

- **Scantling (thickness) determination** of hull plates and stiffeners.
- **Hull girder ultimate strength** assessments under combined bending and shear.
- **Buckling checks** for slender plate panels between stiffeners.
- **Slamming and whipping** response in high-speed vessels.
- **Corrosion addition** — typically **0.5 mm to 2 mm** added to the calculated thickness before applying the FoS.

A well-chosen FoS balances **safety** against **economic penalty** (heavier ship → higher fuel consumption, lower payload).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Allowable Working Stress from FoS

Starting from the fundamental definition of the Factor of Safety for a ductile material (where yielding is the failure criterion):

$$F.o.S = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{\text{working}}}$$

Rearranging to express the working (allowable) stress in terms of yield strength and FoS:

$$\sigma_{\text{working}} \cdot F.o.S = \sigma_{y}$$

$$\sigma_{\text{working}} = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{F.o.S}$$

This is the **Allowable Stress Design (ASD)** formula. The design procedure is:

- **Step 1:** Identify the material yield strength $\sigma_{y}$ (from classification society rules or test data).
- **Step 2:** Determine the design load $P_{\text{design}}$ (e.g., still water bending moment plus wave contribution).
- **Step 3:** Compute the working stress $\sigma_{w} = P/A$ or $Mc/I$.
- **Step 4:** Verify $\sigma_{w} \leq \sigma_{y}/F.o.S$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Required Thickness for a Hull Plate

Consider a simply supported rectangular hull plate of span $l$ and width $b$ subjected to a uniform lateral pressure $p$ (hydrostatic + hydrodynamic). The maximum bending stress at mid-span is:

$$\sigma_{\text{max}} = \frac{M_{\text{max}}}{Z} = \frac{p l^{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{6}{b t^{2}} = \frac{3 p l^{2}}{b t^{2}}$$

For the plate to be safe with a Factor of Safety $n$:

$$\sigma_{\text{max}} \leq \frac{\sigma_{y}}{n}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{3 p l^{2}}{b t^{2}} \leq \frac{\sigma_{y}}{n}$$

Solving for the minimum thickness $t_{\min}$:

$$\frac{3 n p l^{2}}{b \sigma_{y}} \leq t^{2}$$

$$t_{\min} = \sqrt{\frac{3 n p l^{2}}{b \sigma_{y}}}$$

This is the **fundamental plate thickness formula** used (with modifications) by every classification society.

### 3.3 Analytical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** A ship hull plate is made of mild steel with yield strength $\sigma_{y} = 235$ MPa. The plate is subjected to a working tensile stress of $\sigma_{w} = 120$ MPa under normal seagoing conditions.

**(a)** Calculate the Factor of Safety with respect to yielding.
**(b)** If the same plate is expected to encounter a slamming load producing a transient stress of $280$ MPa, calculate the **reduced** Factor of Safety.
**(c)** Comment on the adequacy of design.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Apply the FoS definition based on yield:

$$F.o.S = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{w}} = \frac{235 \text{ MPa}}{120 \text{ MPa}}$$

$$\boxed{F.o.S = 1.958}$$

This falls comfortably in the typical safe range of $1.5$–$2.0$.

**(b)** During slamming, the working stress rises transiently to $\sigma_{w,\text{sl}} = 280$ MPa:

$$F.o.S_{\text{sl}} = \frac{235}{280} = 0.839$$

Since $F.o.S < 1$, the plate **would yield** under this slamming load. Therefore:

**(c)** The design is **inadequate** for transient slamming conditions. Engineering remedies include:

- Increasing plate thickness (reduces working stress).
- Using higher-grade steel (e.g., $\sigma_{y} = 315$ MPa or $355$ MPa).
- Adding local stiffeners to reduce the effective span.
- Applying a partial safety factor approach to transient loads.

### 3.4 Numerical Comparison Matrix

| Loading Condition | Working Stress (MPa) | Yield Strength (MPa) | FoS | Verdict |
|-------------------|----------------------|-----------------------|------|---------|
| Still water (SW) | $80$ | $235$ | $2.94$ | Very Safe |
| Normal seagoing (SW + wave) | $120$ | $235$ | $1.96$ | Safe |
| Heavy weather (slamming) | $280$ | $235$ | $0.84$ | UNSAFE |
| Harbor (light condition) | $40$ | $235$ | $5.88$ | Over-designed (consider lightweighting) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** The Factor of Safety is **load-condition dependent**. A structure may be safe under one condition and unsafe under another. KTU examiners reward answers that distinguish between these cases.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Role of FoS in Ship Structural Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Loads Input] --> B[Load Combination Module]
    C[Material Database] --> D[Material Strength Module]
    B --> E[Working Stress Calculator]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Apply Factor of Safety n]
    F --> G{Stress Check}
    G --> H[Pass: Section OK]
    G --> I[Fail: Redesign]
    I --> J[Increase Thickness]
    I --> K[Use Higher Grade Steel]
    I --> L[Add Stiffeners]
    J --> E
    K --> E
    L --> E
    H --> M[Final Scantling Output]
    M --> N[Classification Society Approval]
```

**Interpretation of the Flowchart:**

- **A → B:** The design process starts by combining still water, wave, dynamic, and accidental loads.
- **C → D:** Material properties (yield, ultimate, modulus) are fetched from the database.
- **E:** The working stress is computed from the combined load using structural mechanics.
- **F:** The Factor of Safety is applied as a multiplier or divisor.
- **G:** A binary stress check evaluates whether the actual stress is within allowable limits.
- **I → J / K / L:** If the check fails, iterative redesign occurs.
- **N:** Only after all class-rule compliance is achieved is the design released.

### 4.2 Hierarchy of Strength Levels

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ultimate Strength sigma_u] --> B[Yield Strength sigma_y]
    B --> C[Allowable Stress sigma_allow]
    C --> D[Working Stress sigma_w]
    D --> E[Safety Margin]
    A -.Factor n_u.-> C
    B -.Factor n_y.-> C
    C -.Margin.-> D
```

This illustrates that the allowable stress sits between the material strength (top) and the working stress (bottom), with the Factor of Safety being the **gap** between them.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Factor of Safety" as applied to a ship structural member. Why is it always kept greater than unity?*

**Model Answer:**
The Factor of Safety is the ratio of the strength (load-carrying capacity) of a structural member to the actual working load applied on it. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$F.o.S = \frac{\text{Strength of member}}{\text{Working load}}$$

It is kept greater than unity (i.e., $F.o.S > 1$) to account for uncertainties in material properties, fabrication tolerances, unforeseen overloads, environmental degradation (corrosion), and the catastrophic consequences of failure. For ship structures, classification societies typically prescribe FoS values between **1.5 and 2.5** depending on the failure mode.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between the Factor of Safety based on yield strength and that based on ultimate strength. Which is preferred for mild shipbuilding steel and why?*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Yield-based FoS | Ultimate-based FoS |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| Definition | $\sigma_{y}/\sigma_{w}$ | $\sigma_{u}/\sigma_{w}$ |
| Failure criterion | First yield / plastic deformation | Fracture / rupture |
| Suitable material | Ductile (mild steel, aluminum) | Brittle (cast iron, ceramics) |

For **mild shipbuilding steel**, the yield-based FoS is preferred because steel is **highly ductile** and yields well before fracture. Designing to ultimate strength would allow large plastic deformations, which are unacceptable for a ship hull (loss of watertight integrity).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** A steel column supporting a deckhouse of a naval vessel has a cross-sectional area of $A = 6000 \text{ mm}^{2}$ and a length of $L = 3.5$ m. It is pinned at both ends. The material is mild steel with Young's modulus $E = 210$ GPa and yield strength $\sigma_{y} = 250$ MPa. Using Euler's formula, determine the **critical buckling load**. If the column carries a working axial compressive load of $P = 180$ kN, calculate the **Factor of Safety against buckling**. State whether the design is safe. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship's hull bottom plate is subjected to a hydrostatic pressure of $p = 35$ kPa. The plate spans between stiffeners over a distance of $l = 0.8$ m and has a width $b = 1$ m. If the yield strength of the plate material is $\sigma_{y} = 235$ MPa, calculate the **minimum required thickness** using a Factor of Safety of $n = 2.0$ against yielding. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a):**

Given:
- Cross-sectional area $A = 6000 \text{ mm}^{2} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$
- Length $L = 3.5$ m
- Young's modulus $E = 210$ GPa $= 210 \times 10^{9}$ Pa
- Yield strength $\sigma_{y} = 250$ MPa
- Working load $P = 180$ kN $= 180 \times 10^{3}$ N
- End condition: pinned-pinned → effective length factor $K = 1$

Assume moment of inertia $I$ for a circular hollow section approximation. Since $I$ is not given explicitly, we use the **slenderness-ratio based** Euler formula:

For buckling, we need $I_{\min}$. Without explicit $I$, the problem requires us to compute the **critical slenderness ratio** first.

Step 1: Critical stress for yielding (Euler validity check):

$$\sigma_{\text{Euler-valid}} = 0.5 \sigma_{y} = 125 \text{ MPa}$$

Step 2: Critical slenderness ratio:

$$\lambda_{c} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{E}{0.5 \sigma_{y}}} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{210 \times 10^{9}}{125 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$\lambda_{c} = \pi \sqrt{1680} = \pi \times 40.99 = 128.78$$

Step 3: Working compressive stress:

$$\sigma_{w} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{180 \times 10^{3}}{6 \times 10^{-3}} = 30 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 30 \text{ MPa}$$

Step 4: To determine buckling behavior, assume a typical structural column with $\lambda = 100$ (slender). Euler critical stress:

$$\sigma_{\text{cr}} = \frac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times 210 \times 10^{9}}{100^{2}} = 207.3 \text{ MPa}$$

Step 5: Critical buckling load:

$$P_{\text{cr}} = \sigma_{\text{cr}} \cdot A = 207.3 \times 10^{6} \times 6 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{cr}} = 1.244 \text{ MN} = 1244 \text{ kN}}$$

Step 6: Factor of Safety against buckling:

$$F.o.S = \frac{P_{\text{cr}}}{P} = \frac{1244}{180} = 6.91$$

$$\boxed{F.o.S = 6.91}$$

Since $F.o.S > 2.0$ (typical for columns), the design is **very safe** against buckling, possibly over-designed. Lightweighting may be considered.

---

**Part (b):**

Using the derived formula from Section 3.2:

$$t_{\min} = \sqrt{\frac{3 n p l^{2}}{b \sigma_{y}}}$$

Substituting values:
- $n = 2.0$
- $p = 35 \times 10^{3}$ Pa
- $l = 0.8$ m
- $b = 1$ m
- $\sigma_{y} = 235 \times 10^{6}$ Pa

$$t_{\min} = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 2.0 \times 35 \times 10^{3} \times (0.8)^{2}}{1 \times 235 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$t_{\min} = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 2.0 \times 35 \times 10^{3} \times 0.64}{235 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$t_{\min} = \sqrt{\frac{134400}{235 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$t_{\min} = \sqrt{5.719 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{t_{\min} = 0.02391 \text{ m} \approx 23.91 \text{ mm}}$$

Including a standard **corrosion addition** of $2$ mm:

$$t_{\text{as-built}} = 23.91 + 2.0 = 25.91 \text{ mm} \approx 26 \text{ mm}$$

A standard shipbuilding plate thickness of $26$ mm should be adopted.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define Factor of Safety. A steel tie-rod in a ship's engine mounting has a diameter of $25$ mm and is subjected to a tensile working load of $80$ kN. The ultimate tensile strength of the material is $\sigma_{u} = 410$ MPa and the yield strength is $\sigma_{y} = 250$ MPa. Determine: (i) working stress, (ii) FoS based on yield, (iii) FoS based on ultimate strength. Comment on which is the governing criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship's hatch cover is supported by four pillars, each of cross-sectional area $A = 4000 \text{ mm}^{2}$. The hatch experiences a cargo load of $W = 320$ kN evenly distributed. The yield strength of pillar material is $\sigma_{y} = 260$ MPa. Determine the Factor of Safety and state whether the design complies with a classification society rule that requires minimum FoS $= 1.8$ for primary supporting members. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a):**

Step 1: Cross-sectional area of the tie-rod:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^{2} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$$

Step 2: Working tensile stress:

$$\sigma_{w} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{80 \times 10^{3}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\sigma_{w} = 162.97 \text{ MPa}$$

Step 3: FoS based on yield:

$$F.o.S_{y} = \frac{250}{162.97} = 1.534$$

Step 4: FoS based on ultimate strength:

$$F.o.S_{u} = \frac{410}{162.97} = 2.516$$

**Comment:** Since mild steel is **ductile**, the **yield-based FoS** ($1.534$) is the governing criterion. Although $F.o.S_u > 2$, the material will yield before reaching ultimate fracture, so yielding is the true failure mode. The FoS is borderline acceptable (typical minimum $= 1.5$); for engine mountings which experience vibration, a higher FoS ($\approx 2.0$) is recommended.

---

**Part (b):**

Step 1: Load per pillar (4 pillars equally share the load):

$$P_{\text{pillar}} = \frac{W}{4} = \frac{320}{4} = 80 \text{ kN}$$

Step 2: Working stress in each pillar:

$$\sigma_{w} = \frac{P_{\text{pillar}}}{A} = \frac{80 \times 10^{3}}{4000 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{80 \times 10^{3}}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 20 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\sigma_{w} = 20 \text{ MPa}$$

Step 3: Factor of Safety:

$$F.o.S = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{w}} = \frac{260}{20} = 13.0$$

Step 4: Compliance check:

$$F.o.S = 13.0 \gg 1.8$$

The design **comfortably complies** with the classification rule. However, this indicates significant **over-design**, suggesting the pillars could be reduced in cross-section to save weight, provided the buckling check is also passed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Unit Consistency:** Always convert areas to **m²** and stresses to **Pa** before substituting into formulas. Mixing mm² with MPa is a common error costing **1–2 marks**.
> - **Showing intermediate steps:** Examiners reward clear working. Simply writing the final answer without intermediate calculations will lose **at least 1 mark** per sub-question.
> - **Identifying the governing criterion:** In any "yield vs. ultimate" problem, you MUST state *which* governs and *why* — this single sentence can earn or cost **1 mark**.
> - **Forgetting corrosion addition:** In ship plate problems, examiners often expect a corrosion allowance (typically $1$–$2$ mm) added to the calculated thickness. Skipping this is a frequent oversight.
> - **Sign convention:** Compressive FoS calculations must use absolute values — students sometimes wrongly apply sign conventions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** FoS = Strength Capacity / Working Load — a dimensionless ratio.
- **Typical values:** **1.5–2.5** for ship steel against yielding; **2.0–3.0** against buckling.
- **Ductile materials (steel):** Use **yield-based** FoS — yielding is the failure mode.
- **Brittle materials (cast iron):** Use **ultimate-based** FoS — fracture is sudden.
- **Allowable stress formula:** $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \sigma_{y}/n$ (for ASD).
- **Hull plate minimum thickness:** $t_{\min} = \sqrt{3 n p l^{2} / (b \sigma_{y})}$.
- **Column buckling (Euler):** $P_{\text{cr}} = \pi^{2} E I / (K L)^{2}$ — valid for slender columns only.
- **Classification societies:** DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, IRS — each prescribe minimum FoS for different components.
- **Corrosion allowance:** Always add **1–2 mm** to calculated plate thickness.
- **Partial safety factor (LRFD) form:** Use $\gamma_{M}$ on material and $\gamma_{F}$ on loads — common in modern rule sets.
- **Load-condition dependency:** FoS changes with operating condition (still water vs. slamming vs. harbor).
- **Consequence of FoS = 1:** Structure is on the verge of failure — design is unsafe.
- **Consequence of FoS >> 1:** Over-design → economic penalty, recommend lightweighting.
- **Always verify** that the slenderness ratio check ($0 < \lambda < \lambda_{c}$) is satisfied before applying Euler's buckling formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Volumetric Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Volumetric Strain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Volumetric Strain** (also called *dilatation* or *bulk strain*) is defined as the ratio of the change in volume of a body to its original volume, due to the action of external loads. Mathematically, for a body of original volume $V$ experiencing a change in volume $\Delta V$, the volumetric strain is:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V}$$

For a body loaded in three orthogonal directions (x, y, z), the linear strains are $\varepsilon_x$, $\varepsilon_y$, and $\varepsilon_z$. For **infinitesimally small strains** (which is the standard KTU assumption under Hookean behavior), the volumetric strain equals the algebraic sum of the three normal (principal) strains:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Naval Architects, volumetric strain governs the design of **pressure hulls of submarines, deep submersibles, and underwater pipelines**, where uniform hydrostatic compression can cause the entire internal cavity to shrink. The capacity of a vessel to retain its habitable/operational volume under crush depth is governed directly by $\varepsilon_v$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge in the Deep Sea"

Imagine a sealed, air-filled **rubber sponge cube** lowered into the ocean. As it descends:
- The water pushes equally on **all six faces** of the cube (hydrostatic pressure).
- The cube does not change shape (no shear), but its **size shrinks** in every direction.
- The air trapped inside is compressed into a smaller volume.

This reduction in total volume, divided by the original volume, is exactly the **volumetric strain**. In a submarine, the *hull* plays the role of the sponge, and the *interior crew space* is the trapped air — the engineer must ensure $\Delta V$ stays within tolerable limits so that equipment, batteries, and human-rated volumes are not unacceptably distorted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - **Tensile strain** → Volume **increases** → $\varepsilon_v > 0$ (positive)
> - **Compressive strain** → Volume **decreases** → $\varepsilon_v < 0$ (negative)
>
> For a body immersed in seawater, hydrostatic pressure produces a **negative** volumetric strain.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (Mild Steel) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | $\mathbf{2.0 \times 10^{5}}$ | $\mathrm{N/mm^2}$ (MPa) |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | $\mathbf{0.30}$ | dimensionless |
| Bulk Modulus | $K$ | $\mathbf{1.67 \times 10^{5}}$ | $\mathrm{N/mm^2}$ (MPa) |
| Seawater Bulk Modulus | $K_{sw}$ | $\mathbf{2.30 \times 10^{3}}$ | $\mathrm{N/mm^2}$ (MPa) |

> [!TIP]
> **Naval Architecture Insight:** Because $K_{sw} \approx 2300\ \mathrm{MPa}$ is roughly **80–100 times smaller** than that of steel ($K \approx 167000\ \mathrm{MPa}$), seawater compresses far more readily than the hull. In **buoyancy and stability** calculations for submarines, the compressibility of seawater (via its bulk modulus) must be included, while the steel hull's volume change is often treated as a *secondary correction*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Breaking Down the Concept

**Step 1 — Identify the Strain State**
- Confirm that the body experiences **only normal stresses** on three mutually perpendicular faces.
- Identify whether the loading is **uniaxial, biaxial, or triaxial**, and whether stresses are equal (hydrostatic) or unequal (general triaxial).

**Step 2 — Resolve Each Linear Strain**
For a linearly elastic, homogeneous, isotropic body, the strain in any one principal direction is the *direct stress contribution* minus the *lateral contraction effects* (governed by Poisson's ratio $\nu$):

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

By symmetry, similar expressions hold for $\varepsilon_y$ and $\varepsilon_z$.

**Step 3 — Sum the Three Principal Strains**
The cubic dilatation (volumetric strain) is the **scalar sum**:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify**
Upon substituting the three strain equations and collecting the stress terms:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$$

This is the **master equation** for volumetric strain under triaxial stress.

**Step 5 — Connect to Bulk Modulus**
By definition, **Bulk Modulus** $K$ is the ratio of hydrostatic stress $\sigma_h$ to volumetric strain $\varepsilon_v$:

$$K = \frac{\sigma_h}{\varepsilon_v} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Formula:**
> - The factor $(1-2\nu)$ appears because **both** the direct stress **and** the Poisson-induced lateral effects influence volume change. A material with $\nu = 0.5$ is **incompressible** — its volume cannot change under any hydrostatic stress, so $K \to \infty$.
> - For steel ($\nu = 0.3$): $1 - 2\nu = 0.4$, so $K = E/(3 \times 0.4) = E/1.2$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V}$ | Basic definition of volumetric strain | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | Sum of principal strains (small-strain theory) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{(1-2\nu)}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$ | Volumetric strain under triaxial stress | dimensionless |
| 4 | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | Bulk Modulus in terms of $E$ and $\nu$ | MPa or $\mathrm{N/mm^2}$ |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\sigma_h}{K} = \dfrac{3\sigma_h(1-2\nu)}{E}$ | Volumetric strain under hydrostatic pressure $\sigma_h$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{-3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$ | Volumetric strain under external hydrostatic pressure $p$ (sign: compression) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{p\,d\,(1-2\nu)}{4\,t\,E}\left[3\right]$ *(thin cyl.)* | Volumetric strain of a thin cylindrical shell of dia. $d$, thickness $t$, internal pressure $p$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{p}{K}$ | Direct relation for hydrostatic loading | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\nu \le 0.5$ | Theoretical upper bound (incompressibility limit) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - In **submarine pressure hull design**, $\varepsilon_v$ sets the limit on **collapse depth**.
> - In **hydrostatic testing** of marine pipelines, $\varepsilon_v$ predictions validate the test pressure.
> - In **bulk-cargo tankers**, free-surface effect corrections depend on the volumetric strain of liquid cargo.

### 2.3 Relation to Shear Strain (For Completeness)

It is useful for a Naval Architect to remember that volumetric strain is **independent of shear strain**. Shear deformation alters *shape* but conserves *volume* (to first order). Hence:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z \quad \text{(volume change)}$$

while the **deviatoric strain** (related to shape distortion) is governed by the **octahedral shear strain** $\gamma_{\text{oct}}$ — a separate quantity not covered under volumetric strain.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Whenever a problem states *"uniform pressure on all faces"* or *"hydrostatic compression"*, jump directly to equation **(6)**. Do not re-derive the three strain components individually.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Volumetric Strain from Three Normal Strains

**Given:** A rectangular parallelepiped (a small elemental cube) of original dimensions $dx$, $dy$, $dz$ along the principal axes, subjected to normal strains $\varepsilon_x$, $\varepsilon_y$, $\varepsilon_z$.

**To Prove:** $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$

**Step 1 — Original Volume**
The original volume of the elemental cube is simply the product of its three sides:

$$V = dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$$

**Step 2 — Deformed Dimensions**
After deformation, the new dimensions are $(1+\varepsilon_x)dx$, $(1+\varepsilon_y)dy$, $(1+\varepsilon_z)dz$.

**Step 3 — New Volume**
The deformed volume is:

$$V' = (1+\varepsilon_x)dx \cdot (1+\varepsilon_y)dy \cdot (1+\varepsilon_z)dz$$

**Step 4 — Expand the Product**
Multiplying out:

$$V' = dx\,dy\,dz \,(1+\varepsilon_x)(1+\varepsilon_y)(1+\varepsilon_z)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Binomial Expansion**
Using the standard expansion $(1+a)(1+b)(1+c) = 1 + a + b + c + ab + ac + bc + abc$:

$$V' = dx\,dy\,dz\,\left[1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z + \varepsilon_x\varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_y\varepsilon_z + \varepsilon_z\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_x\varepsilon_y\varepsilon_z\right]$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Small-Strain Assumption**
Under the KTU small-strain hypothesis, each $\varepsilon \ll 1$, so the **second-order and third-order products are negligible**:

$$\varepsilon_x\varepsilon_y \approx 0,\quad \varepsilon_y\varepsilon_z \approx 0,\quad \varepsilon_z\varepsilon_x \approx 0,\quad \varepsilon_x\varepsilon_y\varepsilon_z \approx 0$$

**Step 7 — Simplify**
The expression reduces to:

$$V' = dx\,dy\,dz\,\left[1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z\right]$$

**Step 8 — Compute the Change in Volume**

$$\Delta V = V' - V = dx\,dy\,dz\,\left[\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z\right] = V\,(\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z)$$

**Step 9 — Form the Ratio**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important result** in the volumetric strain topic. The KTU board almost always awards marks for this derivation in either 3-mark or 14-mark questions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Volumetric Strain under Triaxial Stress

**Given:** A homogeneous isotropic body subjected to principal stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_z$ along the x, y, z axes. Material constants are $E$ (Young's Modulus) and $\nu$ (Poisson's Ratio).

**To Prove:** $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{(1-2\nu)}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$

**Step 1 — Generalized Hooke's Law**
For a triaxially loaded body, the strain in the x-direction is:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

This arises because the stress $\sigma_x$ *directly elongates* the body in x, while the *lateral stresses* $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ cause a *lateral contraction* in x (via Poisson coupling), reducing the net strain.

**Step 2 — Write All Three Strain Components**
By cyclic symmetry of x → y → z:

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Sum the Three Strains**

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{1}{E}\left\{[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)] + [\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)] + [\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)]\right\}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the Bracket**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{1}{E}\left\{\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z - \nu\left[(\sigma_y+\sigma_z)+(\sigma_x+\sigma_z)+(\sigma_x+\sigma_y)\right]\right\}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the Poisson Bracket**
Counting the occurrences of each stress in the bracket:

$$(\sigma_y+\sigma_z) + (\sigma_x+\sigma_z) + (\sigma_x+\sigma_y) = 2\sigma_x + 2\sigma_y + 2\sigma_z = 2(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Back**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{1}{E}\left\{(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z) - 2\nu(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)\right\}$$

**Step 7 — Factor Out the Stress Sum**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)}{E}\left\{1 - 2\nu\right\}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Aid:** "**One minus two-nu over E, times the sum of three stresses**." This is sometimes written using the **mean stress** $\sigma_m = (\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)/3$ as $\varepsilon_v = 3\sigma_m(1-2\nu)/E$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Relation between Bulk Modulus, Young's Modulus, and Poisson's Ratio

**Given:** A body under hydrostatic pressure $p$ (so $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$).

**Step 1 — Identify the Hydrostatic Stress**
Under uniform external pressure $p$, all three principal stresses are equal and compressive:

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Master Equation**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{E}\left[(-p) + (-p) + (-p)\right] = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{E}(-3p)$$

$$\varepsilon_v = -\frac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Definition of Bulk Modulus**
The bulk modulus $K$ is defined as the ratio of hydrostatic stress (taken as a positive magnitude) to the magnitude of volumetric strain:

$$K = \frac{p}{\vert \varepsilon_v \vert} = \frac{p}{\dfrac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **high-yield KTU question** — almost every exam cycle features a 3-mark or 7-mark sub-part asking to derive or apply this relationship.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Submarine Pressure Hull Volume Change

**Problem (Modeled on KTU 2024 Pattern):** A cylindrical submarine pressure hull has internal diameter $d = 2.0\ \mathrm{m}$, wall thickness $t = 40\ \mathrm{mm}$, and is submerged to a depth where the external hydrostatic pressure is $p = 4.0\ \mathrm{MPa}$. The hull material has $E = 2.0 \times 10^5\ \mathrm{MPa}$ and $\nu = 0.30$. Assuming thin-wall theory, calculate the volumetric strain of the hull material.

**Step 1 — Identify the Stress State**
For a thin cylindrical shell under external pressure $p$ (no internal pressurization for a sealed hull):
- Hoop stress: $\sigma_h = -\dfrac{pd}{2t}$
- Longitudinal stress: $\sigma_L = -\dfrac{pd}{4t}$
- Radial stress: $\sigma_r \approx 0$ (thin-wall assumption)

**Step 2 — Calculate the Stresses Numerically**

$$\sigma_h = -\frac{4.0 \times 2000}{2 \times 40} = -\frac{8000}{80} = -100\ \mathrm{MPa}$$

$$\sigma_L = -\frac{4.0 \times 2000}{4 \times 40} = -\frac{8000}{160} = -50\ \mathrm{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Master Volumetric Strain Equation**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{E}\left(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z\right)$$

Substituting $\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z = \sigma_h + \sigma_L + \sigma_r = -100 + (-50) + 0 = -150\ \mathrm{MPa}$:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{(1-2 \times 0.30)}{2.0 \times 10^5} \times (-150)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{0.40 \times (-150)}{2.0 \times 10^5} = \frac{-60}{2.0 \times 10^5} = -3.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the Result**

$$\varepsilon_v = -3.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{(dimensionless)} = -0.03\%$$

The hull material's volume decreases by **0.03%** under this loading — a small but design-significant number for submarine interior volume accounting.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Marking Scheme:** Award **2 marks** for correct stress calculation, **3 marks** for the correct application of the master equation, and **2 marks** for the final numerical answer with proper sign and units.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Volumetric Strain Derivation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block-diagram traces the **logical pipeline** of the volumetric strain derivation, from input stresses to final strain and bulk modulus outputs. Each stage represents a key step in the analytical chain taught in Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Principal Stresses sigma_x, sigma_y, sigma_z] --> B[Step 1: Apply Generalized Hookes Law in 3D]
    B --> C[Compute epsilon_x = 1/E times sigma_x minus nu times sigma_y plus sigma_z]
    C --> D[Compute epsilon_y and epsilon_z by cyclic symmetry]
    D --> E[Step 2: Algebraic Sum of Three Principal Strains]
    E --> F[Step 3: Factor out the stress sum and Poisson factor]
    F --> G[Master Equation: epsilon_v equals 1 minus 2 nu over E times sum of stresses]
    G --> H{Boundary Condition Check}
    H -->|Hydrostatic: sigma_x = sigma_y = sigma_z = minus p| I[Step 4a: Substitute equal stresses]
    H -->|General Triaxial| J[Step 4b: Substitute individual stresses]
    I --> K[Result: epsilon_v equals minus 3 p times 1 minus 2 nu over E]
    J --> L[Result: epsilon_v equals 1 minus 2 nu over E times stress sum]
    K --> M[Step 5: Apply Bulk Modulus Definition K equals p over epsilon_v]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Final Result: K equals E over 3 times 1 minus 2 nu]
    N --> O[Output: Volumetric Strain and Bulk Modulus]
    O --> P[Naval Architecture Application: Pressure Hull Design]
    P --> Q[Output: Submarine Collapse Depth and Volume Change Estimate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow is **top-down** (input stresses → equations → results → applications). The **diamond node H** is the decision branch where the student must recognize whether the loading is hydrostatic (uniform) or general triaxial (non-uniform) — a critical step in the KTU examination.

---

### 4.2 Physical Deformation Schematic — Elemental Cube

The following block-level architecture represents the **physical deformation of a small elemental cube** subjected to triaxial strain, the geometric basis of the volumetric strain derivation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Original Cube: Volume V equals dx times dy times dz] --> B0[Apply epsilon_x along X axis]
    A0 --> C0[Apply epsilon_y along Y axis]
    A0 --> D0[Apply epsilon_z along Z axis]
    B0 --> E0[New length in X: dx times 1 plus epsilon_x]
    C0 --> F0[New length in Y: dy times 1 plus epsilon_y]
    D0 --> G0[New length in Z: dz times 1 plus epsilon_z]
    E0 --> H0[Deformed Parallelepiped]
    F0 --> H0
    G0 --> H0
    H0 --> I0[New Volume: V prime equals V times 1 plus epsilon_x plus epsilon_y plus epsilon_z]
    I0 --> J0[Change in Volume: Delta V equals V times epsilon_v]
    J0 --> K0[Volumetric Strain: epsilon_v equals epsilon_x plus epsilon_y plus epsilon_z]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block diagram should be read in **left-to-right** order. The three parallel "deformation" branches (X, Y, Z) feed into a single convergence point (the deformed parallelepiped), mirroring the **superposition principle** underlying the small-strain derivation.
