[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **State and explain the differential relation between load intensity, shear force, and bending moment.** **Model Answer:** The three fundamental relations are: $$\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$ $$\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$$ $$\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w(x)$$ **Explanation:** - The **rate of change of shear force** with respect to $x$ equals the **negative of the load intensity**. - The **rate of change of bending moment** with respect to $x$ equals the **shear force**. - The **second derivative of bending moment** equals the **negative of the load intensity**. **Engineering Utility:** These relations allow us to (a) determine the shape of SFD and BMD directly from loading, (b) locate the point of maximum BM where $V = 0$, and (c) cross-check calculated SFD/BMD values. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign errors in reactions** — A single sign mistake in $R_A$ or $R_B$ propagates through ALL subsequent SF and BM values. **Always verify** $\sum F_y = 0$ after computing reactions. > 2. **Forgetting the UDL contribution to BM** — Many students write $M = R \cdot x$ but forget to subtract $w x^2 / 2$. The UDL must be included as a force × moment arm. > 3. **Mixing up segment boundaries** — When a point load exists at a section, write SF expressions for $x < x_P$ and $x > x_P$ separately. > 4. **Drawing SFD/BMD with wrong sign on the wrong side** — Positive SF is plotted **above the baseline** (per KTU convention). > 5. **Not verifying $M = 0$ at the free end of a cantilever** — always check boundary conditions. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]** A simply supported beam of span **6 m** is loaded with: - A **uniformly distributed load of 8 kN/m** over the **left half** (from $x = 0$ to $x = 3$ m). - A **point load of 16 kN** at the **center** ($x = 3$ m). - A **concentrated moment of 24 kN·m** (clockwise) at the **right end** ($x = 6$ m). **Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD). Also find the maximum bending moment and its location.** **Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Compute reactions and shear force values.** **Step 1 — Total Loads** UDL on left half = $8 \times 3 = 24$ kN, acting at centroid $x = 1.5$ m. Point load = 16 kN at $x = 3$ m. Applied moment = 24 kN·m (clockwise) at $x = 6$ m. **Step 2 — Reactions (use $\sum M_A = 0$ taking counter-clockwise positive)** $$R_B \cdot 6 - 24 \times 1.5 - 16 \times 3 + 24 = 0$$ Note: The applied clockwise moment is taken as $-24$ when summing moments counter-clockwise. $$6 R_B = 36 + 48 - 24 = 60$$ $$R_B = 10 \text{ kN}$$ From $\sum F_y = 0$: $$R_A = 24 + 16 - 10 = 30 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3 — Shear Force** **Segment 1** ($0 \le x \le 3$): $$V_1(x) = R_A - w \cdot x = 30 - 8x$$ At $x = 0$: $V_1 = 30$ kN. At $x = 3^{-}$: $V_1 = 30 - 24 = 6$ kN. At $x = 3$ m, drop by 16 kN (point load): $$V_2(3^{+}) = 6 - 16 = -10 \text{ kN}$$ **Segment 2** ($3 \le x \le 6$): No load: $$V_2(x) = -10 \text{ kN (constant)}$$ At $x = 6$: $V_2 = -10$ kN. (Does not equal $-R_B = -10$. ✓) **Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Bending moment values and maximum BM.** **Segment 1** ($0 \le x \le 3$): $$M_1(x) = R_A \cdot x - \dfrac{w x^{2}}{2} = 30x - 4x^{2}$$ At $x = 0$: $M_1 = 0$ ✓ At $x = 3$: $M_1 = 90 - 36 = 54$ kN·m. **Segment 2** ($3 \le x \le 6$): $$M_2(x) = R_A \cdot x - 24 \cdot 1.5 - 16(x - 3) - 4 \cdot 9 \quad \text{(UDL ends at 3, so constant load contribution)} $$ $$M_2(x) = 30x - 36 - 16(x-3) - 36$$ Wait — UDL is only in segment 1. The UDL on the left half has a constant moment contribution from its 24 kN total acting at its centroid (1.5 m from A), which from the right of any section $x > 3$ contributes a constant moment of $-24 \times (x - 1.5)$ when summed from the left. Let me redo this systematically: **Sum of moments to the LEFT of section $x$ (for $3 \le x \le 6$):** - $R_A \cdot x$ (counter-clockwise, positive) - $-24 \times (x - 1.5)$ (UDL resultant moment, downward force × distance) - $-16 \times (x - 3)$ (point load moment) $$M_2(x) = 30x - 24(x - 1.5) - 16(x - 3)$$ $$= 30x - 24x + 36 - 16x + 48$$ $$= (30 - 24 - 16)x + 84 = -10x + 84$$ At $x = 3$: $M_2 = -30 + 84 = 54$ kN·m ✓ (continuity) At $x = 6$: $M_2 = -60 + 84 = 24$ kN·m **Check: Applied moment at $x = 6$:** The clockwise 24 kN·m couple should make the BMD jump **downward** by 24 (since a clockwise couple on the right side creates a negative moment jump in the standard KTU convention). The "free end" of a simply supported beam should have $M = 0$ if there is no applied moment; with the applied clockwise moment, the BMD value at the right support is 24 kN·m. The "internal" moment just to the left of the support is 24 kN·m, and the support reaction $R_B$ then drops the shear to zero to the right. This is consistent. **Step 4 — Maximum Bending Moment** In Segment 1, set $V_1(x) = 0$: $$30 - 8x = 0 \Rightarrow x = 3.75 \text{ m}$$ But 3.75 > 3, so this is in Segment 2 where $V$ is constant at $-10$ kN (never zero). So maximum BM in Segment 1 occurs at $x = 3$ m (where $V$ is about to change sign, and the slope of $M$ is still positive). **Maximum BM = 54 kN·m at $x = 3$ m.** In Segment 2, $V$ is negative throughout, so $M$ decreases monotonically from 54 to 24 kN·m. **Final Answer for Question A:** $M_{\max} = 54$ kN·m at $x = 3$ m. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper]** A **cantilever beam of length 4 m** is fixed at the left end (A) and free at the right end (B). It carries: - A **uniformly distributed load of 6 kN/m** over the **rightmost 2 m** (from $x = 2$ m to $x = 4$ m). - A **point load of 10 kN** (downward) at the **free end** ($x = 4$ m). **Draw the SFD and BMD. Find the position and magnitude of the maximum bending moment.** **Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Reactions and SF values.** **Step 1 — Reactions at Fixed End A** For a cantilever, we don't strictly need reactions to draw SFD/BMD (we can work from the free end). But to be thorough: Vertical reaction at A: $R_A = 6 \times 2 + 10 = 12 + 10 = 22$ kN (upward). Fixed-end moment at A: $M_A = 6 \times 2 \times 3 + 10 \times 4 = 36 + 40 = 76$ kN·m. **Step 2 — Shear Force (working from the free end B is easier for cantilevers)** **Segment 2** ($2 \le x \le 4$, measured from A): Use forces to the RIGHT of section. Forces to the right of section at $x$: UDL $w = 6$ kN/m over $(4 - x)$ length, plus point load 10 kN at $x = 4$. $$V_2(x) = -[w(4 - x) + 10] = -[6(4 - x) + 10] = -(24 - 6x + 10) = 6x - 34$$ At $x = 4$ (free end): $V_2 = 24 - 34 = -10$ kN. ✓ At $x = 2^{+}$: $V_2 = 12 - 34 = -22$ kN. **Segment 1** ($0 \le x \le 2$): No load on this segment (UDL starts at $x = 2$). $$V_1(x) = -(0 + 10) = -10 \text{ kN (constant)}$$ At $x = 2^{-}$: $V_1 = -10$ kN. At $x = 2^{+}$: $V_2 = -22$ kN. (Drop of 12 kN due to UDL — consistent: $6 \times 2 = 12$. ✓) **Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: BM values and maximum BM.** **Segment 2** ($2 \le x \le 4$): Sum moments to the RIGHT of section. $$M_2(x) = -\left[w(4 - x) \cdot \dfrac{(4 - x)}{2} + 10 \cdot (4 - x)\right]$$ $$= -\left[3(4 - x)^{2} + 10(4 - x)\right]$$ Let $u = (4 - x)$: $$M_2 = -[3u^2 + 10u]$$ At $x = 4$ (free end): $u = 0$, $M_2 = 0$. ✓ At $x = 2$: $u = 2$, $M_2 = -[3 \times 4 + 20] = -32$ kN·m. **Segment 1** ($0 \le x \le 2$): Only the 10 kN point load is to the right of any section. $$M_1(x) = -10 \cdot (4 - x) = -40 + 10x$$ At $x = 2$: $M_1 = -40 + 20 = -20$ kN·m. Hmm, there's a discrepancy. Let me recheck. At $x = 2$, both $M_1$ and $M_2$ should give the same value (continuity). The issue: $M_1$ from the right only sees the 10 kN load. $M_2$ from the right sees the 10 kN load AND the UDL (12 kN total). The 12 kN UDL has its centroid at $x = 3$ m, which is $1$ m to the right of section $x = 2$. So the additional moment is $12 \times 1 = 12$ kN·m. So $M_2(2) = M_1(2) - 12 = -20 - 12 = -32$ kN·m. ✓ Continuity holds. **Step 3 — Maximum Bending Moment** The magnitude of BM is maximum at the **fixed end** $x = 0$ for cantilevers (BMD is most negative there since shear integrates negatively across the length). At $x = 0$: $$M_1(0) = -40 + 0 = -40 \text{ kN·m}$$ But wait — the fixed end also has the wall moment reaction! The **internal BM at the wall section** = $-M_A = -76$ kN·m (the reaction moment balances external moment). The discontinuity in the BMD at $x = 0$ is exactly the wall reaction moment. **Maximum internal BM magnitude = 76 kN·m at the fixed end A ($x = 0$).** This matches the computed wall moment, confirming the result. **Final Answer for Question B:** $|M|_{\max} = 76$ kN·m at $x = 0$ (fixed end). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Shear Force and Bending Moment** - [ ] **Shear Force** = algebraic sum of transverse forces on one side of a section. **Bending Moment** = algebraic sum of moments of forces on one side of a section. - [ ] **Sign Convention (KTU):** Positive SF = left-side forces act upward. Positive BM = beam sags (concave up). - [ ] **Differential relations:** $dV/dx = -w(x)$, $dM/dx = V$, $d^{2}M/dx^{2} = -w(x)$. - [ ] **Point Load on SFD:** Causes a vertical jump equal to the load magnitude (downward jump for downward load). - [ ] **Point Moment on BMD:** Causes a vertical jump equal to the moment magnitude (jump direction depends on sense of the couple). - [ ] **UDL on SFD:** Produces a straight inclined line with slope $-w$. **UDL on BMD:** Produces a parabola. - [ ] **UVL on SFD:** Produces a parabola. **UVL on BMD:** Produces a cubic curve. - [ ] **Maximum BM location:** Where $V = 0$ or $V$ changes sign. - [ ] **Boundary conditions:** SF and BM are zero at the free end of a cantilever. BM is zero at simple supports (pins and rollers). SF at a simple support equals the reaction. - [ ] **Standard results (must memorize):** • Simply supported beam with central point load: $M_{\max} = PL/4$ at center. • Simply supported beam with full-span UDL: $M_{\max} = wL^{2}/8$ at center. • Cantilever with end point load: $M_{\max} = PL$ at fixed end. • Cantilever with full-span UDL: $M_{\max} = wL^{2}/2$ at fixed end. - [ ] **Naval Architecture Note:** Hull girder bending moment governs midship section modulus; shear governs plate thickness in way of high shear zones (near supports in beam analogy = near ends of ship). - [ ] **Verification trick:** The area under the SFD between two points equals the change in BM between those two points — use this to cross-check your diagram.

topics.title: Beams and Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Beams and Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Beam? (KTU 2024 Definition)

A **beam** is a structural member, typically straight and slender, that is designed primarily to resist transverse (lateral) loads applied perpendicular to its longitudinal axis. In the context of Naval Architecture, beams are the fundamental load-carrying skeletons of every ship — from the smallest frames and stiffeners to the massive **hull girder** that resists the entire weight of the vessel and the hydrodynamic pressure of the sea.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A beam is a long, straight, prismatic structural element whose cross-sectional dimensions are small compared to its length, and which is subjected to loads acting transversely to its axis. The internal effects produced are **bending moment (BM)**, **shear force (SF)**, and in some cases, **torsional moment**.

## 1.2 What is a Load? (Engineering Perspective)

A **load** is any external force, moment, or pressure that tends to deform a structure or change its state of equilibrium. In solid mechanics, loads are mathematically idealized into a few standard forms so that they can be analyzed using the principles of statics and strength of materials.

> [!NOTE]
> **Naval Architecture Insight:** A ship at sea is simultaneously subjected to **gravity loads** (weight of hull, cargo, machinery) and **buoyancy loads** (hydrostatic pressure along the hull surface). Their interaction creates the classic **hogging** and **sagging** conditions in the hull girder.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Imagine holding a wooden ruler flat between your two palms — that is a **simply supported beam**.
- If you place a small weight in the middle, the ruler bends slightly. The top fibers compress, the bottom fibers stretch.
- If you fix one end into a wall (like a bookshelf bracket) and push down on the free end, you have created a **cantilever beam** — the same kind of beam found in a ship's poop deck or bow overhang.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Students:** Every beam in a ship is a magnified, steel-armoured version of the ruler you played with as a child. The mathematics is the same; only the scale changes — typically by a factor of **10³ to 10⁴** in length and a factor of **10⁶ to 10⁹** in stiffness.

## 1.4 Classification of Beams (Based on Support Conditions)

| Support Type | Reactions Provided | Physical Realization in a Ship |
|---|---|---|
| Simply Supported (Pinned + Roller) | Vertical reaction + Horizontal reaction | Decks resting on transverse bulkheads |
| Cantilever (Fixed-Free) | Vertical + Horizontal reaction + Fixing moment | Bow/stern overhangs, deck projections |
| Fixed-Fixed (Built-in both ends) | All 3 reactions at each end | Bracket connections, deep frames |
| Overhanging Beam | Combination of simple support + free end | Continuous decks with flared ends |
| Continuous Beam | Multiple supports, statically indeterminate | Longitudinal stiffeners crossing several frames |

## 1.5 Classification of Loads (Standard Engineering Categories)

1. **Point Load (Concentrated Load)**: A single force acting at a specific location. *Example:* A heavy machine bolted to a deck.
2. **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)**: Force spread uniformly over a length. *Example:* Self-weight of a deck plating, hydrostatic pressure on the hull.
3. **Uniformly Varying Load (UVL)**: Force varying linearly from zero to a maximum. *Example:* Triangular pressure distribution on a triangular bulkhead, or water pressure increasing linearly with depth.
4. **Moment Load (Couple)**: A pure moment applied at a point. *Example:* Torque from a propeller shaft on a thrust bearing seat.
5. **Dynamic / Impact Load**: Loads whose magnitude or direction changes rapidly. *Example:* Slamming forces on the bow in head seas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Insight:** Approximately **70% of Part B questions** in Module 2 require you to convert a real loading (e.g., a triangular water pressure) into a **mathematically equivalent point load** before solving. The conversion rule: a triangular pressure of intensity *w* over length *L* is equivalent to a concentrated force of **½·w·L** acting at the **centroid**, i.e., at **L/3 from the maximum intensity end**.

---

## 1.6 Equilibrium: The Governing Principle

For any beam (or any rigid body), the static equilibrium requires:

$$\sum F_x = 0, \quad \sum F_y = 0, \quad \sum M = 0$$

These three equations of statics form the **analytical foundation** of all beam problems you will solve in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Support Reactions — The First Step of Every Problem

Before we can plot the **Shear Force Diagram (SFD)** or **Bending Moment Diagram (BMD)**, we must compute the **support reactions** — the forces the supports exert on the beam to keep it in equilibrium.

### 2.1.1 The Three Standard Loading Cases

#### Case A: Simply Supported Beam with a Central Point Load

Consider a beam AB of length $L$ supported at A (pin) and B (roller), with a point load $P$ applied at the midspan.

- By symmetry: $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{P}{2}$
- Maximum Bending Moment occurs at midspan: $M_{max} = \dfrac{PL}{4}$

#### Case B: Simply Supported Beam with a UDL over the Entire Span

A UDL of intensity $w$ (units: **N/m** or **kN/m**) acts along the entire length $L$.

- Total load = $w \cdot L$
- By symmetry: $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2}$
- Maximum Bending Moment at midspan: $M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8}$

#### Case C: Simply Supported Beam with a Point Load at Distance 'a' from Left Support

- $R_A = \dfrac{P \cdot b}{L}$ where $b = L - a$
- $R_B = \dfrac{P \cdot a}{L}$
- Maximum Bending Moment occurs under the load: $M_{max} = \dfrac{P \cdot a \cdot b}{L}$

## 2.2 Sign Conventions (KTU 2024 Standard)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Note:** Adopting the wrong sign convention is the **single most common reason** students lose marks in KTU board exams. The two most widely accepted conventions are:
> 1. **Standard Civil/Mechanical Convention** (used in most KTU papers)
> 2. **Naval Architecture Convention** (used in ship structural analysis — see Section 2.4)

### Standard Convention for SFD and BMD

| Quantity | Positive Convention | Diagram Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Shear Force (SF) | Left side pushed up, right side pushed down | SFD plotted **above** the beam axis |
| Bending Moment (BM) | Causes the beam to **sag** (concave up, "smile") | BMD plotted on the **tension side** |

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Mnemonic:** "Sagging is Smiling" — A sagging beam looks like a smile, and a positive bending moment produces sagging.

## 2.3 The Bending Moment – Curvature Relationship

The fundamental differential relationship between distributed load, shear force, and bending moment is:

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$$

$$\frac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$$

These three relations are the **pillars of beam analysis**. Together they tell us:
- The **slope** of the SFD at any point equals the (negative) load intensity.
- The **slope** of the BMD at any point equals the shear force.
- The **curvature** of the BMD at any point equals the (negative) load intensity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In a ship, the bending moment at midship during a heavy storm can exceed the still-water bending moment by **40–60%**. Naval architects use the $dM/dx = V$ relation to size the deck and bottom plating thickness — the section modulus $Z$ must satisfy $\sigma = M/Z \le \sigma_{allowable}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Configuration | Reaction(s) | Max Shear Force $V_{max}$ | Max Bending Moment $M_{max}$ | Position of $M_{max}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SS Beam + Central Point Load $P$ | $R_A = R_B = P/2$ | $P/2$ | $PL/4$ | Midspan |
| 2 | SS Beam + UDL $w$ over $L$ | $R_A = R_B = wL/2$ | $wL/2$ | $wL^2/8$ | Midspan |
| 3 | SS Beam + Point Load $P$ at distance $a$ | $R_A = Pb/L$, $R_B = Pa/L$ | $\max(Pa/L, Pb/L)$ | $Pab/L$ | Under the load |
| 4 | Cantilever + Point Load $P$ at free end | $R_A = P$, $M_A = PL$ | $P$ | $PL$ | At fixed end |
| 5 | Cantilever + UDL $w$ over $L$ | $R_A = wL$, $M_A = wL^2/2$ | $wL$ | $wL^2/2$ | At fixed end |
| 6 | SS Beam + UVL (0 to $w$ over $L$) | $R_A = wL/6$, $R_B = wL/3$ | $wL/3$ | $wL^2/9\sqrt{3}$ | At $x = L/\sqrt{3}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** In naval architecture, bending moments in a hull girder are typically expressed in **kN·m** (small craft) or **MN·m** (large vessels). A VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier) in head seas may experience a still-water bending moment of order **10⁹ N·m**.

## 2.5 The Cantilever Beam — Special Attention for Naval Architects

A **cantilever beam** is fixed at one end and free at the other. In ship design, cantilever structures are found in:
- **Bow and stern overhangs**
- **Wing tanks** projecting from the hull
- **Sponsons** and **outrigger platforms**
- **Propeller shaft brackets** (A-frames)

The fixed end experiences the **maximum bending moment** and the **maximum shear force** — this is the critical section for structural design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** For a cantilever of length $L$ carrying a UDL $w$ over its entire span, the bending moment at the fixed end is $M_{fixed} = wL^2/2$ (not $wL^2/8$ as in a simply supported beam). This factor of **4×** is the reason cantilever overhangs must be carefully designed.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Naval Architecture

The beam-load analysis covered in this module directly feeds into:

1. **Hull Girder Strength Assessment:** A ship is idealized as a long box beam (the "hull girder") supported by a non-uniform buoyancy distribution and loaded by the weight distribution. The still-water bending moment (SWBM) and wave bending moment (WBM) are computed using the very SF/BM techniques we are studying.

2. **Local Strength of Plating and Stiffeners:** Each panel of hull plating, supported by frames and stiffeners, is analyzed as a plate on beam supports. The local bending moment determines plate thickness per **IACS Common Structural Rules (CSR)**.

3. **Transverse Frame Design:** Each transverse frame in a ship behaves like a beam loaded by the hydrostatic pressure of the sea on one side and the cargo/ballast pressure on the other. The frame is typically analyzed as a beam with a UVL (hydrostatic pressure varies linearly with depth).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reactions of a Simply Supported Beam with a Central Point Load

### Problem Setup

A beam AB of length $L$ is supported on a pin at A and a roller at B. A point load $P$ acts vertically downward at the midspan (i.e., at a distance $L/2$ from A).

**Goal:** Determine the vertical reactions $R_A$ and $R_B$.

### Free Body Diagram Description

- The beam is horizontal, lying along the x-axis.
- A pin support at A: provides vertical reaction $R_A$ (upward positive) and a horizontal reaction (zero, since no horizontal loads).
- A roller at B: provides only a vertical reaction $R_B$ (upward positive).
- A concentrated downward force $P$ acts at the midpoint C (i.e., at $x = L/2$).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Apply the vertical force equilibrium equation.

$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies R_A + R_B - P = 0$$

This gives us our first scalar relation:

$$R_A + R_B = P \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$

**Step 2:** Take moments about point A (counter-clockwise positive) to eliminate $R_A$ from the equation.

$$\sum M_A = 0 \implies R_B \cdot L - P \cdot \frac{L}{2} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$R_B \cdot L = P \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

$$R_B = \frac{P \cdot L/2}{L} = \frac{P}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $R_B$ back into Equation 1.

$$R_A = P - R_B = P - \frac{P}{2} = \frac{P}{2}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{R_A = \frac{P}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad R_B = \frac{P}{2}}$$

**Interpretation:** By symmetry of loading and geometry, the two reactions are equal. Each support carries exactly half the applied load.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bending Moment and Shear Force for a Simply Supported Beam with UDL

### Problem Setup

A simply supported beam of length $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of intensity $w$ (in **N/m** or **kN/m**) over its entire span. Compute the reactions, and derive the bending moment and shear force as functions of position $x$.

### Step 1: Total Load and Reactions

The UDL can be replaced by an equivalent concentrated force at the centroid of the load distribution. The total load is:

$$W_{total} = \int_0^L w \, dx = w \cdot L$$

This total force acts at the center of the beam, i.e., at $x = L/2$.

Taking moments about A:

$$\sum M_A = 0 \implies R_B \cdot L - (wL) \cdot \frac{L}{2} = 0$$

$$R_B = \frac{wL}{2}$$

By symmetry:

$$R_A = \frac{wL}{2}$$

### Step 2: Shear Force as a Function of $x$

Consider a section at distance $x$ from the left support A. To find the shear force at this section, isolate the left portion of the beam (from 0 to $x$).

**Free body of left portion:**
- Upward reaction: $R_A = wL/2$
- Downward distributed load over the segment 0 to $x$: equal to $w \cdot x$
- Internal shear force $V(x)$ acting downward on the right face of the cut (by convention)

Applying vertical equilibrium to the left portion:

$$R_A - w \cdot x - V(x) = 0$$

Solving:

$$V(x) = R_A - w \cdot x = \frac{wL}{2} - wx$$

### Step 3: Bending Moment as a Function of $x$

For the same left portion, take moments about the cut section (point at distance $x$):

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - (w \cdot x) \cdot \frac{x}{2}$$

$$M(x) = \frac{wL}{2} \cdot x - \frac{w x^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{M(x) = \frac{wx}{2}(L - x)}$$

### Step 4: Locate the Maximum Bending Moment

For $M(x)$ to be maximum, set $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = 0$:

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = \frac{wL}{2} - wx = 0$$

$$x = \frac{L}{2}$$

This confirms by symmetry that the maximum bending moment occurs at midspan. Substituting $x = L/2$ into the expression for $M(x)$:

$$M_{max} = \frac{w(L/2)}{2} \left(L - \frac{L}{2}\right) = \frac{wL}{4} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{wL^2}{8}$$

### Step 5: Maximum Shear Force

The shear force $V(x) = wL/2 - wx$ is linear in $x$. It is maximum at the supports (where $\vert V \vert$ is largest) and zero at midspan:

$$V_{max} = \frac{wL}{2} \quad \text{(at supports A and B)}$$

### Final Results

| Quantity | Expression | Value at Midspan | Maximum Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shear Force $V(x)$ | $\dfrac{wL}{2} - wx$ | $0$ | $\dfrac{wL}{2}$ (at supports) |
| Bending Moment $M(x)$ | $\dfrac{wx}{2}(L - x)$ | $\dfrac{wL^2}{8}$ | $\dfrac{wL^2}{8}$ (at midspan) |

### Step 6: Shape of the Diagrams

- **SFD:** A straight line sloping from $+wL/2$ at A down to $-wL/2$ at B, passing through zero at the midspan.
- **BMD:** A parabola, starting at zero at A, rising to a maximum of $wL^2/8$ at midspan, and falling back to zero at B.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Verification Trick:** The area under the SFD between any two points equals the change in bending moment between those points. For our UDL case, the SFD is a triangle from $+wL/2$ at A to $0$ at midspan. The area of this triangle is:
>
> $$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{wL^2}{8} = M_{max}$$
>
> This is a beautiful consistency check, and KTU examiners love to see students use it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Simply Supported Beam with a Point Load off-Center

### Problem Setup

A simply supported beam AB of length $L$ carries a concentrated load $P$ at a distance $a$ from A (and $b = L - a$ from B). Compute reactions, shear force, and bending moment.

### Step 1: Reactions

Taking moments about A:

$$\sum M_A = 0 \implies R_B \cdot L - P \cdot a = 0 \implies R_B = \frac{Pa}{L}$$

Vertical equilibrium:

$$R_A = P - R_B = P - \frac{Pa}{L} = \frac{P(L-a)}{L} = \frac{Pb}{L}$$

### Step 2: Shear Force in Two Segments

**Segment 1 ($0 \le x < a$):** Only $R_A$ acts on the left portion.

$$V_1(x) = R_A = \frac{Pb}{L}$$

**Segment 2 ($a < x \le L$):** Both $R_A$ and $P$ act on the left portion.

$$V_2(x) = R_A - P = \frac{Pb}{L} - P = \frac{Pb - PL}{L} = -\frac{Pa}{L}$$

**Observation:** The SFD has a sudden "jump" of magnitude $P$ (downward) at $x = a$, which is a classic indicator of a concentrated load.

### Step 3: Bending Moment in Two Segments

**Segment 1 ($0 \le x \le a$):**

$$M_1(x) = R_A \cdot x = \frac{Pb}{L} \cdot x$$

This is linear, zero at A, and equal to $\dfrac{Pab}{L}$ at $x = a$.

**Segment 2 ($a \le x \le L$):**

$$M_2(x) = R_A \cdot x - P \cdot (x - a) = \frac{Pb \cdot x}{L} - P(x - a)$$

Simplify:

$$M_2(x) = P \left[\frac{bx}{L} - x + a\right] = P\left[\frac{bx - Lx + aL}{L}\right] = P\left[\frac{aL - xL + bx}{L}\right]$$

Since $b = L - a$:

$$M_2(x) = P\left[\frac{aL - xL + (L-a)x}{L}\right] = P\left[\frac{aL - xL + Lx - ax}{L}\right] = P\left[\frac{a(L - x)}{L}\right] = \frac{Pa(L - x)}{L}$$

### Step 4: Maximum Bending Moment

At $x = a$ (i.e., under the point load), both segments give the same value:

$$M_{max} = M_1(a) = \frac{Pb \cdot a}{L} = \frac{Pab}{L}$$

### Final Results

$$\boxed{R_A = \frac{Pb}{L}, \quad R_B = \frac{Pa}{L}, \quad M_{max} = \frac{Pab}{L} \text{ at } x = a}$$

### Sanity Check: Central Load Special Case

If the load is at the center, $a = b = L/2$:

$$M_{max} = \frac{P \cdot (L/2) \cdot (L/2)}{L} = \frac{PL}{4} \checkmark$$

This matches our earlier result for a centrally loaded beam. **Consistency confirmed.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cantilever Beam with a Point Load at the Free End

### Problem Setup

A cantilever beam of length $L$ is fixed at A (left) and free at B (right). A point load $P$ acts downward at B.

### Step 1: Reactions

Vertical equilibrium:

$$R_A = P \quad \text{(upward)}$$

Moment equilibrium about A (to find the fixing moment):

$$M_A = P \cdot L \quad \text{(counter-clockwise to resist the load)}$$

### Step 2: Shear Force at Distance $x$ from Fixed End

At any section, the shear force equals the algebraic sum of forces on one side. Considering the right side (from $x$ to $L$):

$$V(x) = P \quad \text{(constant throughout the length)}$$

### Step 3: Bending Moment at Distance $x$

Considering the right side, the moment at the cut is the load times the lever arm:

$$M(x) = P \cdot (L - x)$$

This is linear:
- At $x = 0$ (fixed end): $M = PL$ (maximum)
- At $x = L$ (free end): $M = 0$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{V_{max} = P, \quad M_{max} = PL \text{ (at fixed end)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Naval Application:** A ship's bow overhang behaves exactly like this cantilever. The hydrodynamic pressure on the overhang creates a bending moment at the section where the overhang meets the parallel middle body — this is often the critical section for fatigue analysis in head-sea conditions.

---

## 3.5 Master Summary Table — Analytical Matrix

| Loading Configuration | $R_A$ | $R_B$ | $V_{max}$ | $M_{max}$ | Location of $M_{max}$ | SFD Shape | BMD Shape |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SS + Central $P$ | $P/2$ | $P/2$ | $P/2$ | $PL/4$ | Midspan | Rectangle + jump | Triangle |
| SS + Full UDL $w$ | $wL/2$ | $wL/2$ | $wL/2$ | $wL^2/8$ | Midspan | Sloping line (triangle) | Parabola |
| SS + Point $P$ at $a$ | $Pb/L$ | $Pa/L$ | $\max(Pa/L, Pb/L)$ | $Pab/L$ | Under $P$ | Two rectangles | Two triangles (peaks at $P$) |
| Cantilever + Free-end $P$ | $P$ | — | $P$ | $PL$ | Fixed end | Rectangle | Triangle |
| Cantilever + Full UDL $w$ | $wL$ | — | $wL$ | $wL^2/2$ | Fixed end | Sloping line (triangle) | Parabola |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Question)]**

**Q:** Define a cantilever beam. Give **two** examples of cantilever structures found in a ship.

**Model Answer:**

A **cantilever beam** is a structural member that is rigidly fixed (built-in) at one end and completely free at the other end. The fixed end provides three reaction components — a vertical force, a horizontal force, and a fixing (restraining) moment — while the free end has no supports.

**Two examples in a ship:**
1. **The bow or stern overhang** — the part of the hull projecting beyond the foremost or aftermost transverse bulkhead. It is fixed at the bulkhead and free at the tip of the bow.
2. **Sponsons or outrigger platforms** — projections from the side shell used to support lifeboats, anchors, or cargo handling gear. They are fixed at the side shell and free at their outer edge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Note:** Full 3 marks require the **definition** (1 mark), and **two distinct ship-specific examples with brief explanation** (1 mark each). A generic example like "a balcony" will get only partial credit.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Question)]**

**Q:** Differentiate between a **point load** and a **uniformly distributed load (UDL)**. Convert a triangular load of intensity varying from 0 at one end to $w_0$ over a length $L$ into an equivalent point load, and state where it acts.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Point Load | Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | A single concentrated force acting at a specific point | A load spread uniformly over a length, expressed as force per unit length |
| Units | **N** or **kN** | **N/m** or **kN/m** |
| Example in a ship | Weight of a heavy engine on a foundation | Self-weight of a deck plating or hydrostatic pressure on a flat bulkhead |

**Conversion of Triangular Load:**

A triangular (UVL) load with intensity increasing linearly from $0$ at one end to $w_0$ at the other over a length $L$ has:

$$\text{Equivalent Point Load } = \frac{1}{2} \cdot w_0 \cdot L$$

**Point of application:** This equivalent concentrated load acts at the **centroid** of the triangular load distribution, which is located at a distance of $\dfrac{L}{3}$ from the **maximum intensity** end (i.e., $\dfrac{2L}{3}$ from the zero-intensity end).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Fact:** Whenever a UDL or UVL appears in a KTU question, the very **first line** of your solution should show this conversion to a point load. Examiners explicitly award marks for this step.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors** (–2 marks): Mixing positive and negative SF/BM without consistency.
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert UVL to Equivalent Point Load** (–2 marks): Especially for hydrostatic pressure on bulkheads.
> 3. **Plotting SFD/BMD without tabulating values** (–1 mark): Always tabulate $V$ and $M$ at all critical sections (supports, load points, midspan) before plotting.
> 4. **Not labelling diagrams** (–1 to –2 marks): A free body diagram without clear labels is incomplete.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** (–1 mark): Mixing N, kN, N·m, kN·m without conversion.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**

A simply supported steel beam of span **6 m** carries a uniformly distributed load of **20 kN/m** over its entire span. Additionally, a concentrated load of **30 kN** acts at a distance of **2 m from the left support**.

**(a)** Determine the support reactions at both ends. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD), clearly indicating the maximum values. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Support Reactions

**Step 1:** Convert the UDL to an equivalent point load.

$$W_{UDL} = w \cdot L = 20 \times 6 = 120 \text{ kN}$$

This acts at the centroid of the UDL, i.e., at the midspan, $x = 3$ m from A.

**Step 2:** Apply vertical equilibrium.

$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies R_A + R_B = 120 + 30 = 150 \text{ kN} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 3:** Take moments about A (counter-clockwise positive).

$$\sum M_A = 0 \implies R_B \cdot 6 - 120 \cdot 3 - 30 \cdot 2 = 0$$

$$R_B \cdot 6 = 360 + 60 = 420$$

$$R_B = \frac{420}{6} = 70 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4:** From Equation (1):

$$R_A = 150 - 70 = 80 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{R_A = 80 \text{ kN}, \quad R_B = 70 \text{ kN}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b): SFD and BMD

**Step 1:** Calculate SF at key sections.

- At A ($x = 0^+$): $V = +R_A = +80$ kN
- Just before B ($x = 6^-$): $V = R_A - 30 - 20 \times 6 = 80 - 30 - 120 = -70$ kN
- At $x = 2$ m (just before the point load): $V = 80 - 20 \times 2 = 80 - 40 = +40$ kN
- At $x = 2$ m (just after the point load): $V = 40 - 30 = +10$ kN
- At midspan $x = 3$ m: $V = 80 - 40 - 30 = +10$ kN
- At $x = 4$ m: $V = 80 - 80 - 30 = -30$ kN
- At $x = 5$ m: $V = 80 - 100 - 30 = -50$ kN
- At B ($x = 6$ m): $V = 80 - 120 - 30 = -70$ kN ✓ (matches $R_B$)

**Step 2:** Shear force changes sign between $x = 3$ m and $x = 4$ m. Find the exact zero-shear location.

For $x$ in the range $2 < x < 6$:

$$V(x) = 80 - 30 - 20x = 50 - 20x$$

Setting $V(x) = 0$:

$$50 - 20x = 0 \implies x = 2.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate BM at key sections.

- At A ($x = 0$): $M = 0$
- At $x = 2$ m (under point load): $M = 80 \times 2 - 20 \times 2 \times 1 = 160 - 40 = 120$ kN·m
- At $x = 2.5$ m (zero shear = max BM): $M = 80 \times 2.5 - 30 \times 0.5 - 20 \times 2.5 \times 1.25 = 200 - 15 - 62.5 = 122.5$ kN·m
- At midspan $x = 3$ m: $M = 80 \times 3 - 30 \times 1 - 20 \times 3 \times 1.5 = 240 - 30 - 90 = 120$ kN·m
- At B ($x = 6$ m): $M = 0$ ✓

$$\boxed{V_{max} = 80 \text{ kN (at A)}, \quad M_{max} = 122.5 \text{ kN·m at } x = 2.5 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4:** SFD and BMD Diagrams (described for plotting):

- **SFD:** Starts at $+80$ kN at A, drops linearly to $+40$ kN at $x = 2^-$ (slope $= -w = -20$), jumps down by $30$ kN to $+10$ kN at $x = 2^+$, continues with the same slope to $-70$ kN at B, then jumps up by $70$ kN back to zero.
- **BMD:** Starts at zero, rises as a parabola to $120$ kN·m at $x = 2$, continues with a kink (slope changes) to peak at $122.5$ kN·m at $x = 2.5$ m, then descends as a parabola back to zero at B.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students miss that the **maximum BM does NOT occur under the point load** when there is also a UDL present. The maximum occurs where the SF is zero, which we found at $x = 2.5$ m, not at $x = 2$ m.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**

A horizontal cantilever beam of length **4 m** is fixed at the left end. It carries a **uniformly distributed load of 15 kN/m** over the entire span, plus a **concentrated load of 25 kN** at the free end.

**(a)** Determine the vertical reaction and fixing moment at the fixed end. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD), and determine the maximum bending moment. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Reactions

**Step 1:** Convert UDL to equivalent point load.

$$W_{UDL} = 15 \times 4 = 60 \text{ kN}$$

This acts at the centroid, i.e., at $x = 2$ m from the fixed end.

**Step 2:** Apply vertical equilibrium.

$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies R_A = 60 + 25 = 85 \text{ kN (upward)}$$

**Step 3:** Take moments about the fixed end A.

$$\sum M_A = 0 \implies M_A = 60 \times 2 + 25 \times 4 = 120 + 100 = 220 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$\boxed{R_A = 85 \text{ kN (upward)}, \quad M_A = 220 \text{ kN·m (counter-clockwise)}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b): SFD and BMD

**Step 1:** Shear force at distance $x$ from the fixed end.

Considering the right portion (from $x$ to the free end at 4 m):

$$V(x) = 25 + 15(4 - x) = 25 + 60 - 15x = 85 - 15x$$

This is linear:
- At $x = 0$ (fixed end): $V = 85$ kN
- At $x = 4$ (free end): $V = 25$ kN

**Step 2:** Bending moment at distance $x$.

Considering the right portion:

$$M(x) = 25(4 - x) + 15(4 - x) \cdot \frac{(4 - x)}{2} = 25(4-x) + \frac{15(4-x)^2}{2}$$

Let $u = 4 - x$ (distance from the free end). Then:

$$M = 25u + \frac{15u^2}{2} = 25u + 7.5u^2$$

Or, expressed in terms of $x$:

$$M(x) = 25(4 - x) + 7.5(4 - x)^2$$

**Step 3:** Maximum bending moment.

Since both the load and shear are positive everywhere along the beam (and $M$ decreases as $x$ increases from the fixed end), the maximum occurs at the **fixed end**:

$$M_{max} = M(0) = 25 \times 4 + 7.5 \times 16 = 100 + 120 = 220 \text{ kN·m} \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{V_{max} = 85 \text{ kN at the fixed end}, \quad M_{max} = 220 \text{ kN·m at the fixed end}}$$

**Step 4:** Diagrams (described):

- **SFD:** A straight line sloping from $+85$ kN at the fixed end to $+25$ kN at the free end.
- **BMD:** A parabolic curve, starting at $M = 220$ kN·m at the fixed end and decreasing to $M = 0$ at the free end. The curve is concave (curving downward, like an inverted bowl) because the UDL creates negative second derivative.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference from Part A:** In a cantilever, $M_{max}$ is always at the **fixed end** (not the free end), regardless of where the loads are applied. The BMD has zero slope where there is a point load and parabolic shape where there is UDL.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Beams and Loads**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- A **beam** resists transverse loads and is characterized by its **bending moment (M)** and **shear force (V)** internal responses.
- A **point load** is concentrated; a **UDL** is $w$ N/m; a **UVL** varies linearly (triangular).

### 📐 Support Reactions — Key Formulas
- **Simply supported + central point $P$:** $R_A = R_B = P/2$
- **Simply supported + UDL $w$ over $L$:** $R_A = R_B = wL/2$
- **Simply supported + point $P$ at distance $a$:** $R_A = Pb/L$, $R_B = Pa/L$
- **Cantilever + point $P$ at free end:** $R_A = P$, $M_A = PL$
- **Cantilever + UDL $w$ over $L$:** $R_A = wL$, $M_A = wL^2/2$

### 📊 Maximum Bending Moments — Must Memorize
- **SS + Central $P$:** $M_{max} = PL/4$ (at midspan)
- **SS + UDL $w$:** $M_{max} = wL^2/8$ (at midspan)
- **SS + Point $P$ off-center:** $M_{max} = Pab/L$ (under the load)
- **Cantilever + Free-end $P$:** $M_{max} = PL$ (at fixed end)
- **Cantilever + Full UDL $w$:** $M_{max} = wL^2/2$ (at fixed end)

### 🔁 Three Differential Relationships (Memorize)
- $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$ — slope of SFD equals (negative) load
- $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$ — slope of BMD equals SF
- $\dfrac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$ — curvature of BMD equals (negative) load

### ⚖️ Sign Conventions
- **Positive SF:** Left portion pushed up, right portion pushed down.
- **Positive BM (Sagging):** Beam curves into a "smile" — top fibers in compression, bottom in tension.
- **Negative BM (Hogging):** Beam curves into a "frown" — top fibers in tension, bottom in compression.

### 🛳️ Naval Architecture Quick Connect
- A **ship hull** is a **box girder** (beam) with the **bottom plating as one flange**, **deck plating as the other flange**, and **side shell as the web**.
- **Hogging** (bow and stern supported, middle in trough) and **Sagging** (middle supported, bow/stern in troughs) are the two extreme bending conditions the hull girder must survive.
- **Transverse frames** are simply supported or fixed beams loaded by **hydrostatic pressure** (a UVL).
- **Overhangs** (bow, stern, sponsons) are **cantilever beams**.

### ✅ Exam Strategy Tips
1. **Always draw the Free Body Diagram (FBD)** before writing equations.
2. **Convert all UDL/UVL to equivalent point loads** at the very start.
3. **Tabulate $V$ and $M$** at every critical section (supports, load points, midspan, free end, points of zero shear) before plotting.
4. **Label every diagram** clearly with values, units, and sign.
5. **Use the area-under-SFD trick** to verify $M$ calculations: $\Delta M = \int V \, dx$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Note for Students:** Mastering "Beams and Loads" is the **gateway skill** for the entire field of Naval Structural Analysis. Every higher-level topic — plate bending, frame analysis, hull girder strength, fatigue, and buckling — builds directly on the SF/BM techniques you have learned in this module. Invest the time now, and the rest of the course will feel intuitive.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sign Conventions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sign Conventions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention** is a standardized, universally accepted rule that assigns a **positive (+)** or **negative (−)** algebraic value to internal forces, stresses, strains, moments, and deformations acting on a material element or structural member. It removes ambiguity in mathematical modeling, equilibrium equations, and the plotting of Shear Force Diagrams (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagrams (BMD).

In **Solid Mechanics for Naval Architects (PCNST303)**, sign conventions are particularly critical because the ship hull behaves as a **box girder** subjected to **wave-induced bending**, **shear**, and **torsion** simultaneously. Every classification society rule (IACS, Lloyd's, DNV, IRS) and every finite-element solver (ANSYS, NASTRAN, SESAM) relies on a strict sign convention to maintain consistency between loading, internal forces, and resultant stresses.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bank Account of Forces"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of sign conventions as a **bank account ledger**.
> - Money coming **IN** (credit / deposit) → marked **+ve**
> - Money going **OUT** (debit / withdrawal) → marked **−ve**
>
> Just as a bank cannot function without deciding whether deposits are positive or negative, a naval architect **cannot analyze a ship hull** without first fixing a sign convention. Two engineers analyzing the same midship section but using opposite conventions would arrive at **opposite stress signs** — and one of them would incorrectly predict **tension where there should be compression** (or vice versa). This could lead to fatal structural mis-design.

## 1.3 Categories of Sign Conventions in PCNST303 Module 2

| Sl. No. | Quantity | Governing Quantity | Standard Sign (KTU / Naval Arch.) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal Stress (σ) | Axial Force | **Tension = +ve**, Compression = −ve |
| 2 | Normal Strain (ε) | Length Change | **Elongation = +ve**, Contraction = −ve |
| 3 | Shear Stress (τ) | Internal Shear | **CW on +ve face = +ve** (complementary pair) |
| 4 | Bending Moment (M) | Curvature | **Hogging = +ve** (ship hull convention) |
| 5 | Shear Force (V) | Transverse Load | Depends on beam convention chosen |
| 6 | Torsion (T) | Right-Hand Rule | **Thumb out along +ve axis** |

---

## 1.4 The Three Master Conventions in Naval Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Structural Mechanics Convention (Tension-Positive)**
> Used universally for **stress and strain** in ship scantlings. If a plate is being pulled apart, σ > 0.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **2. Beam Convention (Standard Civil/Mechanical)**
> Sagging moment is positive, with shear positive when it tends to rotate the element **clockwise**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3. Ship Hull Girder Convention (Hogging-Positive)**
> Because the **wave crest amidships** causes hogging and the **wave trough amidships** causes sagging, naval architects often define **hogging moment as positive** for clarity with seakeeping load cases.

The choice of convention does **not** change the physics — only the algebraic bookkeeping. However, **mixing conventions within one equation** is a catastrophic error and is the **#1 reason students lose marks** in KTU valuation.

---

## 1.5 Why Sign Conventions Matter — The Naval Architect's Perspective

- **Safety**: A sign error in plating stress can under-predict scantlings by tens of millimeters.
- **Regulation Compliance**: IACS S21/S22 requires hogging/sagging bending moments in defined sign frames.
- **FEA Post-Processing**: ANSYS and ABAQUS use element-formulated sign conventions; converting them to ship-frame convention requires careful mapping.
- **Class Drawings**: All midship section diagrams (e.g., Rule scantlings) are drawn with tension on one face and compression on the other — a sign convention is implicit in every line.

---

## 1.6 Unit Standards (Mandatory Bold-Notation)

- Stress: **N/mm²** or **MPa** (1 MPa = 1 N/mm²)
- Strain: **dimensionless** (m/m or mm/mm)
- Moment: **N·m** or **kN·m**
- Force: **N** or **kN**
- Curvature: **1/m** or **rad/m**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Logic — The "Why" Behind Sign Conventions

A sign convention must satisfy **three mathematical requirements**:

1. **Equilibrium Compatibility** — Internal forces at a cut must equal the external load vector with proper sign.
2. **Stress–Strain Consistency** — The sign of strain must follow the sign of stress (via Hooke's Law).
3. **Diagram Continuity** — The slope of the BMD must equal the shear force ($dM/dx = V$) and the slope of the SFD must equal the load ($dV/dx = -w$) — signs must be consistent throughout.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Sign Convention Rules

### 2.2.1 Normal Stress (σ) — Tension-Positive Convention

**Rule:**
- Stress acting **outward** (pulling) on a face whose outward normal points in the **+x direction** is **positive**.
- Stress acting **inward** (pushing) on the same face is **negative**.

**For a 2D element** with faces having outward normals in +x, −x, +y, −y directions:

$$
\sigma_{xx}(+x\text{-face}) = +\sigma \quad \text{(tension)}
$$
$$
\sigma_{xx}(-x\text{-face}) = -\sigma \quad \text{(action–reaction)}
$$

**Why "Tension-Positive"?** Because steel, aluminium, and shipbuilding alloys fail predominantly in tension (brittle fracture risk) and the uniaxial tensile test naturally yields positive σ in the loading direction. Geotechnical engineering uses the **opposite** (compression-positive) because soils fail in compression.

---

### 2.2.2 Shear Stress (τ) — Complementary Pair Rule

**Rule:**
- A shear stress on the **+x face** acting in the **+y direction** is **positive**.
- A shear stress on the **+y face** acting in the **+x direction** is **positive**.
- The pair is **equal in magnitude and opposite in rotational sense** on the opposite faces (Newton's Third Law).

This produces the well-known element diagram with **two pairs of arrows** forming a couple. The shear forces on perpendicular faces are always **equal and opposite** to maintain moment equilibrium of the infinitesimal element.

---

### 2.2.3 Shear Force (V) — Beam Convention

**Standard Beam Convention (used in module problems):**
- Positive shear: tends to rotate the element **clockwise**.
- On a horizontal beam, V > 0 when the **left portion exerts an upward force on the right portion** at the cut.

**Alternative (used in some textbooks):**
- Positive shear: tends to rotate the element **counter-clockwise**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Always check which beam convention your question paper uses. The downward load sign in $dV/dx = -w$ depends on this choice. Getting it backwards will flip your **entire SFD**.

---

### 2.2.4 Bending Moment (M) — Sagging vs. Hogging

**Standard Civil Convention:**
- **Sagging moment (smile shape)** = **positive**.
- A sagging beam is **concave up**, fibers at the bottom are in **tension**, fibers at the top are in **compression**.
- Mathematically: $M > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; d^2y/dx^2 > 0$ (concave up).

**Naval Architecture (Hull Girder) Convention:**
- **Hogging moment** = the hull bends with the ends hanging below the middle (like a sagging camel back).
- Under a wave crest amidships: ship experiences **Hogging**.
- Under a wave trough amidships: ship experiences **Sagging**.
- Some classification society rules take **sagging as positive** for steel yield check; check the rule book.

---

### 2.2.5 Torsion (T) — Right-Hand Rule

**Rule:**
- Point the **thumb of the right hand** along the shaft axis; the direction of **curl of the fingers** is the positive direction of twist.
- For a ship hull, torsion arises from **oblique waves** causing asymmetric wave pressure on port and starboard sides.

---

## 2.3 The Three Coordinate Frames Used in Ship Structural Analysis

| Frame | x-Axis | y-Axis | z-Axis | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Global** | Aft → Forward | Port → Starboard | Down → Up | Whole-ship FEM, still-water & wave loads |
| **Local (Element)** | Element edge 1 | Element edge 2 | Normal to surface | Plate & stiffener stress extraction |
| **Section (Hull)** | Longitudinal (AP→FP) | Vertical (Keel→Deck) | Transverse (PS→SB) | Midship section properties |

> [!NOTE]
> In all three frames, **tension-positive normal stress** holds. Only the orientation changes.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Positive Sign Means | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Axial Stress | $\sigma_x$ | Tension (pulling) | $\sigma_x = N/A$ |
| 2 | Bending Stress | $\sigma_b$ | Tension on +ve y-face | $\sigma_b = M y / I$ |
| 3 | Shear Stress | $\tau_{xy}$ | CCW couple tendency | $\tau_{xy} = V Q / (I b)$ |
| 4 | Torsional Stress | $\tau_{t}$ | Right-hand-rule twist | $\tau_t = T r / J$ |
| 5 | Normal Strain | $\varepsilon_x$ | Elongation | $\varepsilon_x = \sigma_x / E$ |
| 6 | Shear Strain | $\gamma_{xy}$ | Angle decrease | $\gamma_{xy} = \tau_{xy} / G$ |
| 7 | Curvature | $\kappa$ | Concave-up (sagging) | $\kappa = M / (EI)$ |
| 8 | Slope of BMD | $dM/dx$ | Equals shear force | $dM/dx = V$ |
| 9 | Slope of SFD | $dV/dx$ | Equals negative load | $dV/dx = -w(x)$ |
| 10 | Total Bending Stress | $\sigma_{xx}$ | Algebraic sum | $\sigma_{xx} = N/A \pm M y/I$ |

**Where:**
- $N$ = axial force (N), $A$ = cross-section area (mm²), $M$ = bending moment (N·mm), $y$ = distance from NA (mm), $I$ = second moment of area (mm⁴), $V$ = shear force (N), $Q$ = first moment of area (mm³), $b$ = width (mm), $T$ = torque (N·mm), $r$ = radial distance (mm), $J$ = polar moment (mm⁴), $E$ = Young's modulus (MPa), $G$ = shear modulus (MPa), $w$ = distributed load (N/mm).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Naval Architecture

1. **Hull Scantling Determination**: $M_{\max}$ (hogging or sagging) sets the required section modulus $Z = M_{\max}/\sigma_{\text{allow}}$, directly from the chosen sign convention.
2. **Shear Flow Analysis**: $q = V Q / I$ gives shear flow direction around the cross-section — sign convention tells us which web/plate is in **compression diagonal**.
3. **Plate Bending**: A plate panel sign convention (Roark's formulas) is needed for local panel analysis under lateral pressure.
4. **FEA Post-Processing**: Mapping element forces (in element local axes) to global hull axes requires explicit sign tracking.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Tension is Positive for Normal Stress

We start with the **uniaxial tensile test**. Consider a prismatic bar of original length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$ subjected to an axial tensile force $P$ at the ends.

**Step 1 — Define internal axial force at any section:**
By making a fictitious cut at distance $x$ from the left end, equilibrium of the left segment requires an internal force $N(x)$ acting at the cut.

For a uniformly loaded bar with end force $P$:

$$
\sum F_x = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; N(x) - P = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; N(x) = P
$$

**Step 2 — Compute normal stress:**
By definition, normal stress is the internal axial force per unit cross-sectional area:

$$
\sigma_x \;=\; \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta N}{\Delta A} \;=\; \frac{N(x)}{A}
$$

Substituting $N(x) = P$:

$$
\sigma_x \;=\; \frac{P}{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Assign the sign:**
If $P$ is **tensile** (pulling the bar apart, $\rightarrow\!\leftarrow\!\rightarrow$ at the cut), we assign $\sigma_x = +P/A > 0$.
If $P$ is **compressive** (pushing the bar together, $\leftarrow\!\rightarrow\!\leftarrow$ at the cut), we assign $\sigma_x = -P/A < 0$.

**Step 4 — Result:**
$$
\boxed{\sigma_x \;=\; \frac{N}{A} \quad \text{with} \quad \text{Tension } = +ve,\; \text{Compression} = -ve}
$$

**Engineering reasoning:** Choosing tension as positive aligns with the **physical reality of failure** — metals crack from tension (Mode I fracture), and the tensile test is the standardized laboratory measurement. Engineers instinctively want to know "is this fibre pulling apart?" — and positive σ answers that question directly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Relation $dM/dx = V$ and $dV/dx = -w(x)$

This is the **master relationship** between load, shear, and moment — and it is the source of 90% of sign convention errors in KTU papers. We derive it carefully.

**Step 1 — Consider an infinitesimal element of length $dx$.**
Let $V(x)$ be the shear force at the left face and $V(x) + dV$ at the right face.
Let $M(x)$ be the moment at the left face and $M(x) + dM$ at the right face.
Let $w(x)$ be the distributed load acting downward (per unit length, positive downward).

**Step 2 — Vertical force equilibrium:**
Sum of vertical forces = 0 (taking upward as +ve):

$$
V(x) - \bigl[V(x) + dV\bigr] - w(x)\, dx \;=\; 0
$$

Simplifying:

$$
-dV \;-\; w(x)\, dx \;=\; 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\frac{dV}{dx} \;=\; -\, w(x)}
$$

This is **Equation (1)**. The negative sign is the heart of the sign convention: if $w$ is positive (downward load), then $V$ **decreases** as $x$ increases.

**Step 3 — Moment equilibrium about the right face:**
Taking counter-clockwise as positive moment:

$$
-M(x) \;-\; \bigl[M(x) + dM\bigr] \;+\; V(x)\, dx \;-\; w(x)\, dx \cdot \frac{dx}{2} \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 4 — Neglect the higher-order term** $w(x)(dx)^2/2$:

$$
-2M(x) - dM + V(x)\, dx \;\approx\; 0
$$

Wait — let me re-do this carefully. The correct moment equilibrium about the **right face** of the element gives:

$$
M(x) + dM - M(x) - V(x)\, dx + w(x)\, dx \cdot \frac{dx}{2} \;=\; 0
$$

Neglecting the second-order term:

$$
dM - V(x)\, dx \;=\; 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\frac{dM}{dx} \;=\; V(x)}
$$

This is **Equation (2)**. The shear force equals the **slope** of the bending moment diagram.

**Step 5 — Combine (1) and (2):**
Differentiating (2) once more and substituting (1):

$$
\frac{d^2M}{dx^2} \;=\; \frac{dV}{dx} \;=\; -\, w(x)
$$

This triple relationship governs every beam and hull-girder analysis in naval architecture.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bending Stress $\sigma_b = M y / I$ — Sign Tracking

**Step 1 — Geometric assumption (Euler–Bernoulli beam):**
Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending. Strain varies linearly with distance $y$ from the neutral axis:

$$
\varepsilon_x(y) \;=\; -\,\frac{y}{\rho}
$$

where $\rho$ is the radius of curvature. The negative sign accounts for the fact that **fibres above the NA (y > 0)** shorten when the beam bends with sagging curvature (concave up).

**Step 2 — Apply Hooke's Law:**
$$
\sigma_x(y) \;=\; E\,\varepsilon_x(y) \;=\; -\,\frac{E\,y}{\rho}
$$

**Step 3 — Define curvature** $\kappa = 1/\rho$. The moment–curvature relationship gives:

$$
M \;=\; \int_A \sigma_x(y) \cdot y\, dA \;=\; -\,\frac{E}{\rho} \int_A y^2\, dA \;=\; -\,\frac{E\, I}{\rho}
$$

Solving for $1/\rho$:

$$
\frac{1}{\rho} \;=\; -\,\frac{M}{E I}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into stress:**
$$
\sigma_x(y) \;=\; -\,\frac{E\, y}{\rho} \;=\; -\,\frac{E\, y}{(-M/EI)} \;=\; \frac{M\, y}{I}
$$

**Final Result:**
$$
\boxed{\sigma_b \;=\; \frac{M\, y}{I}}
$$

**Sign Interpretation Table for a Sagging Beam (M > 0, Standard Convention):**

| Position y | Sign of y | Sign of M | Sign of σ | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Top fibre (y = +h/2) | + | + | + (Tension) | Top in tension |
| Neutral axis (y = 0) | 0 | + | 0 | No bending stress |
| Bottom fibre (y = −h/2) | − | + | − (Compression) | Bottom in compression |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget that **bending stress is linear through the depth**, and that the **TOP and BOTTOM fibres have OPPOSITE signs**. A midship section under hogging inverts this entire pattern.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Combined Loading — Axial + Bending

For a hull-girder element subjected simultaneously to axial force $N$ (still-water or wave-induced), bending moment $M$ (hogging/sagging), and shear force $V$:

$$
\sigma_{xx}(y) \;=\; \underbrace{\frac{N}{A}}_{\text{axial (uniform)}} \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{M\, y}{I}}_{\text{bending (linear in }y)}
$$

For the hull-girder with deck at $y = +H/2$ and keel at $y = -H/2$:

$$
\sigma_{\text{deck}} \;=\; \frac{N}{A} \;+\; \frac{M\,(H/2)}{I} \quad \text{(for sagging, both add)}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{keel}} \;=\; \frac{N}{A} \;-\; \frac{M\,(H/2)}{I} \quad \text{(for sagging, subtract)}
$$

The **critical stress** (maximum magnitude) determines scantlings.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stress Sign Convention Element (Tension-Positive)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the standard 2D stress element with **tension-positive** convention, including the four shear components forming the complementary pair.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    NodeA["Element Top Face +y"] -->|Tau_xy acts in +x direction| NodeB["Element Right Face +x"]
    NodeA -->|Sigma_y tension outward +y| NodeC["Stress Element Centre"]
    NodeB -->|Sigma_x tension outward +x| NodeC
    NodeC -->|Sigma_x compression inward -x| NodeD["Element Left Face -x"]
    NodeC -->|Sigma_y compression inward -y| NodeE["Element Bottom Face -y"]
    NodeC -->|Tau_xy acts in -x on bottom face| NodeE
    NodeD -->|Tau_xy acts in -y on left face| NodeA
    NodeE -->|Tau_xy acts in +y on right face| NodeB
    NodeF["Tension Positive Rule"] -.->|Governs| NodeC
    NodeG["Complementary Shear Rule"] -.->|Governs| NodeA
    NodeG -.->|Governs| NodeB
    NodeG -.->|Governs| NodeD
    NodeG -.->|Governs| NodeE
```

**Visual Reading:**
- Each face has an **outward normal**.
- A normal stress pointing **outward** (away from the element) is **tension (+ve)**.
- A normal stress pointing **inward** (into the element) is **compression (−ve)**.
- Shear stresses on perpendicular faces are **equal in magnitude, opposite in rotational sense** — together they form a couple that maintains element moment equilibrium.

---

## 4.2 Beam Sign Convention Flow (Sagging vs. Hogging)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **decision logic** for assigning positive and negative bending moments in ship hull analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NodeStart["Beam or Hull Girder Element"] --> NodeQ1{"Is the curvature concave UP?"}
    NodeQ1 -->|Yes| NodeSag["Sagging Moment M positive"]
    NodeQ1 -->|No| NodeQ2{"Is the curvature concave DOWN?"}
    NodeQ2 -->|Yes| NodeHog["Hogging Moment M positive in Ship Convention"]
    NodeQ2 -->|No| NodeZero["Zero Moment No Curvature"]
    NodeSag --> NodeT1["Bottom Fibre: TENSION positive"]
    NodeSag --> NodeC1["Top Fibre: COMPRESSION negative"]
    NodeHog --> NodeT2["Top Fibre: TENSION positive"]
    NodeHog --> NodeC2["Bottom Fibre: COMPRESSION negative"]
    NodeZero --> NodeFlat["No Bending Stress Only Axial"]
    NodeT1 --> NodeFinal["Sigma = plus M y over I"]
    NodeC1 --> NodeFinal
    NodeT2 --> NodeFinal
    NodeC2 --> NodeFinal
```

---

## 4.3 Shear Force Sign Convention Decision Tree

The following Mermaid block diagram provides a decision tree for assigning positive shear force on a beam element.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NodeA["Draw FBD of Left Segment"] --> NodeB{"Does the internal force tend to rotate the cut face CLOCKWISE?"}
    NodeB -->|Yes| NodeC["Shear Force V positive"]
    NodeB -->|No| NodeD["Shear Force V negative"]
    NodeC --> NodeE["On the right face of left segment: V points DOWN"]
    NodeC --> NodeF["On the left face of right segment: V points UP"]
    NodeD --> NodeG["Opposite orientation to above"]
    NodeF --> NodeH["Use relation dV by dx equals minus w of x"]
    NodeE --> NodeH
    NodeH --> NodeI["Plot SFD with sign from left to right"]
```

---

## 4.4 Torsion Sign Convention (Right-Hand Rule)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    NodeShaft["Shaft or Hull Cross Section"] --> NodeThumb["Point RIGHT THUMB along +x shaft axis"]
    NodeThumb --> NodeFingers["Curl of FINGERS shows positive twist direction"]
    NodeFingers --> NodeTau1["Shear stress Tau positive in direction of finger curl"]
    NodeTau1 --> NodeTorque["Applied Torque T positive if it matches the right hand rule"]
    NodeTorque --> NodeAnti["Opposite torque direction gives negative T"]
    NodeAnti --> NodeUse["For ship hull Torsion from oblique waves Check sign by right hand rule"]
```

---

## 4.5 Hull Girder Loading State Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NodeLoad["Wave Profile on Hull"] --> NodeQ{"Where is wave crest relative to midship?"}
    NodeQ -->|Amidships crest| NodeHog["Hogging State Hull ends droop"]
    NodeQ -->|Amidships trough| NodeSag["Sagging State Hull ends rise"]
    NodeHog --> NodeMStress1["Deck in TENSION Keel in COMPRESSION"]
    NodeSag --> NodeMStress2["Deck in COMPRESSION Keel in TENSION"]
    NodeMStress1 --> NodeDesign["Use absolute M_max for scantling"]
    NodeMStress2 --> NodeDesign
```

---

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Sign Convention Integration Across Modules

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Mod1["Module 1 Stress and Strain Fundamentals"] --> Mod2["Module 2 Sign Conventions Internal Forces"]
    Mod3["Module 3 Bending of Beams"] --> Mod4["Module 4 Shear and Torsion"]
    Mod5["Module 5 Combined Loading and Principal Stresses"]
    Mod2 --> Mod3
    Mod2 --> Mod4
    Mod2 --> Mod5
    Mod3 --> Mod6["Midship Section Scantlings"]
    Mod4 --> Mod6
    Mod5 --> Mod6
    Mod6 --> Mod7["Hull Structural Design Output"]
    Mod1 --> SC1["Sign Convention Engine"]
    Mod2 --> SC1
    Mod3 --> SC1
    Mod4 --> SC1
    Mod5 --> SC1
    SC1 --> Mod6
    SC1 --> Mod7
```

**Reading the Architecture:**
- The **Sign Convention Engine** sits as a **horizontal layer** that all technical modules (1 through 5) feed into and out of.
- This illustrates a key pedagogical principle: **sign convention is not a single topic but a unifying language** across the entire PCNST303 syllabus.
- Any error in the Sign Convention Engine propagates downstream and corrupts the scantling output (Module 6).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: State the standard sign convention for normal stress in solid mechanics. Why is "tension-positive" chosen over "compression-positive"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A normal stress is taken as **positive when it acts outward** (i.e., away from the material element) on a face whose outward normal points in the positive coordinate direction. Equivalently, **tensile stress is positive and compressive stress is negative**.

**Justification (1 mark):** This convention is adopted because:
1. **Metals and shipbuilding alloys fail predominantly in tension** (brittle fracture, Mode I crack opening). Engineers need to instantly read off "tension" as +ve.
2. The **uniaxial tensile test** is the standardized laboratory test, naturally producing positive stress in the loading direction.
3. **Strain sign follows stress sign** via Hooke's law, so elongation (a measurable, intuitive quantity) is also +ve.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Distinguish between hogging and sagging of a ship hull. State the sign convention for the resulting bending stresses at the deck and keel plates.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Hogging (1 mark):** The ship is supported amidships by a wave crest; the bow and stern droop downward. The hull is **concave down** at midship. **Deck plate is in TENSION (+ve) and keel plate is in COMPRESSION (−ve)** under standard ship convention.

**Sagging (1 mark):** The ship is supported at bow and stern by wave crests with a trough amidships; midship deflects downward. The hull is **concave up** at midship. **Deck plate is in COMPRESSION (−ve) and keel plate is in TENSION (+ve)** under standard ship convention.

**Sign Convention Formula (1 mark):**

$$
\sigma_x(y) \;=\; \frac{M\, y}{I}
$$

where $y$ is measured upward from the neutral axis (positive towards the deck). With $M > 0$ for hogging (ship convention), $y > 0$ at deck gives $\sigma_{\text{deck}} > 0$ (tension), consistent with the physical state.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]`

**A simply supported steel beam of span $L = 6$ m carries a uniformly distributed load $w = 20$ kN/m over its entire length. Using the standard sign convention (sagging positive, shear causing clockwise rotation positive):**

**(a)** Derive the shear force and bending moment expressions and **sketch the SFD and BMD with correct signs**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the beam has a rectangular cross-section of breadth $b = 200$ mm and depth $h = 400$ mm, determine the **maximum bending stress** and state its **sign and location** on the cross-section. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Reactions (1 mark):**
By symmetry, each support carries half the total load:

$$
R_A \;=\; R_B \;=\; \frac{w L}{2} \;=\; \frac{20 \times 6}{2} \;=\; 60 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Shear force at distance $x$ from A (3 marks):**
Using the convention that positive V tends to rotate the element clockwise (i.e., upward on the right face of the left segment), and applying vertical equilibrium of the left segment of length $x$:

$$
V(x) \;=\; R_A - w x \;=\; 60 - 20 x \quad \text{kN}
$$

**Sign check:**
- At $x = 0^+$: $V = +60$ kN (positive).
- At $x = L^- = 6^-$: $V = 60 - 120 = -60$ kN (negative).
- Zero shear location: $V = 0 \Rightarrow x = 3$ m (midspan).

This matches the standard **right-triangle SFD** sloping linearly from +60 kN at A to −60 kN at B.

**Step 3 — Bending moment at distance $x$ from A (2 marks):**
Taking moments about the cut, sagging positive:

$$
M(x) \;=\; R_A \cdot x \;-\; w x \cdot \frac{x}{2} \;=\; 60 x \;-\; 10 x^2 \quad \text{kN·m}
$$

**Sign check:**
- At $x = 0$: $M = 0$ ✓ (pin support, no moment).
- At $x = 6$: $M = 360 - 360 = 0$ ✓ (roller support).
- At $x = 3$: $M = 180 - 90 = +90$ kN·m ✓ (positive sagging, peak).

**Step 4 — Sketch SFD and BMD (1 mark):**
- **SFD**: Right triangle, +60 kN at A → −60 kN at B, crossing zero at midspan.
- **BMD**: Parabolic (second-degree), zero at both supports, peak +90 kN·m at midspan, **entirely positive** (sagging).

---

#### Part (b) — Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Maximum bending moment (1 mark):**
From part (a), the maximum moment occurs at midspan where $V = 0$:

$$
M_{\max} \;=\; +90 \text{ kN·m} \;=\; 90 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Positive sign indicates **sagging curvature** (concave up).

**Step 2 — Section properties (2 marks):**
For a rectangular cross-section:

$$
I \;=\; \frac{b h^3}{12} \;=\; \frac{200 \times 400^3}{12} \;=\; \frac{200 \times 64 \times 10^6}{12} \;=\; 1.067 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4
$$

Maximum distance from neutral axis:

$$
y_{\max} \;=\; \frac{h}{2} \;=\; 200 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Section modulus (1 mark):**

$$
Z \;=\; \frac{I}{y_{\max}} \;=\; \frac{1.067 \times 10^9}{200} \;=\; 5.333 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum bending stress (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma_{b,\max} \;=\; \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} \;=\; \frac{90 \times 10^6}{5.333 \times 10^6} \;=\; 16.875 \text{ MPa} \approx 16.9 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Sign and location interpretation (1 mark):**
- **Magnitude:** 16.9 MPa.
- **Top fibre ($y = +200$ mm):** $\sigma_{\text{top}} = M \cdot y / I = (+\text{ve}) \times (+\text{ve}) / (+\text{ve}) = +\mathbf{16.9}$ **MPa (TENSION)**.
- **Bottom fibre ($y = -200$ mm):** $\sigma_{\text{bottom}} = M \cdot y / I = (+\text{ve}) \times (-\text{ve}) / (+\text{ve}) = -\mathbf{16.9}$ **MPa (COMPRESSION)**.

This is the **classic sagging stress distribution**: tension on top, compression on bottom.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**A ship's hull girder is idealized as a simply supported beam of span $L = 120$ m between the bow and stern supports. The hull experiences a triangular still-water bending moment distribution: $M(x) = M_0 \cdot (x/L)$ where $x$ is measured from the bow, and $M_0 = 600{,}000$ kN·m at the stern ($x = L$).**

**(a)** Determine the **equivalent shear force distribution** $V(x)$ along the hull length using the relation $dM/dx = V$. State the **sign convention** clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the hull has a vertical section modulus $Z_{\text{deck}} = Z_{\text{keel}} = 8.0 \times 10^9$ mm³ and the neutral axis lies at mid-depth, determine the **bending stress at deck and keel** at midship ($x = L/2$). Identify whether the hull is in **hogging or sagging** and comment on the resulting stress pattern for ship structural design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State the sign convention (2 marks):**
We adopt the **ship hull convention**: bending moment $M > 0$ corresponds to **hogging** (concave-down curvature); shear force $V > 0$ when it produces clockwise rotation of the element (standard beam convention compatible with $dM/dx = V$).

**Step 2 — Differentiate $M(x)$ to get $V(x)$ (3 marks):**

$$
V(x) \;=\; \frac{dM}{dx} \;=\; \frac{d}{dx}\!\left[\,M_0 \cdot \frac{x}{L}\,\right] \;=\; \frac{M_0}{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate and interpret (2 marks):**

$$
V(x) \;=\; \frac{600{,}000}{120} \;=\; 5000 \text{ kN} \;=\; \text{constant positive}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $V$ is **uniformly +5000 kN** along the entire hull length.
- Positive sign means the shear force tends to rotate each hull element **clockwise**.
- Physically, this means at any cut, the **left segment pushes up** and the **right segment pushes down** with 5000 kN.

This is consistent with a triangular $M(x)$ distribution: a linear $M$ has constant slope, and that slope is the shear.

---

#### Part (b) — Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Bending moment at midship (1 mark):**

$$
M(L/2) \;=\; M_0 \cdot \frac{L/2}{L} \;=\; \frac{M_0}{2} \;=\; \frac{600{,}000}{2} \;=\; +300{,}000 \text{ kN·m}
$$

Convert to N·mm:

$$
M(L/2) \;=\; 300{,}000 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} \;=\; 3.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 2 — State the loading state (2 marks):**
Since $M > 0$ under the chosen ship convention, the hull is in **hogging** at midship. Physically, the bow and stern droop downward (concave-down shape).

**Step 3 — Compute bending stress at deck and keel (3 marks):**
With neutral axis at mid-depth and $y_{\text{deck}} = +H/2$ (positive upward), $y_{\text{keel}} = -H/2$:

$$
\sigma_{\text{deck}} \;=\; \frac{M \cdot y_{\text{deck}}}{I} \;=\; \frac{M}{Z_{\text{deck}}} \;=\; \frac{3.0 \times 10^{11}}{8.0 \times 10^9} \;=\; +37.5 \text{ MPa (TENSION)}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{keel}} \;=\; \frac{M \cdot y_{\text{keel}}}{I} \;=\; -\frac{M}{Z_{\text{keel}}} \;=\; -\frac{3.0 \times 10^{11}}{8.0 \times 10^9} \;=\; -37.5 \text{ MPa (COMPRESSION)}
$$

**Step 4 — Design comment (1 mark):**
Under hogging, the **deck experiences tension (+37.5 MPa)** and the **keel experiences compression (−37.5 MPa)**. For ship structural design:
- The **deck plating and deck longitudinals** must be sized to resist the **tensile** stress at hogging (and yield-strength checks apply with appropriate safety factor per IACS).
- The **keel plate and bottom shell** must resist **buckling** under compression (a much more critical failure mode for thin plates — buckling checks use $\sigma_{\text{cr}} = k \pi^2 E / [12 (1-\nu^2)] \cdot (t/b)^2$).
- Hogging is generally **more critical for buckling** of the bottom, while **sagging** (the other extreme) is more critical for **deck plating yield**. Both extremes must be checked.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Errors in Module 2 Sign Convention Questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign Reversal in $dM/dx = V$ vs. $dV/dx = -w$:** If a student inverts either sign, the entire SFD flips upside down, and **7 marks are lost at once**.
> 2. **Mixing Ship Convention with Beam Convention:** In Part B above, students often write $M > 0 = $ sagging when using ship convention. This is the **most common conceptual error**.
> 3. **Forgetting the Sign of $y$ in $\sigma = My/I$:** The distance $y$ is **signed** (positive upward by convention). Treating it as a magnitude flips the stress signs at deck and keel.
> 4. **Plotting SFD/BMD Without Units:** Lose 0.5 to 1 mark even with correct shape.
> 5. **Writing the Stress Formula Without Defining Convention:** Always state the convention at the start of the answer (1 mark reserved for this).
> 6. **Confusing σ (stress) with P (load):** σ has units MPa; P has units N. KTU examiners mark **units strictly**.
> 7. **Compressive Stress in FEA Output Misread as Positive:** Some software outputs principal stresses in a different convention; always check.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sign Conventions (Module 2)**

- [ ] **Tension is positive**, compression is negative — the **universal rule** for normal stress.
- [ ] **Shear stress positive** when the complementary pair rotates the element counter-clockwise (on a +x face, τ acts in +y direction).
- [ ] **Beam convention**: $V > 0$ when it tends to rotate the element **clockwise**; $M > 0$ for **sagging** (concave up, "smile").
- [ ] **Ship hull convention**: $M > 0$ may correspond to **hogging** (concave down, "frown") — **always state which** is being used.
- [ ] **Master relationships:** $dM/dx = V$ and $dV/dx = -w(x)$, giving $d^2M/dx^2 = -w$.
- [ ] **Bending stress formula:** $\sigma_b = M y / I$ — note that **y is signed**.
- [ ] **Sagging state**: Top fibre in tension (+ve), bottom in compression (−ve).
- [ ] **Hogging state**: Top fibre (deck) in tension (+ve) under ship convention; bottom (keel) in compression.
- [ ] **Right-hand rule** for torsion: thumb along shaft axis, fingers curl in +ve twist direction.
- [ ] **Three coordinate frames** (Global, Local, Section) — all use tension-positive normal stress.
- [ ] **Strain sign follows stress sign** via Hooke's law ($ε = σ/E$).
- [ ] **Always state your convention** at the start of any answer — earns marks and avoids ambiguity.
- [ ] **Combined loading:** $\sigma = N/A + My/I$ — superposition is valid because both terms use the **same** sign convention.
- [ ] **IACS / Class rules** typically use **hogging-positive** for hull-girder bending moment — verify against the specific rule book.

---

### 📚 End of Notes — Sign Conventions, Module 2, PCNST303 (KTU 2024 Scheme)
