topics.title: Shear Force and Bending Moment Diagrams for Cantilever, Simply Supported and Overhanging Beams with Various Types of Loading (Point Load, Distributed Load, Couples)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Shear Force and Bending Moment Diagrams for Cantilever, Simply Supported and Overhanging Beams with Various Types of Loading (Point Load, Distributed Load, Couples) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Shear Force — Formal Definition **Shear Force (SF or V)** at any cross-section of a loaded beam is defined as the **algebraic sum of the vertical (transverse) forces** acting on either side of the section. It equals the magnitude of the resultant of the internal shear stresses acting on that cross-section, integrated over the area. Mathematically: $$V = \Sigma F_y \text{ (on either side of the section)}$$ The standard SI unit is **Newtons (N)** or **kiloNewtons (kN)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > Shear Force at a section is the unbalanced vertical force tending to slide one portion of the beam relative to the other along the cross-section. ## 1.2 Bending Moment — Formal Definition **Bending Moment (BM or M)** at any cross-section is defined as the **algebraic sum of the moments** of all the forces (loads and reactions) acting on either side of that section about the section's centroidal axis. Mathematically: $$M = \Sigma M_{\text{about section}} \text{ (on either side)}$$ The standard SI unit is **N·m** or **kN·m**. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Significance of Bending Moment:** > It is the moment of the internal normal (bending) stresses integrated over the cross-sectional area. It tends to bend (curve) the beam at that section. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you are **holding a wooden stick horizontally** with both hands, and a friend presses down on the middle with a heavy book. - The stick tries to **slide vertically** at the point of the book — that resistance inside the wood is what we measure as **Shear Force**. - The stick also **bends into a smile shape** — the curve at any cross-section is resisted internally by what we measure as **Bending Moment**. > [!TIP] > **Naval Architecture Intuition (Ship Hull Girder):** > A ship hull acts exactly like a simply supported beam between the wave crest and trough, or a cantilever when moored at a dock. Shear Force and Bending Moment diagrams (SFD & BMD) for the hull girder are **mandatory** for class approval by classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS) to ensure structural integrity of the vessel. ## 1.4 Sign Convention (KTU Standard) | Quantity | Positive Sign Convention | Visual Cue | |---|---|---| | **Shear Force (V)** | Left portion pushed **UP**, right portion pushed **DOWN** | Causes clockwise rotation of element | | **Bending Moment (M)** | Causes **sagging** (concave upward smile) | Tension on bottom fibre, compression on top | | **Load (w)** | Acting **DOWNWARD** is positive | Standard gravity loading | | **Reaction** | Acting **UPWARD** is positive | Standard support reaction | > [!CAUTION] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** A wrong sign convention will flip the entire SFD and BMD upside-down. Always draw the beam, mark the assumed positive directions first, and stick to them strictly throughout the solution. ## 1.5 The Two Fundamental Differential Relations These relations are the **heart** of SFD/BMD analysis and are heavily tested in KTU exams: $$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$ $$\frac{dM}{dx} = V$$ $$\frac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$$ Where: - $w(x)$ = intensity of distributed load (positive downward, in **kN/m**) - $V$ = Shear force at the section (**kN**) - $M$ = Bending moment at the section (**kN·m**) - $x$ = distance along the beam axis (**m**) ### Key Inference Table | Loading Condition | SFD Shape | BMD Shape | |---|---|---| | Point Load | Step (vertical jump) | Slope discontinuity (kink) | | Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL) | Linear (straight slope) | Parabolic (2nd degree) | | Uniformly Varying Load (UVL) | Parabolic (2nd degree) | Cubic (3rd degree) | | Couple | No change in V (step) | Step (vertical jump) | | No load (free segment) | Constant (horizontal line) | Linear (straight slope) | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Rules for Constructing SFD and BMD The following rules are derived directly from the fundamental differential relations and must be memorized for the KTU ESE: ### Rule 1: Shear Force - Upward force on the **left** of section → **Positive** Shear - Downward force on the **left** of section → **Negative** Shear - Upward force on the **right** of section → **Negative** Shear - Downward force on the **right** of section → **Positive** Shear ### Rule 2: Bending Moment - Clockwise moment on the **left** of section → **Sagging** (Positive) - Anticlockwise moment on the **left** of section → **Hogging** (Negative) - Clockwise moment on the **right** of section → **Hogging** (Negative) - Anticlockwise moment on the **right** of section → **Sagging** (Positive) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Easy Memory Trick:** For Sagging (positive) BM — beam bends like a **smile** ☺. For Hogging (negative) BM — beam bends like a **frown** ☹. ### Rule 3: Point of Zero Shear The **maximum bending moment** occurs where **V = 0** or changes sign (for simple cases). This is the most critical location for design. ### Rule 4: Point of Contraflexure (Inflection Point) A location where the bending moment changes sign (from positive to negative or vice versa). It corresponds to the **point where the beam curvature reverses** and is structurally important for ship hull girders. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet ### 2.2.1 Cantilever Beam with Point Load at Free End | Quantity | Expression | Location | |---|---|---| | Fixed end reaction | $R_A = P$ (upward) | At fixed end | | Fixed end moment | $M_A = P \cdot L$ | At fixed end | | SF at distance $x$ from free end | $V_x = -P$ | All sections | | BM at distance $x$ from free end | $M_x = -P \cdot x$ | All sections | | Max SF | $P$ | Throughout | | Max BM | $P \cdot L$ | At fixed end | ### 2.2.2 Cantilever Beam with UDL Over Entire Span | Quantity | Expression | Location | |---|---|---| | Total load | $W = w \cdot L$ | — | | Fixed end reaction | $R_A = w \cdot L$ (upward) | At fixed end | | Fixed end moment | $M_A = \dfrac{w L^2}{2}$ | At fixed end | | SF at distance $x$ from free end | $V_x = -w \cdot x$ | All sections | | BM at distance $x$ from free end | $M_x = -\dfrac{w x^2}{2}$ | All sections | | Max SF | $w \cdot L$ | At fixed end | | Max BM | $\dfrac{w L^2}{2}$ | At fixed end | ### 2.2.3 Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load | Quantity | Expression | Location | |---|---|---| | Reactions | $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{P}{2}$ | Both supports | | SF between A and load | $V = +\dfrac{P}{2}$ | $0 \le x \le L/2$ | | SF between load and B | $V = -\dfrac{P}{2}$ | $L/2 \le x \le L$ | | Max BM | $\dfrac{P \cdot L}{4}$ | At centre (under load) | ### 2.2.4 Simply Supported Beam with UDL Over Entire Span | Quantity | Expression | Location | |---|---|---| | Reactions | $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{w L}{2}$ | Both supports | | SF at distance $x$ from A | $V_x = \dfrac{w L}{2} - w x$ | All sections | | BM at distance $x$ from A | $M_x = \dfrac{w L}{2} x - \dfrac{w x^2}{2}$ | All sections | | Point of max BM | $x = L/2$ (where $V=0$) | At centre | | Max BM | $\dfrac{w L^2}{8}$ | At centre | ### 2.2.5 Simply Supported Beam with Couple at Section For a couple $M_0$ applied at distance $a$ from left support on a simply supported beam of span $L$: $$R_A = -\frac{M_0}{L} \quad (\text{downward, if } M_0 \text{ is clockwise})$$ $$R_B = +\frac{M_0}{L} \quad (\text{upward})$$ - SF diagram is **uniform** = $R_A$ everywhere (no discontinuity) - BM jumps by $M_0$ at the location of the couple ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > **Application Areas for Naval Architects:** > 1. **Hull Girder Strength** — Calculating the still-water and wave bending moments of the ship hull. > 2. **Deck Plating and Longitudinals** — Sizing stiffeners against local bending and shear. > 3. **Propeller Shaft** — Analyzing torsional and shear loading. > 4. **Davit and Crane Foundations** — Sizing welded attachments for bending loads. > 5. **Bulkhead Stiffeners** — Verifying panel bending capacity. ## 2.4 Standard Shapes of SFD and BMD (KTU Quick Reference) ### 2.4.1 Standard SFD Shapes | Loading Type | SFD Shape | |---|---| | Point Load | Rectangular (step jump) | | UDL | Triangular | | UVL (linearly increasing) | Parabolic (quadratic) | | Couple | Constant rectangle (no step) | ### 2.4.2 Standard BMD Shapes | Loading Type | BMD Shape | |---|---| | Point Load | Triangular (single slope change) | | UDL | Parabolic (opening direction depends on sign) | | UVL | Cubic (S-shape) | | Couple | Step (rectangular jump) | | No load | Linear (single slope) | > [!CAUTION] > **Common Student Mistake:** A **couple does NOT produce a step in the SFD**, only a step in the BMD. A point load produces a step in SFD and a slope change (kink) in BMD. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Slope Relations dV/dx = -w and dM/dx = V Consider an elemental slice of a beam of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from the origin. Let: - Left face: Shear $V$, Moment $M$ - Right face: Shear $V + dV$, Moment $M + dM$ - Distributed load intensity: $w(x)$ (positive downward) ### Vertical Force Equilibrium ($\Sigma F_y = 0$) $$V - (V + dV) - w \cdot dx = 0$$ Expanding: $$-dV - w \, dx = 0$$ $$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w$$ ### Moment Equilibrium about Right Face ($\Sigma M = 0$) $$-M + (M + dM) - V \cdot dx + w \cdot dx \cdot \frac{dx}{2} = 0$$ The term $w \cdot dx \cdot dx/2$ is a second-order infinitesimal and is neglected for small $dx$. Therefore: $$dM - V \, dx = 0$$ $$\frac{dM}{dx} = V$$ Combining both: $$\frac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w$$ This is the **Euler-Bernoulli beam equation** governing the relationship between load, shear, and moment. ## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Simply Supported Beam with UDL **Problem Statement:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load of intensity $w$ kN/m over its entire length. Determine the reactions and draw the SFD and BMD. ### Step 1: Calculate Reactions By symmetry of geometry and loading: $$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2}$$ (Each reaction equals half the total load $wL$.) ### Step 2: Set up Coordinate System and Take Section at Distance x Take origin at A. Cut a section at distance $x$ from A. Consider the left portion. ### Step 3: Shear Force Equation Sum of vertical forces on left portion: $$V_x = R_A - w \cdot x = \frac{wL}{2} - wx$$ ### Step 4: Bending Moment Equation Sum of moments about the section: $$M_x = R_A \cdot x - (w \cdot x) \cdot \frac{x}{2}$$ $$M_x = \frac{wL}{2} x - \frac{wx^2}{2}$$ $$M_x = \frac{w}{2} (Lx - x^2)$$ ### Step 5: Find Maximum Bending Moment Set $\dfrac{dM_x}{dx} = 0$: $$\frac{dM_x}{dx} = \frac{wL}{2} - wx = V_x = 0$$ Solving: $$x = \frac{L}{2}$$ This is consistent with $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V = 0$. Substituting back: $$M_{\max} = \frac{wL}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} - \frac{w}{2} \left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2$$ $$M_{\max} = \frac{wL^2}{4} - \frac{wL^2}{8} = \frac{wL^2}{8}$$ ### Step 6: Key Values for Plotting | Location | SF Value | BM Value | |---|---|---| | $x = 0$ (at A) | $+\dfrac{wL}{2}$ | $0$ | | $x = L/4$ | $+\dfrac{wL}{4}$ | $\dfrac{3wL^2}{32}$ | | $x = L/2$ (centre) | $0$ | $+\dfrac{wL^2}{8}$ (max) | | $x = 3L/4$ | $-\dfrac{wL}{4}$ | $\dfrac{3wL^2}{32}$ | | $x = L$ (at B) | $-\dfrac{wL}{2}$ | $0$ | The SFD is **linear** (straight line from $+wL/2$ to $-wL/2$) and the BMD is a **parabola** with maximum at midspan. ## 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Overhanging Beam with Point Load **Problem Statement:** A beam ABC of length $L_1 + L_2$ is supported at A and B (span $L_1$). It overhangs by $L_2$ beyond B, with a point load $P$ at the free end C. Find reactions and key BM values. ### Step 1: Calculate Reactions Taking moments about A (clockwise positive): $$R_B \cdot L_1 = P \cdot (L_1 + L_2)$$ $$R_B = P \cdot \frac{L_1 + L_2}{L_1} \quad (\text{upward})$$ Vertical equilibrium: $$R_A + R_B = P$$ $$R_A = P - R_B = P - P \cdot \frac{L_1 + L_2}{L_1} = -P \cdot \frac{L_2}{L_1}$$ $R_A$ is **negative**, meaning the reaction acts **downward** (uplift). The support must hold the beam down. ### Step 2: Shear Force Diagram | Segment | Equation | Value | |---|---|---| | AB (between supports) | $V_x = R_A = -\dfrac{P L_2}{L_1}$ | Constant | | BC (overhang, from B to C) | $V_x = R_A + R_B - 0 = +P$ (just right of B), decreasing to 0 at C | Linear (after point load at C) — actually constant $+\dfrac{P(L_1+L_2)}{L_1} - \text{equilibrium}$ — let me redo | **Re-derived carefully:** For segment BC, taking right portion (from C side): At distance $x$ from C (measuring in from C toward B): $$V_x = P \cdot \frac{x}{(L_1 + L_2)} \cdot \text{(not helpful)}$$ Let me re-do: take origin at C, x measured toward B. For section in segment BC at distance $x$ from C: $$V_x = P \cdot \left(\frac{L_1 + L_2 - x}{L_1 + L_2}\right) - \text{(considering all forces to right of section)}$$ Actually, for section in BC, the right portion has only force $P$ acting downward at C. So: $$V_x = -P \quad \text{(for any section in BC, considering right side)}$$ Wait — using standard sign convention: "Downward force on right → Positive SF" So for section in BC, considering right side: only force is $P$ downward. Therefore $V = +P$ throughout BC. For section in AB, considering right side: forces are $P$ at C (down) and $R_B$ at B (up). Right of section: $R_B$ (up) and $P$ (down), with $R_B = P(L_1+L_2)/L_1$. $V = R_B - P = P(L_1+L_2)/L_1 - P = P L_2 / L_1$ So $V = +P L_2/L_1$ in AB. ### Step 3: Bending Moment Diagram For section in BC at distance $x$ from C (considering right portion): $$M_x = -P \cdot x$$ (Negative because right side force is downward, creates clockwise moment = sagging... wait, downward force on right side = sagging? Let's use standard rule: Downward force on right creates **clockwise** rotation tendency on right portion = **hogging** on the section. So $M = -P \cdot x$ — negative, hogging. At B ($x = L_2$): $$M_B = -P \cdot L_2 \quad (\text{maximum hogging moment})$$ For section in AB at distance $x$ from A: $$M_x = R_A \cdot x = -\frac{P L_2}{L_1} \cdot x$$ At B ($x = L_1$): $$M_B = -P L_2 \quad (\text{matches!})$$ At A ($x = 0$): $$M_A = 0$$ ### Step 4: Critical Observations - BMD is **linear** in both segments (no distributed load). - At B, there is a **kink** in BMD because $V$ changes from $+PL_2/L_1$ to $+P$ abruptly (due to the upward reaction $R_B$). - $M_B = -PL_2$ is the **maximum hogging moment** — this is critical for design. ## 3.4 Analytical Derivation: Cantilever with Couple at Free End **Problem Statement:** A cantilever beam of length $L$, fixed at A, free at B, with an applied couple $M_0$ (clockwise) at free end B. ### Step 1: Reactions Vertical: $R_A = 0$ Moment: $M_A = M_0$ (anticlockwise to balance — if applied is clockwise) ### Step 2: Shear Force No vertical forces anywhere: $$V(x) = 0 \quad \text{throughout}$$ ### Step 3: Bending Moment At section distance $x$ from B (free end), considering right portion: $M_x = -M_0$ (the applied couple — if clockwise, gives hogging) At A ($x = L$): $M_A = -M_0$ — must be balanced by reaction couple. The BMD is a **rectangle of constant height $-M_0$** from A to B. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** A couple produces **no shear** but produces a **uniform bending moment** throughout the beam. This is a heavily tested KTU concept. ## 3.5 Analytical Derivation: Simply Supported Beam with UVL (Triangular Load) **Problem Statement:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a triangular load increasing from 0 at A to $w_0$ kN/m at B. Find reactions and draw SFD, BMD. ### Step 1: Equivalent Point Load Total load: $$W = \frac{1}{2} \cdot w_0 \cdot L$$ Acting at centroid of triangle, which is at $2L/3$ from A. ### Step 2: Reactions Taking moments about A: $$R_B \cdot L = W \cdot \frac{2L}{3} = \frac{w_0 L^2}{3}$$ $$R_B = \frac{w_0 L}{3}$$ Vertical equilibrium: $$R_A = W - R_B = \frac{w_0 L}{2} - \frac{w_0 L}{3} = \frac{w_0 L}{6}$$ ### Step 3: Load Intensity Function At distance $x$ from A: $$w(x) = \frac{w_0}{L} \cdot x$$ ### Step 4: Shear Force $$V_x = R_A - \int_0^x w(\xi) \, d\xi = \frac{w_0 L}{6} - \frac{w_0}{L} \cdot \frac{x^2}{2}$$ $$V_x = \frac{w_0 L}{6} - \frac{w_0 x^2}{2L}$$ ### Step 5: Bending Moment $$M_x = R_A \cdot x - \int_0^x w(\xi)(x - \xi) \, d\xi$$ $$M_x = \frac{w_0 L}{6} x - \frac{w_0}{L} \int_0^x \xi (x - \xi) \, d\xi$$ $$M_x = \frac{w_0 L}{6} x - \frac{w_0}{L} \left[ \frac{x \xi^2}{2} - \frac{\xi^3}{3} \right]_0^x$$ $$M_x = \frac{w_0 L}{6} x - \frac{w_0}{L} \left[ \frac{x^3}{2} - \frac{x^3}{3} \right]$$ $$M_x = \frac{w_0 L}{6} x - \frac{w_0}{L} \cdot \frac{x^3}{6}$$ $$\boxed{M_x = \frac{w_0}{6L} (L^2 x - x^3)}$$ ### Step 6: Maximum Bending Moment Set $V_x = 0$: $$\frac{w_0 L}{6} = \frac{w_0 x^2}{2L}$$ $$x^2 = \frac{L^2}{3}$$ $$x = \frac{L}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577 L$$ Substituting: $$M_{\max} = \frac{w_0}{6L} \left[ L^2 \cdot \frac{L}{\sqrt{3}} - \frac{L^3}{3\sqrt{3}} \right]$$ $$M_{\max} = \frac{w_0 L^2}{6\sqrt{3}} \left[ 1 - \frac{1}{3} \right] = \frac{w_0 L^2}{6\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{2}{3}$$ $$\boxed{M_{\max} = \frac{w_0 L^2}{9\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.0642 \, w_0 L^2}$$ ## 3.6 Summary Comparison Table — Critical Sections | Beam Type & Loading | Max SF | Max BM | Location | |---|---|---|---| | Cantilever, point load $P$ at free end | $P$ | $PL$ | At fixed end | | Cantilever, UDL $w$ over full span | $wL$ | $wL^2/2$ | At fixed end | | Cantilever, couple $M_0$ at free end | $0$ | $M_0$ | Throughout | | SSB, central point load $P$ | $P/2$ | $PL/4$ | At centre | | SSB, UDL $w$ over full span | $wL/2$ | $wL^2/8$ | At centre | | SSB, UVL $w_0$ at B | $w_0 L/3$ | $w_0 L^2/(9\sqrt{3})$ | At $x = L/\sqrt{3}$ | | SSB, couple $M_0$ at centre | $M_0/L$ | $M_0/2$ | At supports and under couple | | Overhanging, point load at overhang end | $PL_2/L_1$ | $PL_2$ | At support B (hogging) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cantilever Beam — Schematic of All Standard Loading Types ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CAN["CANTILEVER BEAM SCHEMATIC"] direction TB subgraph L1["LOADING TYPE 1: POINT LOAD AT FREE END"] W1["FIXED SUPPORT"] ==>|"Length L"| L2["POINT LOAD P ↓"] end subgraph L2N["LOADING TYPE 2: UDL OVER FULL SPAN"] W2["FIXED SUPPORT"] ==>|"w kN/m ↓↓↓↓"| L3["FREE END"] end subgraph L3N["LOADING TYPE 3: COUPLE AT FREE END"] W3["FIXED SUPPORT"] ==>|"Length L"| L4["COUPLE M₀ ↻"] end subgraph L4N["LOADING TYPE 4: UVL OVER FULL SPAN"] W4["FIXED SUPPORT"] ==>|"▲ Triangle Load"| L5["FREE END"] end end style W1 fill:#e1f5ff style W2 fill:#e1f5ff style W3 fill:#e1f5ff style W4 fill:#e1f5ff style L2 fill:#ffe1e1 style L3 fill:#ffe1e1 style L4 fill:#ffe1e1 style L5 fill:#ffe1e1 ``` ## 4.2 Simply Supported Beam — SFD and BMD Architecture Block ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BEAM["SIMPLY SUPPORTED BEAM"] A["Support A

Indicate where each occurs in a simply supported beam and a cantilever beam.** **Model Answer:** **Sagging Bending Moment:** A positive bending moment that causes the beam to curve **concave upward** (smile shape ☺). The bottom fibres experience **tension** and top fibres experience **compression**. **Hogging Bending Moment:** A negative bending moment that causes the beam to curve **concave downward** (frown shape ☹). The top fibres experience **tension** and bottom fibres experience **compression**. **Location:** - **Simply Supported Beam:** Sagging moment occurs throughout the span under downward loading. The maximum sagging moment is at the mid-span. - **Cantilever Beam:** Hogging moment occurs throughout the span under downward loading. The maximum hogging moment is at the fixed end. - **Overhanging Beam:** Sagging occurs between supports, hogging occurs over the overhang region (beyond the supports). **Sketch:** Draw a simply supported beam deflected downward (smile), label "Sagging — Tension at Bottom". Draw a cantilever with free end deflected downward (frown), label "Hogging — Tension at Top". --- ### Question A2: Conceptual [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: State and explain the relationship between rate of loading, shear force, and bending moment. Show how you would locate the point of maximum bending moment using this relationship.** **Model Answer:** The fundamental differential relations are: $$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{dM}{dx} = V$$ Where $w$ is the load intensity, $V$ is the shear force, and $M$ is the bending moment. **Explanation:** - The rate of change of shear force with respect to $x$ equals the negative of the load intensity. - The rate of change of bending moment with respect to $x$ equals the shear force at that section. **Locating Maximum Bending Moment:** At the location of maximum bending moment, $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = 0$. Therefore, $V = 0$ at the point of maximum bending moment. **Procedure:** 1. Plot the SFD. 2. Identify the point where $V = 0$ or where $V$ changes sign. 3. The corresponding location on the BMD gives the maximum bending moment. For example, in a simply supported beam with UDL, the SFD is a triangle, and $V = 0$ at the mid-span, where the BMD shows a maximum parabolic value of $wL^2/8$. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **A simply supported beam AB of span 6 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 10 kN/m over the entire span and a point load of 20 kN at a distance of 2 m from the left support A. Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD). Also find the position and magnitude of maximum bending moment.** #### Solution: **Step 1: Calculate Reactions** Let $R_A$ and $R_B$ be vertical reactions at A and B respectively. Taking moments about A ($\Sigma M_A = 0$): $$R_B \cdot 6 = (10 \times 6) \cdot 3 + 20 \times 2$$ $$R_B \cdot 6 = 180 + 40 = 220$$ $$R_B = 36.667 \text{ kN} \approx 36.67 \text{ kN}$$ Vertical equilibrium ($\Sigma F_y = 0$): $$R_A + R_B = 60 + 20 = 80$$ $$R_A = 80 - 36.667 = 43.333 \text{ kN} \approx 43.33 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2: Shear Force Calculations** - At $x = 0^+$ (just right of A): $V = +R_A = +43.33$ kN - For $0 \le x \le 2$ m: $$V_x = 43.33 - 10x$$ - At $x = 2^-$: $V = 43.33 - 20 = +23.33$ kN - At $x = 2^+$ (just right of point load): $V = 23.33 - 20 = +3.33$ kN - For $2 \le x \le 6$ m: $$V_x = 43.33 - 10x - 20 = 23.33 - 10x$$ - At $x = 6^-$: $V = 23.33 - 60 = -36.67$ kN ✓ (matches $R_B$) - Set $V = 0$ in second segment: $23.33 - 10x = 0$ → $x = 2.333$ m **Step 3: Bending Moment Calculations** - At $x = 0$: $M = 0$ - At $x = 2$ m: $$M = 43.33 \times 2 - 10 \times 2 \times 1 = 86.67 - 20 = 66.67 \text{ kN·m}$$ - At $x = 2.333$ m (where $V = 0$): $$M = 43.33 \times 2.333 - 10 \times 2.333 \times 1.167$$ $$M = 101.10 - 27.22 = 73.89 \text{ kN·m}$$ - At $x = 6$: $M = 0$ ✓ **Step 4: Key Values Summary** | $x$ (m) | $V$ (kN) | $M$ (kN·m) | |---|---|---| | 0 | +43.33 | 0 | | 2⁻ | +23.33 | 66.67 | | 2⁺ | +3.33 | 66.67 | | 2.333 | 0 | **73.89 (Max)** | | 6 | −36.67 | 0 | **Step 5: SFD Description** - Linear from $+43.33$ kN at A to $+23.33$ kN at $x = 2$ m - Step drop of 20 kN at $x = 2$ m (due to point load) - Linear from $+3.33$ kN to $-36.67$ kN between $x = 2$ m and $x = 6$ m - Zero crossing at $x = 2.333$ m **Step 6: BMD Description** - Parabolic curve from A (0) to $x = 2$ m (66.67 kN·m) - Continuous parabolic curve from $x = 2$ m (66.67) to $x = 6$ m (0) - Maximum value $= 73.89$ kN·m at $x = 2.333$ m **Maximum Bending Moment = 73.89 kN·m at $x = 2.333$ m from A** --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **A cantilever beam of length 4 m is fixed at A and free at B. It carries a uniformly distributed load of 6 kN/m over the entire length and a concentrated load of 10 kN at the free end B. Draw the SFD and BMD. Also find the maximum shear force and maximum bending moment.** #### Solution: **Step 1: Calculate Reactions at Fixed End A** Total vertical load: $$W = (6 \times 4) + 10 = 24 + 10 = 34 \text{ kN} \quad (\downarrow)$$ For equilibrium: $$R_A = 34 \text{ kN} \quad (\uparrow)$$ Fixing moment (anticlockwise to balance clockwise loads): $$M_A = (6 \times 4) \times 2 + 10 \times 4 = 48 + 40 = 88 \text{ kN·m} \quad (\text{anticlockwise})$$ **Step 2: Shear Force Calculation (Measuring x from Free End B)** At any section distance $x$ from B (taking right portion — easier for cantilever): Forces to the right of section: - Point load $P = 10$ kN at B (downward) - UDL $w = 6$ kN/m acting over length $x$ from B to section Using sign convention: "Downward on right → Positive V" $$V_x = 10 + 6x \quad \text{(positive throughout)}$$ Key values: - At $x = 0$ (at B): $V = 10$ kN - At $x = 4$ (at A): $V = 10 + 24 = 34$ kN **Step 3: Bending Moment Calculation** Taking right portion of section at distance $x$ from B: - Moment of point load: $10 \cdot x$ (clockwise on right → hogging → negative) - Moment of UDL: $(6x) \cdot (x/2) = 3x^2$ (clockwise on right → hogging → negative) $$M_x = -(10x + 3x^2)$$ Key values: - At $x = 0$ (at B): $M = 0$ - At $x = 4$ (at A): $M = -(40 + 48) = -88$ kN·m **Step 4: Key Values Summary** | $x$ (m from B) | $V$ (kN) | $M$ (kN·m) | |---|---|---| | 0 | +10 | 0 | | 1 | +16 | −13 | | 2 | +22 | −32 | | 3 | +28 | −57 | | 4 (at A) | +34 | **−88 (Max Hogging)** | **Step 5: SFD Description** - Linear (straight line) increasing from $+10$ kN at B to $+34$ kN at A - Slope = $w = 6$ kN/m - Entire SFD is positive (using standard sign convention with cantilever) **Step 6: BMD Description** - Parabolic curve from $M = 0$ at B to $M = -88$ kN·m at A - Entirely negative (hogging) — characteristic of cantilever under downward loads - Concave downward parabola **Maximum Shear Force = 34 kN at fixed end A** **Maximum Bending Moment = 88 kN·m (hogging) at fixed end A** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > **1. Wrong Sign Convention:** Students often mix positive and negative directions, especially for cantilever beams. Decide on one convention and stick to it. > > **2. Forgetting UDL Contribution in Moment Equation:** When computing BM for sections in UDL region, students forget to subtract $\frac{wx^2}{2}$. This is the most common error. > > **3. Not Considering Right Portion for Cantilever:** For cantilever beams, it is easier to consider the **right (free) portion** of the section because no reactions need to be calculated first. KTU specifically tests this technique. > > **4. Confusing Point Load Step vs Couple Step:** A point load produces a **step in SFD**, but a couple produces a **step in BMD** (NOT SFD). Confusing these costs full marks. > > **5. Missing Uplift Reaction in Overhanging Beams:** In overhanging beams with point loads on the overhang, the inner support reaction can become **downward** (uplift). Failing to identify this is a major error. > > **6. Not Closing the SFD:** The SFD must return to zero at the free end of a cantilever and at both supports of a simply supported beam. Always verify: $\Sigma V = 0$. > > **7. Arithmetic Errors in Reactions:** Always re-verify reactions by taking moments about both supports. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU ESE Module 2** **Core Definitions:** - Shear Force = Algebraic sum of vertical forces on either side of section - Bending Moment = Algebraic sum of moments on either side of section - Sagging = Positive BM, concave up, tension on bottom - Hogging = Negative BM, concave down, tension on top **Fundamental Relations:** - $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$ - $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ - $\dfrac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w$ **Shape Recognition (HIGH PRIORITY):** - Point Load → SFD: Step jump | BMD: Triangle (kink) - UDL → SFD: Linear triangle | BMD: Parabola - UVL → SFD: Parabola | BMD: Cubic - Couple → SFD: No change | BMD: Step jump **Standard Formulae:** - SSB with central point load $P$: $M_{max} = PL/4$ - SSB with UDL $w$: $M_{max} = wL^2/8$ - Cantilever with point load at free end: $M_{max} = PL$ - Cantilever with UDL: $M_{max} = wL^2/2$ - SSB with UVL ($w_0$ at one end): $M_{max} = w_0 L^2/(9\sqrt{3})$ at $x = L/\sqrt{3}$ - Overhanging beam with point load $P$ at free end: $M_B = -PL_2$ (hogging at support B) **Critical Points:** - Maximum BM occurs at **$V = 0$** or **V changes sign** - Point of Contraflexure = location where BM changes sign (zero crossing) - Sum of SFD areas between any two points = change in BM between those points - For cantilever, max SF and max BM both occur at fixed end - For SSB with symmetric loading, max BM is at mid-span **KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** 1. Always start by drawing FBD and computing all reactions 2. Use sign convention consistently (write it down at the start) 3. Compute key values: SF and BM at supports, at load points, at mid-span 4. Find where $V = 0$ to locate max BM 5. Draw neat SFD and BMD with proper axes, units, and values marked 6. Mention "Sagging is positive" in the legend for clarity 7. For naval architecture applications, mention hull girder analogy where relevant **Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid:** - Mixing sign conventions - Forgetting to subtract UDL contribution in BM equation - Drawing BMD on the tension side (always draw on compression side as per convention) - Missing unit labels on axes - Not verifying equilibrium at the end

topics.title: Simple Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST303 - SOLID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Simple Bending ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Simple Bending > [!IMPORTANT] > **Simple Bending** is defined as the bending of a straight beam under the action of a **constant bending moment** along its length, such that the **shear force over the portion considered is zero**. The beam axis bends into a circular arc, and cross-sections that were originally plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis remain plane and perpendicular to it after bending (Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis). In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology: - A beam is said to be in **pure bending** (the ideal case of simple bending) when it is subjected only to a **bending moment** with **zero transverse shear force** across the section. - When both shear force and bending moment coexist, the condition is called **ordinary bending** or **bending with shear**, which is no longer "simple". > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in Naval Architecture:** The hull girder of a ship, when floating in still water, behaves very closely to a beam in simple bending. The longitudinal strength of the hull — one of the most critical design considerations in shipbuilding — is analyzed using simple bending theory, also called **"Hull Girder Analysis"**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you hold a **flat plastic ruler (scale)** flat on a table with both hands at the ends and gently press your thumbs down in the middle: - The ruler bends downward in a smooth curve. - The **top fibers** of the ruler are squeezed together → they are in **compression**. - The **bottom fibers** are stretched apart → they are in **tension**. - Somewhere in between, there is a layer that does **neither stretch nor compress** — this is the **neutral layer** (or neutral axis in cross-section). This is exactly what happens in a ship's hull when a wave passes under the mid-body: - The deck (top) experiences **compressive bending stress**. - The keel (bottom) experiences **tensile bending stress**. - The neutral axis lies somewhere near the mid-section of the hull. > [!TIP] > Think of a **diving board** at a swimming pool. When a diver stands at the tip, the board bends into a smooth curve, the top is in compression, the bottom in tension, and the board tries to return to its original shape. That restoring mechanism is the **flexural rigidity (EI)** of the beam. ### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics The key physical quantities that must always be carried with their units in KTU answers are: - **Bending moment (M)** → **N·m** (or kN·m in civil/naval problems) - **Moment of Inertia (I)** → **m⁴** (or mm⁴) - **Bending Stress (σ)** → **N/m² = Pa** (or MPa = N/mm²) - **Radius of curvature (R)** → **m** - **Young's Modulus (E)** → **N/m² = Pa** (for ship steel, **E ≈ 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² = 200 GPa**) - **Section Modulus (Z)** → **m³** (or mm³) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Expectation:** Every numerical answer in PCNST303 must end with a boxed final value with **units**, and the bending equation must be quoted in its canonical form before substitution. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Assumptions of Simple Bending Theory The classical theory of simple bending is built upon five fundamental assumptions. KTU examiners **frequently award marks** to students who state these assumptions clearly in Part A answers. 1. **Homogeneous material** – The beam is made of a single, uniform material (e.g., mild steel) throughout its volume. 2. **Isotropic material** – The mechanical properties are the same in all directions at any point. 3. **Linear Elastic behaviour** – The material obeys **Hooke's Law** up to the working stress. The stress is proportional to strain: σ = Eε. 4. **Plane sections remain plane** – Cross-sections that were originally plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis continue to remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending (**Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis**). 5. **Self-weight neglected in pure bending segment** – The portion under pure bending has no transverse loads, hence **SF = 0** and **BM = constant**. ### 2.2 Logical Breakdown of the Bending Phenomenon The complete chain of reasoning behind the bending equation can be summarized in five causal steps: - **Step 1 — Geometric Kinematics:** When the beam bends into a circular arc, fibers above the neutral axis shorten, fibers below elongate. By similar triangles, the strain at a distance *y* from the neutral axis is: $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$$ - **Step 2 — Constitutive (Material) Law:** By Hooke's Law for linear elastic material: $$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$ - **Step 3 — Combine Steps 1 & 2 (Stress Distribution):** $$\sigma = E \cdot \dfrac{y}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$$ - **Step 4 — Force Equilibrium:** The internal stresses on the cross-section must produce a moment equal to the externally applied bending moment M. Integrating: $$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \dfrac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA = \dfrac{E I}{R}$$ - **Step 5 — Final Canonical Bending Equation:** Combining the results: $$\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$$ ### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Naval Architecture - **Hull Girder Bending Moment:** Calculation of sagging and hogging stresses in the ship's hull girder. - **Longitudinal Strength:** Determining the required **section modulus (Z)** of the hull cross-section to resist wave bending moments. - **Local Panel Bending:** Analysis of plating between stiffeners, deck plates under cargo loads, and shell plating under hydrostatic pressure. - **Selection of Longitudinals:** Spacing of longitudinal stiffeners to keep bending stress in plating within allowable limits. - **Classification Society Rules:** Direct use of bending equation in rules of **IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, BV** for scantling determination. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table contains every equation a KTU student requires to solve any simple bending problem asked in PCNST303 Module 3. All equations are mathematically standard and verifiable. | **#** | **Quantity / Relation** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Strain at distance *y* from NA | $\varepsilon = y / R$ | dimensionless | From geometry of arc | | 2 | Bending stress | $\sigma = E y / R$ | N/m² (Pa) | Linear variation across depth | | 3 | Bending moment – curvature | $M = E I / R$ | N·m | Flexural rigidity EI | | 4 | **Canonical Bending Equation** | $M / I = \sigma / y = E / R$ | — | The single most important KTU equation | | 5 | Moment of Resistance | $M_R = \sigma_{\max} \cdot I / y_{\max}$ | N·m | $\sigma_{\max}$ = max bending stress | | 6 | **Section Modulus** | $Z = I / y_{\max}$ | m³ | Used for quick stress check | | 7 | Maximum Bending Stress | $\sigma_{\max} = M / Z$ | Pa | Direct design formula | | 8 | Radius of Curvature | $R = E I / M$ | m | Beam axis curvature | | 9 | Curvature (1/R) | $1 / R = M / (E I)$ | 1/m | Slope of elastic line | | 10 | Section modulus of rectangle (b·d) | $Z = b d^{2} / 6$ | m³ | I = bd³/12, y = d/2 | | 11 | Section modulus of circle (diameter D) | $Z = \pi D^{3} / 32$ | m³ | I = πD⁴/64 | | 12 | Section modulus of hollow rectangle | $Z = (B D^{2} - b d^{2}) / 6$ | m³ | For box girders / ship sections | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** The relation $M/I = \sigma/y = E/R$ is dimensionally valid **only when y is measured from the Neutral Axis**. A common error is to measure y from the top/bottom edge and then misapply the equation. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Bending Equation: σ/y = M/I = E/R Consider a prismatic beam of uniform cross-section, initially straight, loaded in pure bending by end couples of magnitude M. Let us analyze a small element of length dx. **Step A — Initial and Deformed Configurations** Before loading, the cross-sections at ends A and B are vertical and parallel. After loading, the beam bends into a circular arc of radius R, subtending an angle dθ at the centre of curvature O. **Step B — Length of the Neutral Layer** The neutral layer, located on the neutral axis of the cross-section, is neither stretched nor compressed. Its length remains: $$L_{NA} = dx$$ **Step C — Length of a Fiber at Distance y from NA** A fiber located at a distance y from the neutral axis (positive upward, positive in tension direction) will now have length: $$L_f = (R + y) \, d\theta$$ **Step D — Strain in the Fiber** Strain is the change in length divided by the original length. The original length of every longitudinal fiber is dx = R·dθ. Hence: $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{L_f - dx}{dx} = \dfrac{(R + y)\, d\theta - R\, d\theta}{R\, d\theta}$$ Cancelling the dθ: $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$$ **Interpretation:** Strain varies **linearly** with distance y from the neutral axis. This is the **kinematic result** of plane sections remaining plane. **Step E — Apply Hooke's Law** For a linearly elastic material: $$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = E \cdot \dfrac{y}{R}$$ Therefore: $$\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$$ **Interpretation:** Bending stress is **zero at the neutral axis** (y = 0), and **maximum at the extreme fibers** (y = y_max). **Step F — Resultant Force on Cross-Section** The internal stresses on the cross-section must collectively produce no net axial force (since pure bending has no axial load). This equilibrium condition automatically locates the neutral axis at the **centroid** of the cross-section. $$\sum F_x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \int_A \sigma \, dA = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \int_A y \, dA = 0$$ This integral is the first moment of area about the neutral axis, which is zero only when the axis passes through the centroid. **Step G — Resultant Moment = Applied Bending Moment** Taking moments about the neutral axis, the internal stress distribution must yield a couple equal to the applied bending moment M: $$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA$$ Substitute σ = Ey/R: $$M = \int_A \left(\dfrac{E y}{R}\right) y \, dA = \dfrac{E}{R} \int_A y^{2} \, dA$$ **Step H — Recognize Moment of Inertia** By definition, the second moment of area about the neutral axis (which is the centroidal axis for pure bending) is: $$I = \int_A y^{2} \, dA$$ Therefore: $$M = \dfrac{E I}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{M}{I}$$ **Step I — Combine All Three Relations** We have obtained two equations that both equal E/R: $$\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R} \quad \text{and} \quad \dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}$$ Equating them: $$\boxed{\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}}$$ This is the **canonical bending equation** — the cornerstone of beam theory. > [!NOTE] > The quantity $E I$ is called the **Flexural Rigidity** of the beam. A higher EI means the beam resists bending more strongly (smaller curvature for the same moment). ### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Bending Stress Starting from $M/I = \sigma_{\max}/y_{\max}$ and defining section modulus $Z = I/y_{\max}$: $$\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{\max}}{I} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$$ The bending moment that a section can safely resist at an allowable stress σ_allow is: $$M_{\text{allow}} = \sigma_{\text{allow}} \cdot Z$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration (KTU-Standard) **Problem:** A simply supported steel beam of span 4 m carries a central point load of 20 kN. The cross-section is rectangular, 80 mm wide × 160 mm deep. Find: (a) Maximum bending moment (b) Maximum bending stress (c) Radius of curvature at the section of maximum moment Given E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm². **Solution:** **(a) Maximum Bending Moment:** For a simply supported beam with central point load, the maximum bending moment occurs at the centre: $$M_{\max} = \dfrac{W L}{4} = \dfrac{20 \times 4}{4} = 20 \text{ kN·m} = 20 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$ **(b) Section Modulus:** For a rectangle, $Z = b d^{2} / 6$: $$Z = \dfrac{80 \times 160^{2}}{6} = \dfrac{80 \times 25600}{6} = 341333.33 \text{ mm}^{3}$$ **(c) Maximum Bending Stress:** $$\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \dfrac{20 \times 10^{6}}{341333.33} = 58.6 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \approx 58.6 \text{ MPa}$$ **(d) Radius of Curvature:** First, find moment of inertia: $$I = \dfrac{b d^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{80 \times 160^{3}}{12} = 27306666.67 \text{ mm}^{4}$$ Then: $$R = \dfrac{E I}{M} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{5} \times 27306666.67}{20 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{5.461 \times 10^{12}}{2 \times 10^{7}} = 273066.67 \text{ mm} \approx 273 \text{ m}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Tip:** Always state the **answer with units in a box**. KTU examiners explicitly check for unit consistency. Converting kN·m to N·mm requires multiplying by 10⁶ — missing this is a top reason for losing marks. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Simple Bending Theory The following Mermaid diagram represents the complete logical flow of the bending theory derivation, from physical loading to the final design equations. It is the recommended figure to reproduce in KTU 14-mark answers for full marks. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Beam subjected to pure bending: SF=0, BM=M constant] --> B0[Cross sections remain plane and perpendicular to NA] B0 --> C0[Geometry of circular arc: fiber strain epsilon = y / R] C0 --> D0[Hookes Law: sigma = E times epsilon] D0 --> E0[Stress distribution: sigma over y = E over R] E0 --> F0[Force equilibrium: NA passes through centroid] F0 --> G0[Moment equilibrium: M = integral of sigma y dA] G0 --> H0[Define I = integral of y squared dA] H0 --> I0[M = EI over R, so E over R = M over I] I0 --> J0[Canonical equation: M over I = sigma over y = E over R] J0 --> K0[Define section modulus Z = I over y max] K0 --> L0[Design formula: sigma max = M over Z] L0 --> M0[Application: Hull girder, deck, keel, plating analysis] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Stress Distribution Across a Rectangular Beam Section ```mermaid flowchart LR N0[Neutral Axis at centroid y = 0
Stress sigma = 0] --> N1[Linear variation: sigma = E y over R] N1 --> N2[Top extreme fiber y = plus d over 2
Maximum compressive stress] N1 --> N3[Bottom extreme fiber y = minus d over 2
Maximum tensile stress] N2 --> N4[Stress block: triangular distribution
Compression above NA] N3 --> N5[Stress block: triangular distribution
Tension below NA] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Ship Hull Girder Analogy ```mermaid flowchart TD H0[Ship floating in still water
Weight W equals Buoyancy B] --> H1[Wave passes amidships] H1 --> H2[Hull bends as a beam
Sagging or Hogging condition] H2 --> H3[Deck plating: top fiber in compression] H2 --> H4[Keel plate: bottom fiber in tension] H2 --> H5[Neutral axis: near mid-height of hull] H3 --> H6[Bending stress sigma = M over Z] H4 --> H6 H5 --> H6 H6 --> H7[Check sigma less than sigma allowable per IRS rules] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Each box represents a logical state in the derivation or application chain. The arrows (--> ) indicate the direction of deduction. In the KTU answer script, drawing such a flowchart before the algebra earns **1–2 method marks** even if the algebra has an arithmetic slip. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Bank]** **Define simple bending. State any two assumptions made in the theory of simple bending.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (2 marks):** Simple bending is the bending of a beam under the action of a constant bending moment, with zero shear force over the region considered. The beam bends into a circular arc, and cross-sections that were originally plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis remain plane and perpendicular to it after bending. > > **Assumptions (1 mark — any two):** > 1. The material is homogeneous and isotropic. > 2. The material obeys Hooke's Law (linear elastic behaviour). > 3. Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis. > 4. The radius of curvature is large compared to the cross-sectional dimensions. > 5. Self-weight of the beam is neglected in the portion considered. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** **What is section modulus? Write the section modulus for a rectangular and a circular cross-section.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Section Modulus (1 mark):** It is the ratio of the moment of inertia of a cross-section to the distance of the extreme fiber from the neutral axis: > $$Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$$ > It is a geometric property used to determine the bending strength of a beam. > > **Rectangular section b × d (1 mark):** > $$Z = \dfrac{b d^{2}}{6}$$ > > **Circular section of diameter D (1 mark):** > $$Z = \dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{32}$$ --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In 14-mark Part B questions, students often: > - Forget to convert kN·m into N·mm before substituting in formulas → loses **2 marks** > - Quote the bending equation with y measured from the wrong reference → loses **3 marks** > - Skip the assumption statements before deriving → loses **2 marks** > - Forget to box the final numerical answer → loses **1 mark** > - Mix up moment of resistance and bending moment terminology → loses **1 mark** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3] **(a)** Derive the bending equation $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ for a beam subjected to pure bending. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A simply supported timber beam of span 3 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 6 kN/m over its entire span. The cross-section is rectangular, 120 mm wide and 240 mm deep. Determine: - (i) the maximum bending moment, - (ii) the maximum bending stress, - (iii) the section modulus required if the allowable bending stress is 10 MPa. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** Follow Section 3.1 of these notes. Award 1 mark for assumptions, 1 mark each for the geometric, constitutive, equilibrium steps, 2 marks for the final canonical equation in boxed form, and 1 mark for stating the position of the NA at the centroid. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Maximum bending moment** for a simply supported beam with UDL: $$M_{\max} = \dfrac{w L^{2}}{8} = \dfrac{6 \times 3^{2}}{8} = \dfrac{54}{8} = 6.75 \text{ kN·m} = 6.75 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$ **(ii) Section modulus and maximum bending stress:** $$Z = \dfrac{b d^{2}}{6} = \dfrac{120 \times 240^{2}}{6} = \dfrac{120 \times 57600}{6} = 1152000 \text{ mm}^{3}$$ $$\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \dfrac{6.75 \times 10^{6}}{1152000} = 5.86 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 5.86 \text{ MPa}$$ **(iii) Section modulus required for allowable stress of 10 MPa:** $$Z_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{M_{\max}}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}} = \dfrac{6.75 \times 10^{6}}{10} = 675000 \text{ mm}^{3} = 675 \times 10^{3} \text{ mm}^{3}$$ **Final Answers (boxed for KTU):** $$\boxed{M_{\max} = 6.75 \text{ kN·m}, \quad \sigma_{\max} = 5.86 \text{ MPa}, \quad Z_{\text{req}} = 675000 \text{ mm}^{3}}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3] **(a)** A hollow circular shaft of outer diameter 100 mm and inner diameter 60 mm is subjected to a bending moment of 5 kN·m. Calculate: - (i) the section modulus, - (ii) the maximum bending stress, - (iii) the radius of curvature, given E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm². **(7 marks)** **(b)** A steel bar 40 mm wide and 80 mm deep is used as a simply supported beam over a span of 2.5 m. Find the uniformly distributed load the beam can carry if the bending stress is not to exceed 120 MPa. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Hollow Circular Shaft (7 marks):** **(i) Section modulus:** $$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{64}\left(100^{4} - 60^{4}\right)$$ $$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}\left(1.0 \times 10^{8} - 1.296 \times 10^{7}\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{64} \times 8.704 \times 10^{7}$$ $$I = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 8.704 \times 10^{7}}{64} = 4.271 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}$$ $$Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}} = \dfrac{4.271 \times 10^{6}}{50} = 85420 \text{ mm}^{3} \approx 8.542 \times 10^{4} \text{ mm}^{3}$$ **(ii) Maximum bending stress:** $$\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{6}}{85420} = 58.53 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \approx 58.53 \text{ MPa}$$ **(iii) Radius of curvature:** $$R = \dfrac{E I}{M} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{5} \times 4.271 \times 10^{6}}{5 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{8.542 \times 10^{11}}{5 \times 10^{6}} = 1.708 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm} = 170.8 \text{ m}$$ **(b) UDL on Simply Supported Beam (7 marks):** For simply supported beam with UDL, $M_{\max} = w L^{2} / 8$. Maximum allowable moment: $$M_{\max} = \sigma_{\text{allow}} \cdot Z = 120 \times \dfrac{40 \times 80^{2}}{6} = 120 \times \dfrac{256000}{6} = 120 \times 42666.67 = 5.12 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$ Therefore: $$w = \dfrac{8 M_{\max}}{L^{2}} = \dfrac{8 \times 5.12 \times 10^{6}}{(2500)^{2}} = \dfrac{40.96 \times 10^{6}}{6.25 \times 10^{6}} = 6.554 \text{ N/mm}$$ Converting to kN/m: $$w = 6.554 \text{ N/mm} = 6.554 \text{ kN/m}$$ **Final Answers (boxed for KTU):** $$\boxed{Z = 85420 \text{ mm}^{3}, \quad \sigma_{\max} = 58.53 \text{ MPa}, \quad R = 170.8 \text{ m}, \quad w = 6.554 \text{ kN/m}}$$ --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** - [x] **Canonical equation** is $M/I = \sigma/y = E/R$ — quote it in **this exact form**. - [x] **Neutral Axis (NA)** always passes through the **centroid** of the cross-section in pure bending. - [x] **Bending stress is zero at the NA** and **maximum at the extreme fibers** (top and bottom). - [x] **Section Modulus** $Z = I / y_{\max}$ — units in **mm³** for ship/section problems. - [x] **Flexural Rigidity** $EI$ controls beam curvature: $R = EI / M$. - [x] For a **rectangle** of size $b \times d$: $I = bd^{3}/12$ and $Z = bd^{2}/6$. - [x] For a **solid circle** of diameter $D$: $I = \pi D^{4}/64$ and $Z = \pi D^{3}/32$. - [x] For a **hollow circle** of $D, d$: $I = \pi(D^{4} - d^{4})/64$ and $Z = \pi(D^{4} - d^{4})/(32 D)$. - [x] For a **hollow rectangle** of $B \times D$ outside and $b \times d$ inside: $I = (BD^{3} - bd^{3})/12$ and $Z = (BD^{3} - bd^{3})/(6D)$. - [x] Always write **5 assumptions** before derivation: homogeneous, isotropic, plane sections remain plane, linear elastic, R >> cross-section dimensions. - [x] Always quote the value of $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm² for **mild steel** unless otherwise given. - [x] Always **box the final answer** with proper units — KTU examiners reward this presentation habit. - [x] **Naval relevance:** Simple bending theory is the basis of **hull girder analysis** — the longitudinal strength of the ship depends directly on its section modulus about the horizontal neutral axis. - [x] Remember: $y$ in the bending equation is **always measured from the neutral axis**, never from the top or bottom surface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-line memory hook for the exam:** *"MI equals sigma-y equals E-over-R — that's the only bending equation you'll ever need."*
